name: metronidazole
moa: Antiprotozoal/Antibacterial — Nitroimidazole — Diffuses into organism, nitro group reduced by ferredoxin → toxic intermediates damage DNA helix, inhibit nucleic acid synthesis → cell death. Active against anaerobes (Bacteroides, Clostridium) and protozoa (Entamoeba, Giardia, Trichomonas).
commonUses:
1. Amoebiasis — intestinal and extraintestinal including liver abscess, as tissue amebicide.
2. Giardiasis — symptomatic infection.
3. Trichomoniasis — urogenital infection, treat sexual partners concurrently.
4. Bacterial vaginosis — oral or topical.
5. Anaerobic bacterial infections — intra-abdominal, pelvic, brain abscess, endocarditis (in combination).
6. Pseudomembranous colitis — alternative to oral vancomycin.
7. Helicobacter pylori eradication — as part of triple/quadruple therapy.
8. Surgical prophylaxis — colorectal, gynecologic procedures.
dose: Amoebiasis: 500–750mg orally every 8 hours for 7–10 days. Giardiasis: 250mg orally every 8 hours for 5–7 days. Trichomoniasis: 2g single oral dose or 500mg twice daily for 7 days. Bacterial vaginosis: 500mg orally twice daily for 7 days. Anaerobic infections: 500mg IV every 8 hours, infuse over 30–60 minutes. H. pylori: 500mg orally twice daily as part of regimen for 14 days.
maxDose: 4g/day (oral or IV) for serious anaerobic infections.
pedDose: Amoebiasis: 35–50mg/kg/day orally divided every 8 hours for 7–10 days, max 750mg/dose. Giardiasis: 15mg/kg/day orally divided every 8 hours for 5–7 days, max 250mg/dose. Trichomoniasis: 5mg/kg orally every 8 hours for 7 days. Anaerobic infections: 30mg/kg/day IV divided every 8 hours, max 500mg/dose. Infuse over 30–60 minutes.
pedAgeLimit: Safe in neonates (dose adjustment needed: <40 weeks gestational age, use longer dosing intervals).
pedDoseAr: الأميبيا: 35–50 مجم لكل كجم يوميًا — عن طريق الفم — مقسمة كل 8 ساعات — لمدة 7–10 أيام. الجيارديا: 15 مجم لكل كجم يوميًا — مقسمة كل 8 ساعات — لمدة 5–7 أيام. العدوى اللاهوائية: 30 مجم لكل كجم يوميًا — في الوريد — مقسمة كل 8 ساعات — تعطى على مدى 30–60 دقيقة.
lactationNote: Excreted in breast milk; consider interrupting breastfeeding for 12–24 hours after single 2g dose. For multiple doses, consider alternative or discontinue breastfeeding.
metabolism: Hepatic — oxidation and glucuronidation; active hydroxy metabolite
elimination: Renal — 60–80% excreted, some unchanged; half-life 6–8 hours
interactions: Alcohol — disulfiram-like reaction (flushing, tachycardia, nausea) — avoid during and for 48 hours after last dose. Warfarin — enhanced anticoagulant effect — monitor INR closely. Lithium — increased lithium levels — monitor lithium levels. Phenytoin/Phenobarbital — reduced metronidazole effect — may need dose adjustment. Cyclosporine — increased cyclosporine levels — monitor levels.
doseAdjustments: Renal: eGFR <10mL/min: reduce dose by 50%. Hepatic (Child-Pugh C): reduce dose by 50%, prolonged half-life. Elderly: no adjustment needed with normal renal/hepatic function.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): metallic taste, nausea, dry mouth, dark urine (benign), headache. | Serious: peripheral neuropathy (prolonged use, reversible), seizures, encephalopathy, aseptic meningitis, Stevens-Johnson syndrome (rare). | Monitoring: check for neuropathy symptoms on prolonged courses (>4 weeks).
contra: First trimester of pregnancy (relative — use only if clearly needed). Hypersensitivity to nitroimidazoles.

name: nifuroxazide
moa: Intestinal Antiseptic/Antibacterial — Nitrofuran derivative — Acts locally in intestinal lumen; inhibits bacterial enzymes involved in carbohydrate metabolism → disrupts bacterial cell wall synthesis → bacteriostatic and bactericidal at higher concentrations. Narrow spectrum: primarily active against Gram-negative enteric bacteria (E. coli, Shigella, Salmonella). Not systemically absorbed.
commonUses:
1. Acute bacterial diarrhea — non-invasive, community-acquired.
2. Acute gastroenteritis — when bacterial cause suspected.
3. Traveler's diarrhea — mild to moderate cases.
4. Adjunct in acute diarrheal illness — when no indication for systemic antibiotics.
dose: 200–400mg orally every 6–8 hours for 3–7 days depending on severity. Take with a full glass of water. Maximum treatment duration: 7 days.
pedDose: Infants >1 month: 200mg orally every 8–12 hours for 3 days. Children >2 years: 200mg orally every 6–8 hours. Suspension: 220mg/5ml — 5ml every 6–8 hours for children >2 years. Maximum 800mg/day.
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for neonates <1 month.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال أكبر من سنتين: 200 مجم (5 مل من المعلق 220 مجم/5 مل) — كل 6–8 ساعات — لمدة 3 أيام. الأطفال أقل من سنتين: 200 مجم — كل 8–12 ساعة.
lactationNote: Minimal systemic absorption; limited data in lactation. Use only if clearly needed.
metabolism: Not systemically absorbed — acts locally in the intestinal lumen
elimination: Fecal — nearly 100% unchanged in stool; half-life not applicable (no systemic levels)
interactions: No known significant systemic drug interactions due to lack of systemic absorption. Oral rehydration solutions — compatible use.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment (not systemically absorbed). Hepatic impairment: no data available; no adjustment expected. Elderly: no specific adjustment.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): nausea (mild), dark-colored stool (benign). | Serious: allergic reactions (rash, urticaria — rare). | Monitoring: none specific; if diarrhea persists >3 days, reassess diagnosis.
contra: Hypersensitivity to nitrofuran derivatives. Invasive bacterial diarrhea (fever, bloody stools — requires systemic antibiotics).

name: dihydrostreptomycin / sulfonamides
moa: Antibiotic Combination — Aminoglycoside + Sulfonamide — Dihydrostreptomycin: binds 30S ribosomal subunit → inhibits bacterial protein synthesis (bactericidal). Sulfonamides: structural analogs of PABA → inhibit dihydropteroate synthetase → block folic acid synthesis → bacteriostatic. Combined: synergistic bactericidal effect against enteric Gram-negative bacilli. Spectrum: Enterobacteriaceae (E. coli, Shigella, Salmonella).
commonUses:
1. Acute bacterial diarrhea — including shigellosis and enterotoxigenic E. coli.
2. Bacillary dysentery — as part of combination therapy.
3. Enteric infections — when susceptible organisms confirmed.
4. Traveler's diarrhea — moderate to severe cases.
5. Cholera — adjunctive therapy (rarely used now, resistance concerns).
dose: Streptoquin (dihydrostreptomycin 100mg + sulfadimidine 250mg + sulfathiazole 250mg): 2 tablets orally every 8 hours for 5–7 days. Or according to brand-specific dosing; follow product insert for combination ratio.
pedDose: Children >6 years: half the adult dose. Enteroquin suspension/tablet: based on weight — approximately 25–50mg/kg/day of sulfonamide component divided every 8 hours for 5 days. Maximum 3 tablets/day. Not recommended for infants <6 months due to sulfonamide component.
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for infants <6 months. Use with extreme caution in children <2 years.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال أكبر من 6 سنوات: نصف الجرعة للكبار — قرص واحد أو 5–10 مل معلق — كل 8 ساعات — لمدة 5 أيام. لا يُنصح به للرضع أقل من 6 شهور.
lactationNote: Sulfonamides excreted in breast milk; risk of kernicterus in neonates and G6PD-deficient infants. Aminoglycosides minimal oral absorption but caution advised. Avoid in nursing mothers of preterm infants or infants with hyperbilirubinemia.
metabolism: Hepatic — sulfonamides undergo acetylation and glucuronidation; dihydrostreptomycin minimally absorbed orally, not metabolized
elimination: Renal — sulfonamides excreted unchanged and as metabolites; aminoglycoside component not absorbed orally, excreted in feces. Half-life: sulfonamides 6–12 hours
interactions: Warfarin — enhanced anticoagulation (sulfonamide protein displacement) — monitor INR. Methotrexate — increased methotrexate toxicity — monitor for myelosuppression. Oral hypoglycemics (sulfonylureas) — enhanced hypoglycemic effect — monitor blood glucose. Methenamine — reduced efficacy — avoid combination. Probenecid — increased sulfonamide levels.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment (eGFR <30mL/min): avoid use; sulfonamide accumulation risk. Hepatic impairment: avoid in severe liver disease. Elderly: use with caution; increased risk of adverse effects.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): nausea, vomiting, loss of appetite, crystalluria (ensure adequate hydration). | Serious: Stevens-Johnson syndrome, toxic epidermal necrolysis (sulfonamides), hemolytic anemia (G6PD deficiency), nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity (aminoglycoside component with prolonged use), bone marrow suppression. | Monitoring: renal function, CBC if prolonged course, ensure fluid intake >2L/day.
contra: Hypersensitivity to sulfonamides or aminoglycosides. G6PD deficiency (hemolysis risk). Severe renal impairment. Pregnancy. Neonates and infants <2 months (kernicterus risk). Porphyria.

name: probiotics (lactobacillus / saccharomyces boulardii)
moa: Probiotic/Digestant — Live microorganisms — Colonize intestinal epithelium → compete with pathogens for nutrients and adhesion sites; produce antimicrobial substances (bacteriocins); enhance intestinal barrier function; modulate immune response (IgA secretion); restore disrupted gut microbiota. Saccharomyces boulardii is a non-pathogenic yeast; Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium are lactic acid bacteria.
commonUses:
1. Acute infectious diarrhea — adjunct to reduce duration by ~1 day.
2. Antibiotic-associated diarrhea — prevention and treatment.
3. Clostridioides difficile infection — adjunct to standard therapy.
4. Traveler's diarrhea — prophylaxis and treatment.
5. Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) — symptom improvement, especially bloating.
6. Helicobacter pylori eradication — adjunct to reduce antibiotic-related GI side effects.
7. Lactose intolerance — Lactobacillus strains assist lactose digestion.
8. Infantile colic — some evidence for Lactobacillus reuteri.
dose: Varies by strain and CFU count. Lactobacillus: 1–10 billion CFU orally once or twice daily. Saccharomyces boulardii: 250–500mg (5–10 billion CFU) orally once or twice daily. Take 1–2 hours apart from antibiotics. Duration: 5–14 days depending on indication.
maxDose: No established maximum; commonly up to 20 billion CFU/day. No overdose reported.
pedDose: Infants and children: Lactobacillus: 1–5 billion CFU orally once or twice daily. Saccharomyces boulardii: 250mg (5 billion CFU) once or twice daily. Sachet contents mixed with water, milk, or soft food. Do not mix with hot liquids. Duration: 5–10 days.
pedAgeLimit: Safe in neonates and preterm infants for most strains. Saccharomyces boulardii: caution in immunocompromised children.
pedDoseAr: الرضع والأطفال: كيس واحد — مرة إلى مرتين يوميًا — يُذاب في ماء أو لبن بدرجة حرارة الغرفة — لمدة 5–10 أيام.
lactationNote: Generally recognized as safe; no adverse effects reported in breastfed infants.
metabolism: Not metabolized — live organisms act locally in the GI tract; no systemic absorption
elimination: Fecal — organisms pass through the GI tract, some colonize transiently, most eliminated in stool
interactions: Antibiotics — reduced probiotic viability — separate administration by at least 2 hours. Antifungals — may reduce Saccharomyces boulardii efficacy — avoid concurrent use. Immunosuppressants — theoretical risk of systemic infection with live organisms — use caution.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment. Hepatic impairment: no adjustment. Elderly: no specific adjustment. Immunocompromised patients: avoid due to risk of translocation and systemic infection.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): flatulence, bloating (mild, transient). | Serious: fungemia/bacteremia (rare, almost exclusively in immunocompromised or critically ill patients with central lines). | Monitoring: none required in immunocompetent patients.
contra: Severe immunocompromise (chemotherapy, transplant, AIDS with CD4 <200). Central venous catheter (Saccharomyces — risk of fungemia). Hypersensitivity to milk proteins (check product for dairy-derived ingredients).

name: loperamide
moa: Antidiarrheal — Opioid receptor agonist (peripheral) — Binds to mu-opioid receptors in intestinal myenteric plexus → inhibits acetylcholine and prostaglandin release → reduces peristalsis, prolongs intestinal transit time, increases anal sphincter tone. Does not cross intact blood-brain barrier at therapeutic doses. Structurally related to pethidine but no analgesic effects.
commonUses:
1. Acute nonspecific diarrhea — symptomatic relief.
2. Traveler's diarrhea — mild to moderate, without fever or dysentery.
3. Chronic diarrhea — IBS-D, short bowel syndrome (adjunct).
4. Diarrhea associated with ileostomy/colostomy — reduces output volume.
5. Chemotherapy-induced diarrhea — mild to moderate cases (after excluding infection).
dose: Acute diarrhea: 4mg orally initially, then 2mg after each loose stool. Maximum 16mg/day. Not to exceed 48 hours without clinical reassessment. Chronic diarrhea: 2–4mg orally once or twice daily, titrated to response.
maxDose: 16mg/day orally. Duration: acute use — 48 hours maximum without medical review.
pedDose: Children 2–5 years: 1mg orally up to 3 times daily. Children 6–12 years: 2mg orally up to 3 times daily. Use weight-based dosing: 0.08–0.24mg/kg/day divided every 6–8 hours. Maximum 3 days without reassessment. Not recommended for children <2 years (risk of paralytic ileus).
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for children <2 years. Use extreme caution in children 2–6 years.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال 2–5 سنوات: 1 مجم (نصف قرص أو نصف كبسولة 2 مجم) — حتى 3 مرات يوميًا — لمدة لا تزيد عن 3 أيام. الأطفال 6–12 سنة: 2 مجم — حتى 3 مرات يوميًا — لمدة لا تزيد عن 3 أيام. لا يُستخدم للأطفال أقل من سنتين.
lactationNote: Minimal amounts excreted in breast milk. Generally considered compatible at recommended doses. Avoid prolonged use.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP3A4 and CYP2C8; undergoes extensive first-pass metabolism; parent drug and metabolites undergo enterohepatic circulation
elimination: Fecal (primarily) — 30% renal; half-life 9–14 hours (prolonged in hepatic impairment)
interactions: CYP3A4 inhibitors (clarithromycin, ketoconazole, ritonavir) — increased loperamide levels and cardiotoxicity risk — avoid combination. CYP3A4 inducers (rifampicin) — reduced loperamide efficacy. Quinidine — P-glycoprotein inhibition → increased CNS effects — avoid. Alcohol — additive CNS depression (dizziness, drowsiness) — caution.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no specific adjustment, but use with caution. Hepatic impairment: reduced first-pass metabolism → increased systemic exposure; monitor for CNS effects. Elderly: use with caution; lower starting dose (2mg).
sideEffects: Common (>1%): constipation, abdominal cramping, dry mouth, dizziness, flatulence. | Serious: paralytic ileus (high doses or in infectious diarrhea), toxic megacolon (if used in dysentery), QT prolongation and torsades de pointes (high doses, concomitant CYP3A4 inhibitors), anaphylaxis (rare). | Monitoring: stop immediately if abdominal distension, no bowel movements; ECG if high doses combined with interacting drugs.
contra: Acute dysentery (bloody stools, high fever — risk of toxic megacolon). Pseudomembranous colitis (C. difficile). Children <2 years. Constipation, ileus, abdominal distension. Acute ulcerative colitis (relative). Known QT prolongation or concomitant QT-prolonging drugs (at high doses).

name: racecadotril
moa: Antidiarrheal — Enkephalinase inhibitor — Inhibits membrane-bound enkephalinase in intestinal epithelium → increases local enkephalin levels → reduces cAMP-mediated water and electrolyte hypersecretion → pure antisecretory effect. No effect on intestinal motility (unlike loperamide). Does not penetrate CNS.
commonUses:
1. Acute watery diarrhea — adults and children, as antisecretory agent.
2. Acute gastroenteritis — symptomatic treatment to reduce stool output.
3. Traveler's diarrhea — mild to moderate, in combination with ORS.
4. Infantile diarrhea — reduces duration and stool volume.
5. Cholera — adjunctive therapy with rehydration.
dose: 100mg orally every 8 hours for up to 7 days. Take before meals. Continue until 2 normal stools are passed. Not to exceed 7 days of treatment.
pedDose: Infants 1–12 months: 10mg orally every 8 hours. Children 1–3 years: 20mg orally every 8 hours. Children >3 years: 30mg orally every 8 hours. Infants <1 month: not recommended. Sachet contents dissolved in water, milk, or food. Duration: up to 5 days.
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for neonates <1 month.
pedDoseAr: الرضع 1–12 شهر: كيس 10 مجم — كل 8 ساعات. الأطفال 1–3 سنوات: كيس 20 مجم — كل 8 ساعات. الأطفال أكبر من 3 سنوات: كيس 30 مجم — كل 8 ساعات — يُذاب في ماء أو لبن — لمدة حتى 5 أيام.
lactationNote: Limited data available. Minimal systemic absorption suggests low risk. Use only if benefit outweighs risk.
metabolism: Hepatic — rapidly hydrolyzed to active metabolite thiorphan; further metabolized to inactive compounds
elimination: Renal — >90% as metabolites; fecal — 8%; half-life of active metabolite: 3 hours
interactions: No significant drug interactions identified. Compatible with ORS. May be used with loperamide (different mechanisms — but not routinely recommended). ACE inhibitors — theoretical increased effect (both affect enkephalin pathway) — not clinically significant.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no dose adjustment required. Hepatic impairment: no data; use with caution in severe hepatic disease. Elderly: no specific adjustment.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): headache, nausea (mild). | Serious: angioedema (rare; discontinue immediately — can be severe), skin rash, urticaria. | Monitoring: discontinue if facial/lip/tongue swelling or difficulty breathing.
contra: Hypersensitivity to racecadotril. Angioedema history with ACE inhibitors (cross-reactivity possible). Bloody diarrhea or fever >38.5°C (requires investigation for invasive bacterial cause). Neonates <1 month.

name: diosmectite
moa: Antidiarrheal/Gastrointestinal protectant — Adsorbent clay mineral — Natural hydrated aluminomagnesium silicate with lamellar structure → binds to intestinal mucus glycoproteins → forms protective film on GI mucosa; adsorbs bacterial toxins (E. coli, C. difficile), viruses (rotavirus), and bile salts; restores intestinal barrier integrity. Not absorbed systemically. Reduces water and electrolyte loss without affecting motility.
commonUses:
1. Acute diarrhea — adults and children, as adsorbent and mucosal protectant.
2. Chronic diarrhea — symptomatic management.
3. Gastroenteritis — reduces stool frequency and duration.
4. Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS-D) — symptom relief, abdominal pain.
5. Gastroesophageal reflux — adjunctive (mucosal coating).
6. Infantile colic — some evidence for reducing crying time.
dose: Acute diarrhea: 3g (1 sachet) orally 3 times daily for 3–5 days. May double initial dose (2 sachets) at start of treatment. Can be taken between meals or mixed with semi-liquid food. Chronic diarrhea: 3g orally twice daily.
maxDose: 9g/day (3 sachets) orally. Duration: 7 days maximum for acute use without reassessment.
pedDose: Infants <1 year: 1.5g (half sachet) orally twice daily for 3 days. Children 1–2 years: 1.5–3g orally twice daily for 3–5 days. Children >2 years: 3g orally twice or 3 times daily for 3–5 days. Sachet dissolved in water, milk, or mixed with semi-liquid food (puree, yogurt).
pedAgeLimit: Safe in neonates and preterm infants. Do not mix with infant formula as primary fluid — use separate feeding.
pedDoseAr: الرضع أقل من سنة: نصف كيس (1.5 جرام) — مرتين يوميًا — يُذاب في ماء أو لبن — لمدة 3 أيام. الأطفال 1–2 سنة: نصف إلى كيس كامل — مرتين يوميًا — لمدة 3–5 أيام. الأطفال أكبر من سنتين: كيس كامل — مرتين إلى ثلاث مرات يوميًا — لمدة 3–5 أيام.
lactationNote: Not absorbed systemically; no risk to breastfed infant.
metabolism: Not metabolized — acts locally in GI tract; no systemic absorption
elimination: Fecal — entirely eliminated unchanged in stool
interactions: Oral medications — reduced absorption due to adsorption by diosmectite — administer other drugs at least 2 hours apart. Antibiotics — reduced antibiotic absorption — separate by 2–3 hours. Digoxin, warfarin — significant interaction potential — separate by 3 hours. Oral contraceptives — theoretical reduced absorption — separate administration.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment (not absorbed). Hepatic impairment: no adjustment (not absorbed). Elderly: no specific adjustment but ensure adequate fluid intake to prevent constipation.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): constipation (mild, reversible — reduce dose if occurs), flatulence. | Serious: none significant. Intestinal obstruction risk in patients with severe constipation/motility disorders. | Monitoring: none required; ensure adequate hydration.
contra: Intestinal obstruction or severe chronic constipation. Fructose intolerance (some formulations contain sucrose). Hypersensitivity to diosmectite.

name: rifaximin
moa: Antibiotic — Rifamycin derivative — Binds to beta-subunit of bacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase → inhibits RNA synthesis → bactericidal. Non-systemic (<0.4% oral absorption); acts locally in GI tract. Broad-spectrum Gram-positive and Gram-negative activity including E. coli, Clostridium, Enterococcus; minimal effect on normal gut flora compared to systemic antibiotics.
commonUses:
1. Traveler's diarrhea — caused by noninvasive E. coli.
2. Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO) — off-label but widely used.
3. Hepatic encephalopathy — prevention of recurrence.
4. Irritable bowel syndrome with diarrhea (IBS-D) — FDA approved.
5. Clostridioides difficile infection — adjunct or alternative therapy.
6. Pouchitis — inflammatory condition of ileal pouch.
7. Diverticulitis — uncomplicated, off-label use.
8. COVID-19-associated GI symptoms — investigational.
dose: Traveler's diarrhea: 200mg orally 3 times daily for 3 days. Hepatic encephalopathy: 550mg orally twice daily. IBS-D: 550mg orally 3 times daily for 14 days; may repeat once if recurrence. SIBO: 400–550mg orally 3 times daily for 10–14 days. Take with or without food.
pedDose: Children >12 years: same as adult dosing. Children 2–12 years: traveler's diarrhea — 200mg orally 3 times daily for 3 days (limited data). Hepatic encephalopathy in children — no established dosing. SIBO: 200–400mg orally 3 times daily for 10 days (off-label, specialist use). Safety and efficacy not established for children <2 years.
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for children <2 years.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال أكبر من 12 سنة: مثل جرعة الكبار. الأطفال 2–12 سنة: 200 مجم — ثلاث مرات يوميًا — لمدة 3 أيام (لإسهال المسافرين).
lactationNote: Minimal systemic absorption; limited data in human lactation. Considered low risk. Monitor infant for diarrhea.
metabolism: Minimal hepatic metabolism (<0.4% absorbed); undergoes some metabolism to 25-desacetylrifaximin (active)
elimination: Fecal — 97% unchanged; renal — <0.1%; half-life 5–6 hours (only relevant for systemically absorbed fraction)
interactions: P-glycoprotein inhibitors (cyclosporine, verapamil) — increase systemic rifaximin exposure — monitor for systemic effects. Warfarin — INR changes reported rarely — monitor INR. Oral contraceptives — theoretical reduced efficacy (rifamycin class effect) — additional contraception advised. No significant CYP450 interactions.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment required (minimal absorption). Hepatic impairment: in hepatic encephalopathy patients, systemic exposure may be increased due to increased intestinal permeability; no dose adjustment per manufacturer but monitor. Elderly: no specific adjustment.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): peripheral edema, nausea, dizziness, fatigue, flatulence, headache. | Serious: Clostridioides difficile-associated diarrhea, hypersensitivity reactions (angioedema, anaphylaxis — rare). | Monitoring: for hepatic encephalopathy — monitor mental status; for IBS-D — assess symptom response at 2-week follow-up.
contra: Hypersensitivity to rifamycin antibiotics (rifampin, rifabutin). Diarrhea complicated by fever and/or blood in stool (requires systemic antibiotics).

name: diloxanide / metronidazole
moa: Antiprotozoal combination — Dichloroacetamide derivative + Nitroimidazole — Diloxanide: acts locally in intestinal lumen against E. histolytica cysts (mechanism not fully characterized; interferes with protein synthesis). Metronidazole: reduced by ferredoxin in anaerobic organisms → toxic intermediates damage DNA → tissue amebicide. Together: systemic tissue clearance + luminal cyst eradication.
commonUses:
1. Amoebiasis — combination therapy; metronidazole for tissue amebicide, diloxanide for luminal amebicide.
2. Asymptomatic cyst passers of Entamoeba histolytica — diloxanide alone.
3. Intestinal amoebiasis — after tissue-phase treatment with metronidazole.
4. Prevention of invasive amoebiasis — in endemic areas after metronidazole course.
dose: Diloxanide furoate 500mg + metronidazole 200–400mg combination: 1 tablet orally 3 times daily for 5–10 days depending on indication. For asymptomatic cyst carriers: diloxanide alone 500mg 3 times daily for 10 days. Take with food to reduce GI side effects.
maxDose: Diloxanide: 1500mg/day; Metronidazole: 1200mg/day in this combination. Maximum duration: 10 days.
pedDose: Children >2 years: diloxanide 20mg/kg/day + metronidazole 10–15mg/kg/day orally divided every 8 hours for 5–10 days. Children <2 years: safety and efficacy not established. Use with specialist guidance only.
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for children <2 years.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال أكبر من سنتين: الجرعة تحسب بالوزن — حوالي 20 مجم/كجم من الديلوكسانيد يوميًا — مقسمة كل 8 ساعات — لمدة 5–10 أيام.
lactationNote: Metronidazole excreted in milk; diloxanide safety profile limited. Consider withholding breastfeeding or using alternative if prolonged treatment needed.
metabolism: Diloxanide: hydrolyzed in GI tract to diloxanide (active) → further glucuronidation. Metronidazole: hepatic oxidation and glucuronidation.
elimination: Diloxanide: renal >90% as glucuronide conjugate; half-life 6 hours. Metronidazole: renal 60–80%; half-life 6–8 hours.
interactions: Alcohol — disulfiram-like reaction with metronidazole component — avoid during and 48 hours after. Warfarin — enhanced anticoagulant effect — monitor INR. Lithium — increased lithium levels — monitor. Phenytoin — reduced metronidazole efficacy.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment (eGFR <10): reduce metronidazole component by 50%. Hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C): reduce metronidazole; prolonged half-life. Elderly: no specific adjustment.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): metallic taste, nausea, flatulence, dark urine (benign from metronidazole). | Serious: peripheral neuropathy with prolonged use, seizures (metronidazole), angioedema (diloxanide — rare). | Monitoring: signs of neuropathy on courses >7 days.
contra: First trimester pregnancy (relative). Hypersensitivity to nitroimidazoles or diloxanide. Alcohol use (during and 48 hours post-treatment). Blood dyscrasias — metronidazole may exacerbate.

name: oral rehydration salts (ors) / zinc
moa: Rehydration solution + Mineral supplement — Oral rehydration salts exploit sodium-glucose cotransport in small intestine → water follows passively → effective even during active secretion. WHO formula: glucose 75mmol/L, sodium 75mmol/L, chloride 65mmol/L, potassium 20mmol/L, citrate 10mmol/L, osmolarity 245mOsm/L. Zinc: essential micronutrient → enhances immune function, intestinal mucosal repair, reduces duration and severity of diarrhea.
commonUses:
1. Acute diarrhea — prevention and treatment of dehydration (WHO recommended).
2. Cholera — cornerstone of management.
3. Acute gastroenteritis — all ages, first-line therapy.
4. Traveler's diarrhea — rehydration component.
5. Vomiting — prevention of dehydration when oral intake tolerated.
6. Heat exhaustion/dehydration — rehydration.
7. Zinc supplementation — reduces severity and duration of acute diarrhea in children (WHO/UNICEF recommendation).
dose: ORS: 200–400mL after each loose stool, or as needed for thirst/hydration maintenance. For moderate dehydration: 50–100mL/kg over 2–4 hours. Dissolve contents of sachet in prescribed volume of clean water (usually 200mL or 1L per packet). Do not use less/more water than instructed. Zinc: 20mg elemental zinc orally once daily for 10–14 days (adjunct in acute diarrhea).
maxDose: ORS: no maximum — guided by thirst and ongoing losses. Zinc: 40mg/day maximum.
pedDose: ORS: Infants and children — 50–100mL/kg over 2–4 hours for mild to moderate dehydration. After each loose stool: <2 years — 50–100mL; 2–10 years — 100–200mL; >10 years — as adult. Zinc: <6 months — 10mg elemental zinc orally once daily for 10 days; >6 months — 20mg orally once daily for 10 days.
pedAgeLimit: Safe from birth. Do not give zinc to infants <1 month (limited data).
pedDoseAr: محلول الجفاف: يُذاب الكيس في الكمية المحددة من الماء — يُعطى 50–100 مل لكل كجم على مدى 2–4 ساعات — ثم 50–100 مل بعد كل إسهال. الزنك: الرضع أقل من 6 شهور — 10 مجم يوميًا — أكبر من 6 شهور — 20 مجم يوميًا — لمدة 10 أيام.
lactationNote: ORS and zinc are fully compatible with breastfeeding. Continue breastfeeding during diarrhea.
metabolism: Not metabolized — ORS components are normal physiological electrolytes and glucose. Zinc is absorbed in duodenum/jejunum and distributed throughout body.
elimination: Renal — excess water and electrolytes excreted via kidneys; zinc primarily excreted in feces with some renal elimination
interactions: Zinc: fluoroquinolones, tetracyclines — reduced absorption (chelated by zinc) — administer antibiotics 2 hours before or 4–6 hours after zinc. Iron — competition for absorption — separate by 2 hours. Penicillamine — reduced efficacy — avoid or separate. ORS: no significant drug interactions.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: use with caution; monitor for hypernatremia, hyperkalemia. Hepatic impairment: no adjustment. Elderly: monitor for fluid overload in cardiac/renal disease.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Zinc — nausea, metallic taste, vomiting (dose-dependent; reduce dose or give with food). ORS — none significant when properly diluted. | Serious: ORS — hypernatremia if incorrectly mixed (too little water), water intoxication if over-diluted. Zinc — copper deficiency with prolonged high-dose use. | Monitoring: hydration status, urine output, weight.
contra: Severe dehydration requiring IV fluids (ORS alone insufficient). Intestinal obstruction or ileus. Unconsciousness or inability to drink. Zinc: hypersensitivity.

name: pectin / kaolin
moa: Antidiarrheal/Adsorbent — Natural clay + polysaccharide — Kaolin: hydrated aluminum silicate adsorbent → binds bacterial toxins (E. coli enterotoxins), bile acids, and water → reduces stool liquidity. Pectin: soluble fiber → absorbs water, forms gel-like consistency → adds bulk to stool. Both act locally in GI tract; not systemically absorbed. Demulcent effect soothes irritated mucosa.
commonUses:
1. Acute nonspecific diarrhea — symptomatic relief as adsorbent.
2. Gastroenteritis — reduces stool liquidity and frequency.
3. Traveler's diarrhea — mild cases as adjunct.
4. Chronic diarrhea — symptomatic management.
5. Irritable bowel syndrome — diarrhea-predominant, for stool consistency.
6. Food poisoning — adsorb toxins from GI tract (limited evidence).
dose: 15–30mL oral suspension every 4–6 hours as needed, or 2 tablets/capsules every 4–6 hours. Maximum 6 doses per day. Shake suspension well before use. Take between meals and at bedtime. Separate from other oral medications by 2 hours.
maxDose: 6 doses/day. Not to exceed 48 hours without medical review.
pedDose: Children 3–6 years: 5–10mL suspension every 4–6 hours. Children 6–12 years: 10–15mL suspension every 4–6 hours. Maximum 4 doses/day. Shake well. Do not use in children <3 years without medical advice. Separate from other medications by 2 hours.
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for children <3 years. Use with caution in children 3–6 years.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال 3–6 سنوات: 5–10 مل معلق — كل 4–6 ساعات. الأطفال 6–12 سنة: 10–15 مل معلق — كل 4–6 ساعات — بحد أقصى 4 جرعات يوميًا — يُرج جيدًا.
lactationNote: Not systemically absorbed; considered safe during breastfeeding.
metabolism: Not metabolized — both compounds act locally in GI tract; no systemic absorption.
elimination: Fecal — entirely eliminated unchanged in stool.
interactions: Oral medications — reduced absorption due to adsorption by kaolin/pectin — administer other drugs at least 2 hours before or after. Digoxin — significantly reduced absorption — separate by 3 hours. Antibiotics — reduced efficacy — separate by 2–3 hours. Warfarin — possible reduced absorption — monitor INR.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: kaolin contains aluminum — avoid prolonged use in severe renal impairment (theoretical aluminum accumulation, though minimal absorption). Hepatic impairment: no adjustment. Elderly: monitor for constipation; ensure adequate hydration.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): constipation (mild, reversible), abdominal bloating. | Serious: intestinal obstruction (rare, in patients with severe constipation or motility disorders), aluminum toxicity with prolonged use in renal impairment (theoretical). | Monitoring: none specific; ensure adequate fluid intake.
contra: Intestinal obstruction or fecal impaction. Severe dehydration (requires rehydration first). Hypersensitivity to kaolin or pectin. Do not use in dysentery (bloody stools, fever — may mask serious infection).

name: tiliquinol / tilbroquinol
moa: Antiprotozoal/Antifungal — Hydroxyquinoline derivatives — Act locally in intestinal lumen; mechanism incompletely defined but involve chelation of metal ions essential for protozoal/fungal metabolism, disruption of mitochondrial function. Tiliquinol and tilbroquinol are luminal amebicides; not systemically absorbed, targeting E. histolytica cysts and trophozoites in intestinal lumen. Narrow spectrum.
commonUses:
1. Intestinal amoebiasis — luminal amebicide for E. histolytica.
2. Asymptomatic cyst passers — eradication of cysts.
3. Amoebic dysentery — as part of combination therapy.
4. Giardiasis — alternative treatment (limited data).
5. Intestinal candidiasis — some activity against fungal overgrowth.
dose: Tiliquinol 100mg + tilbroquinol 200mg combination: 1–2 capsules orally 3 times daily for 5–10 days depending on severity. Take with meals. Course may be repeated after 2–3 weeks if needed.
maxDose: 6 capsules/day. Maximum treatment duration: 10 days per course.
pedDose: Children >2 years: half the adult dose — 1 capsule orally 2–3 times daily for 5–7 days. Children <2 years: safety not established; avoid use. Follow weight-based dosing from product insert.
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for children <2 years.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال أكبر من سنتين: كبسولة واحدة — مرتين إلى ثلاث مرات يوميًا — مع الأكل — لمدة 5–7 أيام.
lactationNote: Minimal systemic absorption suggests low risk; limited data available. Use only if clearly needed.
metabolism: Minimal metabolism — acts locally in GI lumen; no significant systemic absorption.
elimination: Fecal — nearly entirely eliminated unchanged in stool.
interactions: Oral iron preparations — reduced absorption of both drugs — separate by 2–3 hours. Antacids containing aluminum/magnesium — reduced efficacy of hydroxyquinolines — separate administration. No known systemic drug interactions due to lack of absorption.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment (not absorbed). Hepatic impairment: caution in severe liver disease (limited data). Elderly: no specific adjustment.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): nausea, abdominal discomfort, diarrhea (mild). | Serious: peripheral neuropathy with prolonged high-dose use (hydroxyquinoline class effect — rare), optic neuritis (very rare). | Monitoring: discontinue if tingling, numbness, or visual changes occur.
contra: Hypersensitivity to hydroxyquinolines. Optic nerve disease or peripheral neuropathy. Severe hepatic impairment. Pregnancy (relative — limited safety data).

name: paracetamol (acetaminophen)
moa: Analgesic/Antipyretic — Para-aminophenol derivative — Mechanism not fully defined; proposed: COX-2 inhibition in CNS (hypothalamus) → reduced prostaglandin synthesis in thermoregulatory center → antipyresis. Influences descending serotonergic pain pathways. Minimal peripheral COX inhibition → no significant anti-inflammatory effect. Does not inhibit platelet aggregation. No gastric mucosal damage.
commonUses:
1. Mild to moderate pain — headache, toothache, musculoskeletal pain, dysmenorrhea.
2. Fever — antipyretic of first choice.
3. Osteoarthritis — first-line analgesic.
4. Postoperative pain — as part of multimodal analgesia.
5. Migraine — in combination with antiemetics.
6. Pediatric pain and fever — first-line agent.
7. Post-vaccination fever prophylaxis (controversial).
dose: 500–1000mg orally every 4–6 hours as needed. Maximum 4000mg/day (3000mg/day preferred for long-term use). IV: 1000mg in 100mL NS or D5W, infuse over 15 minutes every 6 hours, or 650mg IV every 4 hours. Rectal: 650mg every 4–6 hours. Take oral form with or without food.
maxDose: 4000mg/day orally (3000mg/day for chronic use or in patients with risk factors). Single dose: 1000mg. IV: 4000mg/day. Toxicity threshold: >150mg/kg single ingestion or >7.5g/day.
pedDose: Oral: 10–15mg/kg every 4–6 hours. Maximum 5 doses/day (75mg/kg/day). Neonate (28–32 weeks): 10–12mg/kg every 6–8 hours. IV: <10kg — 7.5mg/kg every 6 hours; >10kg — 15mg/kg every 6 hours, max 60mg/kg/day. Rectal: 15–20mg/kg every 6 hours. Weight-based dosing preferred.
pedAgeLimit: Safe from birth (dose adjustment for preterm neonates). IV formulation: approved for >28 weeks gestational age.
pedDoseAr: بالفم: 10–15 مجم لكل كجم — كل 4–6 ساعات — بحد أقصى 5 مرات يوميًا. التحاميل: 15–20 مجم لكل كجم — كل 6 ساعات. الحقن الوريدي: 15 مجم لكل كجم — كل 6 ساعات.
lactationNote: Minimal excretion in breast milk; analgesic of choice during breastfeeding at standard doses.
metabolism: Hepatic — primarily glucuronidation (60%) and sulfation (30%); minor CYP2E1 oxidation → toxic NAPQI metabolite (glutathione-detoxified). In overdose, glutathione depletion → NAPQI accumulation → hepatotoxicity.
elimination: Renal — 90–100% as metabolites; half-life 2–4 hours (prolonged in hepatic impairment and overdose).
interactions: Alcohol (chronic) — CYP2E1 induction → increased NAPQI → hepatotoxicity risk even at therapeutic doses — avoid heavy alcohol use. Warfarin — enhanced anticoagulant effect with prolonged high-dose paracetamol (>2g/day for >3 days) — monitor INR. Isoniazid — increased hepatotoxicity risk. Anticonvulsants (phenytoin, carbamazepine) — increased NAPQI production — reduced efficacy and increased toxicity risk.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment (eGFR <30): reduce dosing interval to every 8 hours; no reduction in single dose. Hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh B/C): reduce dose to 2000–3000mg/day maximum. Chronic alcoholism: reduce maximum to 2000mg/day. Elderly frail patients: consider 2000–3000mg/day maximum.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): none significant at therapeutic doses. | Serious: acute liver failure (overdose — single ingestion >7.5g or repeated supratherapeutic dosing), Stevens-Johnson syndrome (rare), anaphylaxis (very rare). IV formulation: hypotension, tachycardia with rapid infusion (infuse over 15 minutes). | Monitoring: pain scores, temperature, liver function tests in overdose or prolonged use, INR if on warfarin.
contra: Hypersensitivity to paracetamol. Severe hepatic impairment (relative — dose reduction needed). Acute overdose — N-acetylcysteine protocol indicated.

name: diclofenac
moa: Analgesic/Anti-inflammatory — NSAID (phenylacetic acid derivative) — Non-selective COX-1 and COX-2 inhibitor → blocks prostaglandin synthesis from arachidonic acid → reduces inflammation, pain, fever. Inhibits platelet aggregation (reversible, unlike aspirin). Additional mechanisms: inhibition of leukotriene synthesis, modulation of opioid receptors centrally.
commonUses:
1. Acute musculoskeletal pain — strains, sprains, low back pain.
2. Osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis — chronic inflammatory pain.
3. Dysmenorrhea — primary and secondary.
4. Acute gout — rapid pain relief.
5. Postoperative pain — multimodal analgesia.
6. Renal colic — analgesic effect.
7. Migraine — acute attack (with antiemetic).
8. Dental pain — post-extraction or pulpitis.
9. Tendonitis and bursitis — inflammatory conditions.
dose: Oral: 25–50mg every 8 hours. Maximum 150mg/day. Extended-release: 100mg once daily. IM injection: 75mg deep intramuscular injection as single dose or twice daily; maximum 150mg/day for 2 days. Rectal: 100mg once daily. Topical gel/cream: apply 2–4g to affected area 3–4 times daily. Take oral forms with food.
pedDose: Children >1 year: 0.5–1mg/kg orally every 8 hours. Maximum 3mg/kg/day. Not routinely recommended for children <1 year. For juvenile idiopathic arthritis: 1–3mg/kg/day divided every 8–12 hours. IM not recommended in children.
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for children <1 year. Avoid in children <14 years (some guidelines prefer alternatives).
pedDoseAr: الأطفال أكبر من سنة: 0.5–1 مجم لكل كجم — كل 8 ساعات — بحد أقصى 3 مجم/كجم يوميًا — مع الأكل.
lactationNote: Minimal excretion in breast milk. Generally considered compatible at short-term, low doses. Avoid prolonged high-dose use.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP2C9 (major), CYP3A4 (minor); undergoes enterohepatic circulation; forms inactive metabolites via glucuronidation and sulfation.
elimination: Renal — 65% as metabolites; fecal — 35%; half-life 1–2 hours (prolonged in hepatic impairment).
interactions: Aspirin — additive GI toxicity + reduced diclofenac efficacy — avoid combination. Warfarin — increased bleeding risk — avoid or monitor INR closely. ACE inhibitors/ARBs — reduced antihypertensive effect + increased nephrotoxicity risk — monitor BP and renal function. Methotrexate — increased methotrexate toxicity — avoid if possible. Lithium — increased lithium levels — monitor. Corticosteroids — increased GI ulcer risk. Diuretics — reduced diuretic efficacy. SSRIs — increased bleeding risk.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment (eGFR 30–60): reduce dose 50%; eGFR <30: avoid (risk of acute kidney injury). Hepatic impairment: reduce dose, monitor liver function. Elderly: lowest effective dose for shortest duration; consider PPI for GI protection.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): GI upset (nausea, dyspepsia, diarrhea), headache, dizziness, elevated transaminases (mild), injection site pain (IM). | Serious: GI bleeding/perforation, peptic ulcer, acute kidney injury, hypertension exacerbation, myocardial infarction/stroke (increased cardiovascular risk with prolonged use), hepatotoxicity, Stevens-Johnson syndrome (rare), aplastic anemia (very rare). | Monitoring: renal function (especially in elderly, diuretic users), liver function tests, blood pressure, signs of GI bleeding (stool color, hemoglobin), cardiovascular risk assessment for long-term use.
contra: Active or history of peptic ulcer. Severe renal impairment (eGFR <30). Severe heart failure. Third trimester of pregnancy. Aspirin/NSAID allergy (including asthma, urticaria after aspirin). Coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) surgery — perioperative period.

name: ibuprofen
moa: Analgesic/Anti-inflammatory/Antipyretic — NSAID (propionic acid derivative) — Non-selective COX-1 and COX-2 inhibitor → blocks conversion of arachidonic acid to prostaglandins → reduces inflammation, pain, and fever. Reversibly inhibits platelet aggregation. Induces closure of ductus arteriosus in neonates via prostaglandin inhibition.
commonUses:
1. Mild to moderate pain — headache, dental, musculoskeletal.
2. Fever — antipyretic alternative to paracetamol.
3. Osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis — chronic inflammatory pain.
4. Dysmenorrhea — first-line NSAID.
5. Acute gout — rapid symptom relief.
6. Patent ductus arteriosus (IV form) — closure in premature infants.
7. Migraine — acute attack.
8. Postoperative pain — multimodal analgesia.
9. Juvenile idiopathic arthritis — first-line NSAID in children.
dose: Analgesic: 200–400mg orally every 4–6 hours as needed. Maximum 1200mg/day over-the-counter. Anti-inflammatory: 400–800mg orally every 6–8 hours. Maximum 2400mg/day (prescription). Take with food or milk to reduce GI upset.
maxDose: OTC: 1200mg/day. Prescription: 2400mg/day (3200mg/day for some rheumatologic conditions under medical supervision). Single dose: 800mg.
pedDose: Fever/pain: 5–10mg/kg orally every 6–8 hours. Maximum 30–40mg/kg/day. Children 3–6 months: 5mg/kg every 8 hours. Juvenile idiopathic arthritis: 30–40mg/kg/day divided every 6–8 hours. PDA closure (IV): 10mg/kg IV, then 5mg/kg at 24 and 48 hours (neonates).
pedAgeLimit: Oral: >3 months (safe). IV for PDA: premature neonates >500g, >28 weeks gestational age.
pedDoseAr: 5–10 مجم لكل كجم — كل 6–8 ساعات — مع الأكل — بحد أقصى 30–40 مجم/كجم يوميًا. للأطفال أكبر من 3 شهور.
lactationNote: Minimal excretion in breast milk; generally considered compatible at standard doses. Preferred NSAID in lactation.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP2C9 (major), CYP2C8; undergoes extensive oxidation and glucuronidation.
elimination: Renal — 45–80% as metabolites; half-life 2–4 hours (10 hours in neonate PDA closure).
interactions: Aspirin — reduced ibuprofen efficacy + increased GI risk — avoid. Warfarin — increased bleeding risk — monitor INR. ACE inhibitors/ARBs — reduced antihypertensive effect and increased nephrotoxicity — monitor BP and renal function. Methotrexate — increased toxicity — avoid with high-dose methotrexate. Lithium — increased lithium levels — monitor. Furosemide — reduced diuretic effect. SSRIs — additive bleeding risk.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment (eGFR 30–60): reduce dose; eGFR <30: avoid. Hepatic impairment: reduce dose in severe disease. Elderly: lowest effective dose for shortest duration; consider PPI.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): dyspepsia, nausea, diarrhea, abdominal pain, headache, dizziness. | Serious: GI bleeding/perforation, peptic ulcer, acute kidney injury, hypertension, myocardial infarction/stroke (dose and duration-dependent), hepatotoxicity, aseptic meningitis, Stevens-Johnson syndrome (rare). | Monitoring: renal function, liver function, blood pressure, signs of GI bleeding, cardiovascular risk (long-term use).
contra: Active or history of peptic ulcer. Severe renal impairment. Severe heart failure. CABG perioperative period. Aspirin/NSAID hypersensitivity. Third trimester pregnancy.

name: ketorolac
moa: Analgesic/Anti-inflammatory — NSAID (heteroaryl acetic acid derivative) — Potent non-selective COX-1 and COX-2 inhibitor → blocks prostaglandin synthesis from arachidonic acid → analgesic, anti-inflammatory, antipyretic. 5–10 times more potent as analgesic than anti-inflammatory. Structurally related to indomethacin. Inhibits platelet aggregation reversibly.
commonUses:
1. Moderate to severe acute pain — short-term (≤5 days) management.
2. Postoperative pain — first-line parenteral NSAID.
3. Renal colic — analgesic effect equivalent to opioids.
4. Acute musculoskeletal trauma — fractures, dislocations.
5. Sickle cell crisis pain — adjunctive.
6. Ophthalmic postoperative inflammation and pain — topical formulation.
7. Acute migraine — second-line parenteral option.
dose: IM: 30–60mg single dose, then 15–30mg every 6 hours. Maximum 120mg/day (90mg/day for elderly/patients <50kg). IV: 15–30mg every 6 hours (slow push over >15 seconds). Oral: 10mg every 4–6 hours. Maximum treatment duration: 5 days. Take oral form with food. Not for chronic use.
maxDose: 120mg/day IM/IV (90mg/day for age >65, weight <50kg, or renal impairment). Duration: 5 days maximum.
pedDose: Children >2 years: 0.5mg/kg IV/IM every 6 hours. Maximum 30mg/dose. Maximum 2mg/kg/day. Maximum duration 48 hours. Not recommended for children <2 years. Oral: 0.2–0.4mg/kg every 6 hours.
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for children <2 years.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال أكبر من سنتين: 0.5 مجم لكل كجم — في الوريد أو العضل — كل 6 ساعات — بحد أقصى 30 مجم للجرعة — لمدة لا تزيد عن 48 ساعة.
lactationNote: Excreted in breast milk; generally considered compatible at low doses for short duration. Monitor infant for GI effects.
metabolism: Hepatic — glucuronidation (major), hydroxylation; undergoes enterohepatic circulation.
elimination: Renal — 90% as metabolites; half-life 4–6 hours (prolonged in elderly >9 hours, renal impairment >13 hours).
interactions: Other NSAIDs or aspirin — additive GI and renal toxicity — avoid combination. Warfarin — increased bleeding risk — monitor INR closely. Methotrexate — increased toxicity — avoid. ACE inhibitors/ARBs — reduced antihypertensive effect, nephrotoxicity — avoid if possible. Lithium — increased lithium levels — monitor. Probenecid — reduced ketorolac clearance — avoid. Pentoxifylline — increased bleeding risk.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment (eGFR 30–60): reduce dose to 60mg/day, monitor renal function; eGFR <30: avoid. Hepatic impairment: caution; reduce dose in severe disease. Elderly (>65 years): maximum 90mg/day. Weight <50kg: maximum 90mg/day.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): GI pain, nausea, dyspepsia, diarrhea, injection site pain, drowsiness, dizziness. | Serious: GI bleeding (higher risk than other NSAIDs, especially in elderly), peptic ulcer, acute kidney injury, anaphylaxis, bronchospasm, hepatotoxicity, myocardial infarction (longer use). | Monitoring: renal function before and during treatment, blood pressure, urine output, signs of GI bleeding (black stool, hematemesis), CBC if prolonged use.
contra: Active or history of peptic ulcer. Severe renal impairment. CABG perioperative. Aspirin/NSAID hypersensitivity. Bleeding disorders or anticoagulant therapy (relative). Third trimester pregnancy. Pediatrics <2 years. Neuraxial anesthesia preparation (increased epidural hematoma risk). Use as prophylactic analgesic before surgery. Lactation (relative — single dose acceptable).

name: tizanidine
moa: Muscle relaxant/Antispasmodic — Alpha-2 adrenergic agonist (centrally acting) — Stimulates presynaptic alpha-2 receptors in spinal cord → inhibits release of excitatory amino acids (glutamate, aspartate) from presynaptic terminals → reduces facilitatory input to motor neurons → decreases muscle spasticity. Structurally related to clonidine but with less hypotensive effect due to lower affinity for imidazoline receptors.
commonUses:
1. Muscle spasticity — multiple sclerosis, spinal cord injury.
2. Acute muscle spasms — low back pain, cervical strain.
3. Chronic tension-type headache — off-label, muscle relaxant effect.
4. Myofascial pain syndrome — symptomatic relief.
5. Fibromyalgia — off-label, reduces muscle tension.
6. Trigeminal neuralgia — adjunctive (limited evidence).
7. Spasticity in cerebral palsy — pediatric use.
dose: Initial: 2mg orally every 6–8 hours as needed. May increase by 2–4mg per dose every 1–4 days. Usual maintenance: 8mg orally every 6–8 hours. Maximum 36mg/day. Take consistently with or without food (food changes absorption).
maxDose: 36mg/day (3 doses of 12mg or 4 doses of 9mg). Single dose: 16mg.
pedDose: Children >2 years: 0.05mg/kg/dose orally every 6–8 hours initially. Titrate gradually (0.05mg/kg every 3 days). Maximum 0.3mg/kg/dose. Limited to specialist use. Not for children <2 years.
pedAgeLimit: Safety and efficacy not established for children <2 years. Use with specialist guidance only.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال أكبر من سنتين: 0.05 مجم لكل كجم — كل 6–8 ساعات — تزيد الجرعة تدريجيًا تحت إشراف الطبيب — بحد أقصى 0.3 مجم/كجم للجرعة.
lactationNote: Excreted in animal milk; no human data. Use only if benefit outweighs risk. Monitor infant for sedation.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP1A2 (major); extensive first-pass metabolism; active metabolites have 50% of parent compound activity.
elimination: Renal — 60% as metabolites; fecal — 20%; half-life 2.5 hours (prolonged in hepatic impairment: 4–9 hours, renal impairment: up to 14 hours).
interactions: CYP1A2 inhibitors (ciprofloxacin, fluvoxamine, cimetidine, oral contraceptives) — significantly increased tizanidine levels → profound hypotension, sedation — contraindicated. CYP1A2 inducers (smoking, rifampicin) — reduced tizanidine efficacy. Alcohol — additive CNS depression — avoid. Other CNS depressants — enhanced sedation — caution. Antihypertensives — additive hypotension — monitor BP.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment (eGFR <25): reduce dose by 50%; start at 2mg/day and titrate slowly. Hepatic impairment: avoid use (extensively metabolized). Elderly: start at 2mg/day; monitor for hypotension and sedation.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): dry mouth, sedation, dizziness, asthenia, fatigue, hypotension (dose-dependent), bradycardia. | Serious: hepatotoxicity (rare; monitor LFTs at baseline and periodically), hallucinations, severe hypotension, rebound hypertension with abrupt discontinuation (less than clonidine). | Monitoring: liver function tests at baseline, 1 month, and periodically; blood pressure; sedation level (caution with driving); renal function.
contra: Concomitant use with ciprofloxacin or fluvoxamine (CYP1A2 inhibitors — fatal drug interaction possible). Severe hepatic impairment. Hypersensitivity.

name: orphenadrine
moa: Muscle relaxant/Anticholinergic — Centrally acting antimuscarinic — Blocks muscarinic acetylcholine receptors in CNS → reduces facilitatory cholinergic input to motor neurons → decreases muscle spasm. Also weak antihistaminic and local anesthetic properties. Structurally related to diphenhydramine (antihistamine). Does not directly relax skeletal muscle.
commonUses:
1. Acute musculoskeletal pain — adjunct with rest and physical therapy.
2. Muscle spasms — low back pain, cervical strain.
3. Tension headache — as muscle relaxant component.
4. Parkinson's disease — anticholinergic for rigidity (second-line).
5. Drug-induced extrapyramidal symptoms — anticholinergic effect.
dose: Oral: 100mg twice daily. IM: 60mg every 12 hours. IV: 60mg every 12 hours (slow push). Take oral form with or without food. Maximum 400mg/day.
pedDose: Children >6 years: 25–50mg orally every 8 hours. Maximum 150mg/day. Limited data; not routinely recommended for children. Safety <6 years not established.
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for children <6 years.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال أكبر من 6 سنوات: 25–50 مجم — كل 8 ساعات — بحد أقصى 150 مجم يوميًا — تحت إشراف الطبيب.
lactationNote: Excreted in breast milk; anticholinergic effects in infant possible. Use only if benefit outweighs risk.
metabolism: Hepatic — extensive first-pass metabolism; CYP450 enzymes (mainly CYP2B6, CYP3A4); active metabolite nororphenadrine.
elimination: Renal — 60% as metabolites; half-life 14 hours (parent), 10 hours (active metabolite).
interactions: Other anticholinergics — additive anticholinergic effects (dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention). CNS depressants (alcohol, benzodiazepines) — enhanced sedation. MAO inhibitors — possible hypertensive crisis — avoid. Propoxyphene — additive CNS depression — avoid. Antipsychotics — increased risk of hyperthermia.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: use with caution; reduce dose in severe impairment. Hepatic impairment: avoid in severe disease. Elderly: start with lower dose (50mg); increased sensitivity to anticholinergic effects.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): dry mouth, blurred vision, dizziness, drowsiness, urinary hesitancy, constipation, tachycardia. | Serious: confusion (especially elderly), arrhythmia, angle-closure glaucoma precipitation, ileus. | Monitoring: symptom improvement, signs of anticholinergic toxicity, intraocular pressure in glaucoma patients.
contra: Angle-closure glaucoma. Urinary retention (BPH with obstruction). Myasthenia gravis. Megacolon/intestinal obstruction. Hypersensitivity.

name: tramadol
moa: Analgesic — Opioid receptor agonist + SNRI — Weak mu-opioid receptor agonist (M1 metabolite 200× more potent) → inhibits ascending pain pathways. Additionally inhibits reuptake of norepinephrine and serotonin → enhances descending inhibitory pain pathways (dual mechanism). Less respiratory depression than morphine at equianalgesic doses. Not a controlled substance in some countries (monitored in many due to abuse potential).
commonUses:
1. Moderate to moderately severe pain — acute and chronic.
2. Postoperative pain — multimodal analgesia.
3. Neuropathic pain — second-line when first-line agents ineffective.
4. Osteoarthritis — when paracetamol/NSAIDs insufficient.
5. Cancer pain — step 2 WHO analgesic ladder.
6. Fibromyalgia — off-label, modest benefit.
7. Restless legs syndrome — off-label (limited evidence).
dose: Immediate-release: 50–100mg orally every 4–6 hours as needed. Maximum 400mg/day (300mg/day for patients >75 years). Start at 25mg/day if hepatic/renal impairment. Extended-release: 100mg orally once daily, titrate by 100mg every 5 days. Maximum 300mg/day. Take with or without food.
maxDose: 400mg/day immediate-release (300mg for age >75); 300mg/day extended-release. Single dose: 100mg.
pedDose: Children >12 years: 1–2mg/kg orally every 4–6 hours. Maximum 400mg/day. Children 1–12 years: 1–2mg/kg/dose every 6 hours (specialist use only). Not recommended for children <1 year.
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for children <1 year. Use with caution in children <12 years (limited data).
pedDoseAr: الأطفال أكبر من 12 سنة: 1–2 مجم لكل كجم — كل 4–6 ساعات — بحد أقصى 400 مجم يوميًا. الأطفال 1–12 سنة: 1–2 مجم لكل كجم — كل 6 ساعات — تحت إشراف الطبيب المختص.
lactationNote: Excreted in breast milk in small amounts. Short-term use (<2–3 days) at low doses considered compatible. Monitor infant for sedation and poor feeding. Neonatal withdrawal possible with prolonged use.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP2D6 (converts to active M1 metabolite 200× more potent than tramadol), CYP3A4; glucuronidation. Genetic variability in CYP2D6: poor metabolizers (reduced efficacy) and ultra-rapid metabolizers (increased toxicity risk).
elimination: Renal — 90% (30% unchanged); half-life 6–8 hours (parent), 7–9 hours (M1 metabolite). Prolonged in renal impairment.
interactions: Serotonergic drugs (SSRIs, SNRIs, MAOIs, triptans) — serotonin syndrome risk — use with caution. CYP2D6 inhibitors (fluoxetine, paroxetine) — reduced conversion to active M1 → reduced analgesia. CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, erythromycin) — increased tramadol levels. Opioid antagonists (naloxone) — antagonize effect. Alcohol — additive CNS depression — avoid. Warfarin — increased INR reported — monitor. MAO inhibitors — avoid within 14 days.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment (eGFR <30): reduce dose to 50–100mg every 12 hours; maximum 200mg/day. Hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh B/C): reduce dose; maximum 50mg every 12 hours. Elderly (>75 years): maximum 300mg/day.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): nausea (up to 40%), vomiting, constipation, dizziness, headache, somnolence, dry mouth. | Serious: seizures (dose-related; risk increases with SSRIs, TCAs, MAOIs), serotonin syndrome (especially with other serotonergic agents), respiratory depression (less than morphine), dependence and withdrawal, hyponatremia (SIADH). | Monitoring: pain scores, sedation level, respiratory rate, signs of serotonin syndrome (agitation, hyperthermia, clonus), bowel function, seizure history.
contra: Acute intoxication with alcohol, opioids, or other CNS depressants. MAO inhibitor use within 14 days. Severe respiratory depression. Epilepsy (uncontrolled) — increased seizure risk. Hypersensitivity.

name: colchicine
moa: Anti-inflammatory — Alkaloid with antimitotic properties — Binds to tubulin → inhibits microtubule polymerization → disrupts cellular functions: inhibits neutrophil chemotaxis, adhesion, and mobilization; blocks inflammasome activation (NALP3) → reduces IL-1β production; inhibits leukotriene B4 and prostaglandin release. Selectively concentrates in leukocytes. No analgesic effect; purely anti-inflammatory for crystal-induced inflammation (gout, pseudogout).
commonUses:
1. Acute gout flare — rapid symptom relief.
2. Gout flare prophylaxis — when initiating urate-lowering therapy (allopurinol, febuxostat).
3. Familial Mediterranean fever (FMF) — prevention of attacks and amyloidosis.
4. Pericarditis — acute and recurrent (second-line).
5. Behçet's disease — mucocutaneous manifestations.
6. Pseudogout (CPPD) — acute flare.
dose: Acute gout: 1.2mg orally at first sign of flare, then 0.6mg 1 hour later. Maximum 1.8mg over 1 hour. Do not repeat within 3 days. Alternative: 0.6mg orally 1–3 times daily until pain relief or GI toxicity. Prophylaxis: 0.6mg orally once or twice daily. Maximum 1.2mg/day. FMF: 1.2–2.4mg orally once daily or divided.
maxDose: Acute gout: 1.8mg over 1 hour (or 6mg over 4 days with traditional regimen — rarely used now). Prophylaxis: 1.2mg/day. Toxicity threshold: >0.5mg/kg.
pedDose: FMF: Children >4 years: 0.5–1.5mg/day orally divided every 12 hours, titrated to response. Acute gout in children: rarely indicated; 0.6mg once, then 0.3mg 1 hour later. Not recommended for children <4 years.
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for children <4 years (except FMF specialist use).
pedDoseAr: حمى البحر الأبيض المتوسط: الأطفال أكبر من 4 سنوات: 0.5–1.5 مجم يوميًا — مقسمة كل 12 ساعة — تحت إشراف الطبيب.
lactationNote: Excreted in breast milk; generally considered compatible at standard doses. Monitor infant for GI symptoms. Avoid prolonged use.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP3A4 (major); undergoes extensive deacetylation; undergoes enterohepatic circulation; active metabolites.
elimination: Fecal — major (unchanged drug and metabolites); renal — 10–20%; half-life 4–27 hours (prolonged in renal impairment: up to 48 hours).
interactions: CYP3A4 inhibitors (clarithromycin, ketoconazole, ritonavir, verapamil) — life-threatening colchicine toxicity (rhabdomyolysis, multiorgan failure) — contraindicated in renal impairment; reduce dose in normal renal function. P-glycoprotein inhibitors (cyclosporine) — increased colchicine toxicity — avoid. Statins — additive rhabdomyolysis risk — caution. Grapefruit juice — increased colchicine levels — avoid. Vitamin B12 — reduced absorption with prolonged colchicine use.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment (eGFR 30–50): reduce dose by 50%; eGFR <30: avoid (toxicity risk). Hepatic impairment: reduce dose; avoid in severe disease. Elderly: use lowest effective dose; monitor for toxicity.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): diarrhea (dose-limiting), nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain (early signs of toxicity). | Serious: rhabdomyolysis, myelosuppression (pancytopenia), peripheral neuropathy, myopathy, hepatotoxicity, alopecia with prolonged use. | Monitoring: CBC, renal function, liver function, CPK levels (for myopathy), GI symptoms.
contra: Severe renal impairment (eGFR <30). Severe hepatic impairment. Concomitant CYP3A4/P-gp inhibitor use in renal impairment. Hypersensitivity. Pregnancy (relative — use only if clearly needed).

name: baclofen
moa: Muscle relaxant/Antispasmodic — GABA-B receptor agonist (centrally acting) — Activates presynaptic and postsynaptic GABA-B receptors in spinal cord → inhibits release of excitatory neurotransmitters (glutamate, aspartate) from primary afferent terminals → reduces monosynaptic and polysynaptic reflex transmission → decreases muscle spasticity. Structural analog of GABA.
commonUses:
1. Spasticity — multiple sclerosis, spinal cord injury, cerebral palsy.
2. Muscle spasms — chronic conditions, stroke.
3. Trigeminal neuralgia — second-line.
4. Hiccups — intractable, off-label.
5. Alcohol withdrawal syndrome — off-label for muscle rigidity.
6. Dystonia — adjunctive treatment.
dose: Initial: 5mg orally 3 times daily. Titrate by 5mg per dose every 3 days. Usual maintenance: 10–20mg orally 3–4 times daily (30–80mg/day). Maximum 80mg/day. Take with food or milk. Avoid abrupt discontinuation (withdrawal: hallucinations, seizures, rebound spasticity).
pedDose: Children 2–7 years: 10–15mg/day orally divided every 8 hours, titrate slowly (5mg every 3 days). Maximum 40mg/day. Children >8 years: same as adult dosing. Not for children <2 years.
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for children <2 years.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال 2–7 سنوات: 10–15 مجم يوميًا — مقسمة كل 8 ساعات — تزيد تدريجيًا 5 مجم كل 3 أيام — بحد أقصى 40 مجم يوميًا.
lactationNote: Excreted in breast milk in small amounts. Generally considered compatible at low doses. Monitor infant for sedation.
metabolism: Hepatic — minimal (15%); largely excreted unchanged.
elimination: Renal — 70–80% unchanged; half-life 3–8 hours (prolonged in renal impairment).
interactions: CNS depressants (alcohol, benzodiazepines, opioids) — additive sedation — caution. Antihypertensives — additive hypotensive effect — monitor BP. Tricyclic antidepressants — enhanced muscle relaxant effect. Lithium — may worsen spasticity. Levodopa — may exacerbate Parkinson's symptoms.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment (eGFR 30–60): reduce dose 50%; eGFR <30: avoid or reduce dose significantly (50% increase in half-life). Hepatic impairment: caution. Elderly: start at 5mg twice daily; increased risk of sedation and confusion.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): drowsiness, dizziness, weakness, fatigue, nausea, confusion (especially elderly). | Serious: seizures with abrupt withdrawal, hallucinations, respiratory depression (rare), hypotension. | Monitoring: muscle tone, sedation level, renal function, signs of withdrawal with tapering.
contra: Hypersensitivity. Abrupt discontinuation (must taper over 1–2 weeks).

name: cyclobenzaprine
moa: Muscle relaxant — Centrally acting — Structurally related to tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline). Mechanism not fully defined; acts primarily at brainstem level → reduces tonic somatic motor activity → decreases muscle spasm. Anticholinergic, antihistaminic (H1), and alpha-2 adrenergic antagonist properties.
commonUses:
1. Acute muscle spasms — low back pain, neck pain (short-term, 2–3 weeks).
2. Fibromyalgia — off-label for sleep and pain improvement.
3. Tension headache — muscle relaxant adjunct.
4. Myofascial pain syndrome — short-term.
5. TMJ disorder — bruxism-related muscle pain.
dose: 5–10mg orally 3 times daily. Extended-release: 15–30mg orally once daily. Maximum 60mg/day. Use for 2–3 weeks only (lack of evidence for longer use). Take with or without food. Take last dose before evening if once-daily extended-release.
pedDose: Children >15 years: same as adult dose (5–10mg 3 times daily). Children <15 years: safety and efficacy not established. Not routinely recommended for pediatric use.
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for children <15 years.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال أكبر من 15 سنة: 5–10 مجم — ثلاث مرات يوميًا — لمدة 2–3 أسابيع — تحت إشراف الطبيب.
lactationNote: Excreted in breast milk (structurally similar to TCAs). Use only if benefit outweighs risk. Monitor infant for sedation.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP1A2, CYP3A4, CYP2D6; extensive first-pass metabolism; active metabolites.
elimination: Renal — primarily as glucuronide conjugates; half-life 18 hours (range 8–37 hours; prolonged in elderly: up to 46 hours).
interactions: MAO inhibitors — possible hypertensive crisis or serotonin syndrome — contraindicated within 14 days. Serotonergic drugs (SSRIs, SNRIs, triptans) — serotonin syndrome risk — caution. CNS depressants — additive sedation. Anticholinergics — additive anticholinergic effects. CYP1A2 inhibitors (ciprofloxacin) — increased cyclobenzaprine levels.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: caution; no established adjustment. Hepatic impairment (mild to moderate): reduce dose; avoid in severe impairment. Elderly: start at 5mg twice daily; increased sedation and anticholinergic effects.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): drowsiness (40%), dry mouth, dizziness, fatigue, headache. | Serious: serotonin syndrome (with serotonergic drugs), arrhythmia (underlying cardiac disease), confusion, urinary retention, angle-closure glaucoma. | Monitoring: symptom improvement at 2–3 weeks; discontinue if no response.
contra: Hyperthyroidism. Congestive heart failure. Arrhythmias or conduction abnormalities. Recent MAO inhibitor use (within 14 days). Acute recovery phase of myocardial infarction. Hypersensitivity.

name: indomethacin
moa: Analgesic/Anti-inflammatory — NSAID (indole acetic acid derivative) — Potent non-selective COX-1 and COX-2 inhibitor → blocks prostaglandin synthesis from arachidonic acid → reduces inflammation, pain, fever. Also inhibits phospholipase A2 and C; reduces neutrophil migration. More potent anti-inflammatory than aspirin but higher GI and CNS side effect profile. Reversibly inhibits platelet aggregation.
commonUses:
1. Acute gout — rapid relief (traditional first-line).
2. Ankylosing spondylitis — highly effective.
3. Osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis — severe cases.
4. Patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) — closure in premature infants (IV).
5. Pericarditis — acute, reduces inflammation.
6. Hemicrania continua — indomethacin-responsive headache.
7. Bartter syndrome — reduces prostaglandin-mediated hyperreninemia.
dose: Oral: 25–50mg 2–3 times daily. Maximum 200mg/day. Acute gout: 50mg 3 times daily until pain relief (3–5 days). Extended-release: 75mg once or twice daily. Take with food or antacids. Use suppositories if oral not tolerated.
maxDose: 200mg/day orally. Rectal: 200mg/day. Duration: acute gout 3–5 days; other conditions — use lowest effective dose.
pedDose: PDA closure (IV): 0.2mg/kg IV initially, then 0.1–0.2mg/kg every 12–24 hours for 3 doses depending on response. Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (>2 years): 1–2mg/kg/day orally divided every 6–12 hours. Maximum 4mg/kg/day or 200mg/day. Not for children <2 years for non-PDA indications.
pedAgeLimit: PDA: premature neonates >500g, >28 weeks GA. Oral: not for children <2 years.
pedDoseAr: القناة الشريانية السالكة: 0.2 مجم لكل كجم — في الوريد — ثم 0.1–0.2 مجم/كجم — كل 12–24 ساعة — لثلاث جرعات. التهاب المفاصل: 1–2 مجم/كجم يوميًا — مقسمة كل 6–12 ساعة — بحد أقصى 4 مجم/كجم.
lactationNote: Excreted in breast milk. Generally considered compatible at low doses, short-term. Monitor infant for GI effects. Avoid prolonged use.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP2C9; undergoes O-demethylation and N-deacylation; enterohepatic circulation.
elimination: Renal — 60% (as metabolites, 10–20% unchanged); fecal — 30%; half-life 4.5 hours (prolonged in hepatic/renal impairment).
interactions: Aspirin — additive GI toxicity, reduced indomethacin levels — avoid. Warfarin — increased bleeding risk — contraindicated. ACE inhibitors/ARBs — reduced antihypertensive effect, nephrotoxicity. Methotrexate — increased toxicity. Lithium — increased lithium levels — monitor. Triamterene — acute kidney injury. Diflunisal — increased indomethacin levels — avoid. Probenecid — increased indomethacin levels.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment (eGFR 30–60): reduce dose 50%; eGFR <30: avoid. Hepatic impairment: reduce dose; avoid in severe disease. Elderly: start at 25mg twice daily; highest risk of GI and CNS toxicity.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): GI pain, nausea, dyspepsia, headache (10–25%), dizziness. | Serious: GI bleeding/perforation (highest risk among NSAIDs), acute kidney injury, hepatotoxicity, aplastic anemia, thrombocytopenia, premature closure of ductus arteriosus (third trimester), severe headaches (indomethacin-specific). | Monitoring: renal function, liver function, CBC, blood pressure, GI symptoms, CNS effects (headache, confusion — especially elderly).
contra: Active or history of peptic ulcer. Severe renal impairment. CABG perioperative. Aspirin/NSAID hypersensitivity (including aspirin-induced asthma). Third trimester pregnancy. Thrombocytopenia. Neonates with necrotizing enterocolitis or active bleeding (for PDA).

name: methocarbamol
moa: Muscle relaxant — Centrally acting — Mechanism not fully defined. Depresses polysynaptic reflexes in spinal cord and subcortical areas of brain → reduces skeletal muscle spasm. Does not directly relax skeletal muscle. Weak carbamate derivative (structurally related to meprobamate). No anti-anxiety effect.
commonUses:
1. Acute musculoskeletal pain — short-term adjunct with rest and physical therapy.
2. Muscle spasms — low back pain, cervical strain.
3. Tetanus — adjunctive for muscle relaxation (IV form).
4. Orthopedic procedures — muscle relaxation.
5. Myofascial pain — short-term symptomatic relief.
dose: Oral: 1500mg 3–4 times daily initially, then 1000mg 3–4 times daily for maintenance. IV/IM: 1g every 8 hours; maximum 3g/day for tetanus (IV infusion rate ≤300mg/min). Take oral form with food to reduce GI upset.
maxDose: Oral: 6–8g/day for 48–72 hours (acute phase), then reduce to 4g/day. IV: 3g/day.
pedDose: Children >12 years: same as adult dose. Children <12 years: safety and efficacy not established. Tetanus in children: 15mg/kg IV every 6 hours (specialist only). Not recommended for routine pediatric use.
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for children <12 years for routine indications. Tetanus: no age restriction but specialist use only.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال أكبر من 12 سنة: 1500 مجم — ثلاث إلى أربع مرات يوميًا — ثم 1000 مجم للاستمرار — مع الأكل.
lactationNote: Excreted in breast milk in small amounts. Generally considered compatible at standard doses. Monitor infant for sedation.
metabolism: Hepatic — via dealkylation and hydroxylation; undergoes conjugation.
elimination: Renal — 40–50% as metabolites; half-life 1–2 hours (prolonged in hepatic impairment).
interactions: CNS depressants (alcohol, benzodiazepines, opioids) — additive sedation — caution. Anticholinergics — additive anticholinergic effects. Pyridostigmine — may antagonize effect in myasthenia gravis. Urine drug screens — may cause false-positive for vanillylmandelic acid (VMA) and 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA).
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: caution; data limited (primarily renally excreted). Hepatic impairment: reduce dose; avoid in severe disease. Elderly: start at lower dose; monitor for sedation and confusion.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): drowsiness, dizziness, headache, nausea, metallic taste. | Serious: anaphylaxis, seizures (rare), syncope with rapid IV infusion (rate-dependent), leukopenia (rare). | Monitoring: symptom improvement, sedation level, infusion site (IV — thrombophlebitis risk).
contra: Hypersensitivity. Coma or pre-coma states. Severe renal impairment (IV formulation contains polyethylene glycol — nephrotoxic). Myasthenia gravis (may worsen weakness).

name: nalbuphine
moa: Analgesic — Opioid agonist-antagonist — Mu-opioid receptor antagonist and kappa-opioid receptor agonist → kappa activation mediates analgesia, sedation, and miosis; mu antagonism limits respiratory depression at higher doses (ceiling effect). Structurally related to naloxone and oxymorphone. Less abuse potential than pure mu agonists. Does not suppress spontaneous uterine contractions (safe in labour).
commonUses:
1. Moderate to severe pain — acute and chronic.
2. Postoperative pain — parenteral analgesia.
3. Labour pain — opioid during delivery.
4. Myocardial infarction-related pain.
5. Opioid-induced pruritus — reversal of itching without reversing analgesia.
6. Procedural sedation — with benzodiazepines.
7. Sickle cell crisis pain.
dose: IV/IM/SC: 10mg every 3–6 hours as needed. Maximum 160mg/day (single dose 20mg). For myocardial infarction: 10mg IV, repeat after 30 minutes, then every 3–6 hours. Titrate to effect. There is a ceiling effect for respiratory depression at 30mg.
pedDose: Children >1 year: 0.1–0.2mg/kg IV/IM every 3–6 hours. Maximum 20mg/dose. Children <1 year: safety and efficacy not established.
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for children <1 year.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال أكبر من سنة: 0.1–0.2 مجم لكل كجم — في الوريد أو العضل — كل 3–6 ساعات — بحد أقصى 20 مجم للجرعة.
lactationNote: Excreted in breast milk in small amounts. Generally considered compatible at standard doses for short-term use. Monitor infant for sedation.
metabolism: Hepatic — extensive first-pass metabolism; glucuronidation.
elimination: Renal — primarily as metabolites; half-life 5 hours (prolonged in hepatic and renal impairment).
interactions: Pure mu-opioid agonists (morphine, fentanyl) — may precipitate withdrawal in opioid-dependent patients — avoid. CNS depressants — additive sedation. MAO inhibitors — possible hypertensive crisis — avoid within 14 days. Opioid antagonists (naloxone) — reversal of effect. Buprenorphine — reduced nalbuphine effect.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment (eGFR <30): reduce dose and increase interval. Hepatic impairment: reduce dose. Elderly: start with lower dose; increased sensitivity.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): sedation, nausea, vomiting, dizziness, dry mouth. | Serious: respiratory depression (ceiling effect at 30mg — less than morphine), withdrawal in opioid-dependent patients, anaphylaxis (rare). | Monitoring: respiratory rate, sedation level, pain scores, signs of withdrawal in dependent patients.
contra: Opioid-dependent patients (precipitates acute withdrawal). Severe respiratory depression. MAO inhibitor use within 14 days. Hypersensitivity.

name: tamsulosin / urologicals
moa: Alpha-1 adrenergic antagonist — Selective alpha-1A and alpha-1D receptor blocker — Blockade of alpha-1A receptors in prostatic smooth muscle → relaxation of bladder neck and prostatic urethra → improves urine flow and reduces LUTS. Selectivity for alpha-1A over alpha-1B (vascular) reduces systemic hypotension compared to non-selective alpha blockers (terazosin, doxazosin). Also relaxes distal ureter → facilitates stone passage.
commonUses:
1. Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) — symptomatic treatment.
2. Ureteral calculi expulsion — medical expulsive therapy.
3. Chronic prostatitis/chronic pelvic pain syndrome — off-label.
4. Lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS) — in men with BPH.
5. Urinary retention — postoperative or post-partum (off-label).
6. Bladder outlet obstruction — symptomatic relief.
dose: Tamsulosin: 0.4mg orally once daily, 30 minutes after the same meal each day. May increase to 0.8mg once daily if inadequate response after 2–4 weeks. For ureteral stones: 0.4mg once daily for up to 4 weeks. Capsule must be swallowed whole; do not crush or chew.
pedDose: Not indicated for pediatric use. Safety and efficacy not established for children <18 years.
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for children <18 years.
pedDoseAr: غير مخصص للاستخدام في الأطفال أقل من 18 سنة.
lactationNote: Not indicated for use in women. No lactation data.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP3A4 (major), CYP2D6 (minor); extensive metabolism.
elimination: Renal — 76% (10% unchanged); fecal — 21%; half-life 9–15 hours.
interactions: Other alpha blockers — additive hypotension — avoid combination. PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil) — additive hypotensive effect — separate by 4 hours; initiate PDE5 at lowest dose. CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, ritonavir) — increased tamsulosin levels — avoid. Warfarin — increased bleeding risk reported (limited data). Antihypertensives — additive hypotension — monitor BP.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment needed (mild to moderate); caution in severe impairment (eGFR <10). Hepatic impairment: avoid in severe impairment (Child-Pugh C). Elderly: no specific adjustment.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): dizziness, orthostatic hypotension, ejaculatory dysfunction (retrograde ejaculation — up to 10%), headache, rhinitis. | Serious: intraoperative floppy iris syndrome (IFIS — during cataract surgery, inform ophthalmologist), priapism (rare), syncope with first dose. | Monitoring: symptom improvement (IPSS score), blood pressure, surgical history documentation for cataract surgery.
contra: Hypersensitivity. History of orthostatic hypotension. Severe hepatic impairment. Cataract surgery (relative — must inform surgeon).

name: lidocaine
moa: Anesthetic/Antiarrhythmic — Class IB antiarrhythmic/Amide local anesthetic — Blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal and cardiac cell membranes → inhibits sodium influx → prevents depolarization → blocks nerve impulse conduction.
