In cardiac tissue: shortens action potential duration, effective refractory period. Rapid onset (45–90 seconds IV, 2–5 minutes topical), duration 30–90 minutes.
commonUses:
1. Local anesthesia — infiltration, nerve block, epidural, spinal.
2. Surface anesthesia — skin, mucous membranes (pre-procedural).
3. Topical analgesia — minor burns, insect bites, pruritus.
4. Postherpetic neuralgia — lidocaine patch.
5. Cardiac arrhythmias — ventricular tachycardia/fibrillation (IV).
6. Endotracheal intubation — suppress cough reflex (IV).
7. Premature ejaculation — topical spray/gel.
8. Painful procedures — venipuncture, lumbar puncture.
9. Hemorrhoids and anal fissures — topical gel.
dose: Topical: Apply thin layer to affected area 1–3 times daily (gel, cream, ointment). Spray: 1–3 sprays to target area. Maximum 3 doses/day. Local infiltration: 1–2% solution; maximum 4.5mg/kg/dose (not to exceed 300mg). IV for arrhythmia: 1–1.5mg/kg bolus, then 1–4mg/min infusion. Do not apply to broken skin or mucous membranes unless formulated for that use.
maxDose: Topical: no established maximum (limited systemic absorption). Injectable: 4.5mg/kg (without epinephrine) or 7mg/kg (with epinephrine). Maximum total dose: 300mg. IV infusion: 4mg/min.
pedDose: Topical: apply sparingly 1–2 times daily. Injectable: 0.5–1% solution; maximum 4.5mg/kg/dose. Children <1 year: maximum 2–3mg/kg/dose. IV for arrhythmia (rare): 1mg/kg bolus then 20–50mcg/kg/min infusion.
pedAgeLimit: Topical: safe from birth. Injectable and IV: dose adjustment. Do not use in neonates with bradycardia or heart block.
pedDoseAr: الدهان الموضعي: طبقة رقيقة — مرة إلى مرتين يوميًا — بكمية قليلة للأطفال. الحقن: الجرعة تُحسب بالوزن — بحد أقصى 4.5 مجم لكل كجم — بواسطة الطبيب فقط.
lactationNote: Minimal excretion in breast milk. Topical use fully compatible with breastfeeding.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP3A4, CYP1A2; extensive first-pass metabolism; active metabolites (monoethylglycinexylidide).
elimination: Renal — 90% as metabolites; half-life 1.5–2 hours (prolonged in hepatic impairment and CHF).
interactions: Class I antiarrhythmics (mexiletine, tocainide) — additive cardiac effects — avoid. Beta blockers — reduced hepatic clearance of lidocaine. Cimetidine — increased lidocaine levels. Other local anesthetics — additive systemic toxicity. Succinylcholine — prolonged neuromuscular blockade.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no specific adjustment for topical; caution with IV (accumulation of metabolites). Hepatic impairment: reduce dose 50% for IV; topical use safe. Elderly: reduce dose; increased sensitivity.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): local — burning, stinging, erythema (transient). | Serious: systemic toxicity — perioral numbness, metallic taste, tinnitus, dizziness → seizures, coma, respiratory depression, cardiac arrest (dose-dependent); methemoglobinemia (with excessive topical use, especially benzocaine); anaphylaxis (rare). | Monitoring: signs of systemic toxicity, cardiac monitoring (IV use), application site.
contra: Hypersensitivity to amide local anesthetics. Severe heart block (without pacemaker). Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome. Do not apply to severely traumatized mucosa or large areas of broken skin.

name: hyaluronic acid / ocular lubricants
moa: Ocular lubricant — Viscoelastic polymer — Hyaluronic acid: naturally occurring glycosaminoglycan → binds water (up to 1000× its weight) → forms viscoelastic protective film over corneal epithelium → reduces tear film osmolarity, improves tear film stability, and reduces friction during blinking. Non-preserved formulations avoid benzalkonium chloride toxicity to corneal epithelium.
commonUses:
1. Dry eye syndrome — aqueous-deficient and evaporative types.
2. Keratoconjunctivitis sicca — associated with Sjögren's syndrome.
3. Post-LASIK dry eye — temporary protection.
4. Corneal epithelial protection — after surgery or trauma.
5. Contact lens discomfort — rewetting drops.
6. Computer vision syndrome — ocular surface hydration.
7. Meibomian gland dysfunction — adjunctive lubrication.
8. Ocular surface disease in glaucoma patients — conjunctival protection.
dose: Instill 1–2 drops in affected eye(s) 3–4 times daily or as needed. Can be used more frequently (every 1–2 hours) in severe dry eye. Remove contact lenses before use (unless labeled for contact lens use). Wait 5–10 minutes between different eye drops.
maxDose: No maximum; frequency can be up to every hour for preservative-free formulations.
pedDose: Children: 1 drop in affected eye(s) 3–4 times daily. Safe for all ages. For infants: single-use preservative-free vials preferred.
pedAgeLimit: Safe from birth (preservative-free formulations preferred for neonates).
pedDoseAr: نقطة إلى نقطتين — في العين المصابة — 3–4 مرات يوميًا — أو عند الحاجة — يُفضل النوع الخالي من المواد الحافظة للرضع.
lactationNote: No systemic absorption from ocular use. Fully compatible with breastfeeding.
metabolism: Not metabolized — acts locally on ocular surface; minimal to no systemic absorption.
elimination: Local — cleared via nasolacrimal duct and tear turnover.
interactions: Other eye drops — may dilute and wash out hyaluronic acid — separate by 5–10 minutes. Contact lens solutions — some formulations may interact — remove lenses before application.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment (no systemic absorption). Hepatic impairment: no adjustment. Elderly: no specific adjustment; safe for frequent use.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): transient blurred vision (immediately after instillation), mild stinging. | Serious: hypersensitivity (rare), eye infection if bottle tip contaminated. | Monitoring: symptom relief, corneal staining, tear break-up time.
contra: Hypersensitivity to any component. Do not use if solution becomes cloudy or discolored. Do not share bottles to prevent infection.

name: benzydamine
moa: Analgesic/Anti-inflammatory — NSAID (non-selective) — Inhibits COX-1 and COX-2 → reduces prostaglandin synthesis → analgesic and anti-inflammatory effect on mucous membranes. Also membrane stabilizer: inhibits histamine release from mast cells. Weak antimicrobial and antifungal properties. Locally acting; poorly absorbed systemically.
commonUses:
1. Oral mucositis — radiation or chemotherapy-induced.
2. Pharyngitis and tonsillitis — symptomatic relief.
3. Aphthous ulcers — pain relief.
4. Post-dental procedures — socket pain.
5. Sore throat — acute and chronic.
6. Oral candidiasis — adjunctive symptomatic relief.
7. Musculoskeletal pain — topical gel/cream.
8. Post-episiotomy pain — topical.
dose: Oral rinse: 15mL (0.15% solution) gargle or rinse every 1.5–3 hours as needed. Do not swallow. Spray: 2–4 sprays to affected area every 1.5–3 hours. Lozenges: 1 lozenge (3mg) 3–4 times daily. Topical gel: apply thin layer to affected area 2–3 times daily. Maximum 12 doses/day.
pedDose: Children >6 years: mouthwash 15mL (0.15%) every 3 hours as needed. Spray: 1–2 sprays every 3 hours. Lozenges: 1 lozenge 3 times daily. Children 2–6 years: mouthwash 5–10mL every 3 hours. Not for children <2 years.
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for children <2 years. Do not give lozenges to children <6 years (choking risk).
pedDoseAr: الأطفال أكبر من 6 سنوات: 15 مل غرغرة — كل 3 ساعات — أو 1–2 بخة — كل 3 ساعات. الأطفال 2–6 سنوات: 5–10 مل غرغرة — كل 3 ساعات.
lactationNote: No data on excretion in breast milk. Topical oral use likely low risk due to minimal systemic absorption.
metabolism: Hepatic — minimal metabolism; absorbed fraction undergoes oxidation and conjugation.
elimination: Renal — 50% as metabolites; fecal — minor; half-life 13 hours.
interactions: No significant drug interactions reported. Other oral products — may reduce local concentration — separate by 30 minutes.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment needed (minimal systemic absorption). Hepatic impairment: no specific data; likely safe. Elderly: no adjustment.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): oral numbness/stinging (transient), dry mouth. | Serious: hypersensitivity (rare), bronchospasm in aspirin-sensitive patients (theoretical cross-reactivity), photosensitivity with topical gel. | Monitoring: symptom relief, mucosal healing.
contra: Hypersensitivity to benzydamine or other NSAIDs. Aspirin-sensitive asthma (theoretical cross-reactivity). Do not swallow mouthwash or spray in large quantities.

name: choline salicylate / otologicals
moa: Analgesic/Anti-inflammatory — Salicylate ester (NSAID) — Choline salicylate is hydrolyzed to salicylic acid in ear canal → inhibits COX-1 and COX-2 locally → reduces prostaglandin synthesis → analgesic and anti-inflammatory effect on external auditory canal and tympanic membrane. Glycerin base provides osmotic dehydration of edematous canal skin. Locally acting; minimal systemic absorption.
commonUses:
1. Otitis externa — mild to moderate, analgesic component.
2. Ear pain (otalgia) — temporary symptomatic relief.
3. Tympanitis (myringitis) — pain relief.
4. Acute otitis media — adjunctive analgesic in intact tympanic membrane.
5. Ear wax removal — pre-procedure softening and analgesia.
6. External ear canal inflammation — traumatic or irritant.
dose: Instill 2–3 drops into the affected ear canal every 3–4 hours as needed for pain. Lie with affected ear upward for 5 minutes after instillation. Do not use if tympanic membrane is perforated (otorrhea, suspected rupture). Maximum 6 doses/day. Duration: 48–72 hours without reassessment.
maxDose: 6 doses/day. Maximum duration: 3 days without medical review.
pedDose: Children >2 years: 2–3 drops in affected ear every 3–4 hours. Children <2 years: 1–2 drops every 4–6 hours. Do not use if tympanic membrane perforation suspected or if ear tubes in place.
pedAgeLimit: Safe from 6 months for short-term use. Not recommended for neonates <6 months.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال أكبر من سنتين: 2–3 نقاط — في الأذن المصابة — كل 3–4 ساعات. الأطفال أقل من سنتين: 1–2 نقطة — كل 4–6 ساعات — لا تستخدم إذا كان هناك ثقب في طبلة الأذن.
lactationNote: Topical otic use with minimal systemic absorption. Considered safe during breastfeeding.
metabolism: Minimal local metabolism — choline salicylate hydrolyzed to salicylic acid in ear canal.
elimination: Local — minimal systemic absorption unless tympanic membrane ruptured.
interactions: No significant systemic drug interactions expected with otic use. Other otic preparations — separation by 30 minutes. Warfarin — theoretical salicylate absorption if TM perforated — unlikely significant.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment (minimal systemic absorption). Hepatic impairment: no adjustment. Elderly: no specific issues.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): local stinging/burning (mild, transient), ear fullness. | Serious: ototoxicity if tympanic membrane is perforated (salicylate contact with middle/inner ear), hypersensitivity reactions. | Monitoring: ear pain severity, signs of tympanic membrane perforation (discharge, worsening pain, hearing loss).
contra: Perforated tympanic membrane (known or suspected). Otorrhea (ear discharge). Hypersensitivity to salicylates or NSAIDs. Chickenpox or influenza (Reye's syndrome association with systemic salicylates — caution). Tympanostomy tubes in place.

name: dantrolene
moa: Muscle relaxant — Hydantoin derivative — Directly acts on skeletal muscle: inhibits ryanodine receptors (RyR1) on sarcoplasmic reticulum → reduces calcium release from SR → uncouples excitation-contraction → decreases muscle contraction force. Unlike other muscle relaxants, acts peripherally at muscle level, not CNS. IV for malignant hyperthermia: blocks excessive calcium release triggered by halogenated anesthetics/succinylcholine.
commonUses:
1. Chronic spasticity — spinal cord injury, multiple sclerosis, cerebral palsy, stroke.
2. Malignant hyperthermia — acute crisis treatment and prophylaxis (IV).
3. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome — adjunctive treatment (IV).
4. Tetanus — muscle relaxation adjunct.
5. Ecstasy (MDMA) overdose — severe hyperthermia treatment (off-label).
dose: Spasticity (oral): 25mg once daily initially, increase by 25mg every 4–7 days. Usual maintenance: 25–100mg 3–4 times daily. Maximum 400mg/day. Malignant hyperthermia (IV): 2.5mg/kg rapid IV push, repeat every 5–10 minutes until symptoms resolve (up to 10mg/kg total). Take oral form with food or milk.
maxDose: 400mg/day oral. IV: 10mg/kg total dose (malignant hyperthermia crisis).
pedDose: Spasticity (>5 years): 0.5mg/kg orally once daily initially, titrate by 0.5mg/kg every 4–7 days. Maximum 3mg/kg/dose, up to 400mg/day. Malignant hyperthermia: same as adult IV dosing (2.5mg/kg). Not recommended for children <5 years for chronic spasticity.
pedAgeLimit: Oral spasticity: safety not established <5 years. IV malignant hyperthermia: no age restriction (life-saving).
pedDoseAr: الأطفال أكبر من 5 سنوات: 0.5 مجم لكل كجم — مرة يوميًا — تزيد تدريجيًا — الجرعة المعتادة: 0.5–3 مجم/كجم — ثلاث إلى أربع مرات يوميًا.
lactationNote: Excreted in breast milk. Contraindicated per manufacturer. Consider alternative or discontinue breastfeeding.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP3A4; extensive first-pass metabolism; active metabolite (5-hydroxydantrolene).
elimination: Renal — 70% as metabolites; fecal — 20%; half-life 8–12 hours (oral), 4–12 hours (IV).
interactions: CNS depressants — additive sedation. Verapamil, diltiazem — hyperkalemia and myocardial depression (IV dantrolene) — avoid. Anticholinergics — additive anticholinergic effects. Estrogens — increased hepatotoxicity risk (especially women >35 years). Alcohol — additive CNS depression. Statins — additive myopathy/hepatotoxicity risk.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: caution; limited data. Hepatic impairment: contraindicated in active liver disease. Elderly: start at 25mg/day; increased hepatotoxicity and sedation risk.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): drowsiness, dizziness, weakness, fatigue, nausea, diarrhea. | Serious: hepatotoxicity (elevated transaminases; fatal hepatitis reported — monitor LFTs monthly), pleural effusion with pericarditis, seizures, severe muscle weakness. IV: phlebitis at injection site, respiratory depression. | Monitoring: liver function tests at baseline and monthly for 3 months, then periodically; muscle strength; CNS effects.
contra: Active hepatic disease (including hepatitis, cirrhosis). Women >35 years (increased hepatotoxicity risk — relative). Lactation. Hypersensitivity.

name: sildenafil
moa: Erectile enhancer/Vasodilator — PDE5 inhibitor — Selectively inhibits phosphodiesterase type 5 (PDE5) in corpus cavernosum and pulmonary vasculature → prevents degradation of cGMP → enhances nitric oxide-mediated vasodilation → increases cavernosal blood flow during sexual stimulation → facilitates erection. In PAH: reduces pulmonary vascular resistance. Does not cause erection without sexual stimulation. No direct smooth muscle relaxant effect on corpus cavernosum.
commonUses:
1. Erectile dysfunction — organic, psychogenic, or mixed.
2. Pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) — WHO Group 1, improves exercise capacity.
3. Raynaud's phenomenon — off-label, secondary to systemic sclerosis.
4. Altitude sickness — high-altitude pulmonary edema prevention (off-label).
5. Female sexual arousal disorder — off-label, limited evidence.
6. Digital ulcers in systemic sclerosis — off-label.
dose: Erectile dysfunction: 25–100mg orally as single dose 30–60 minutes before sexual activity. Maximum 1 dose/day. Start at 50mg; adjust based on response and tolerability. Take on empty stomach (food delays absorption). PAH: 20mg orally 3 times daily. IV: 10mg (12.5mL) IV bolus 3 times daily (PAH).
maxDose: Erectile dysfunction: 100mg/day (single dose). PAH: 60mg/day (20mg 3 times daily).
pedDose: PAH in children (>1 year): 0.5–2mg/kg/dose orally 3–4 times daily. Maximum 20mg/dose. Erectile dysfunction: not indicated for children. Not recommended for pediatric PAH <1 year.
pedAgeLimit: Not indicated for erectile dysfunction in pediatrics. PAH: >1 year (specialist use).
pedDoseAr: ارتفاع ضغط الشريان الرئوي للأطفال أكبر من سنة: 0.5–2 مجم لكل كجم — ثلاث إلى أربع مرات يوميًا — بحد أقصى 20 مجم للجرعة.
lactationNote: Excreted in breast milk in small amounts. Used in nursing mothers for PAH when benefit outweighs risk.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP3A4 (major), CYP2C9 (minor); active metabolite (50% potency of sildenafil).
elimination: Fecal — 80% as metabolites; renal — 13%; half-life 4 hours (parent and active metabolite).
interactions: Nitrates (all forms) — profound hypotension → fatal — absolutely contraindicated. Riociguat — additive hypotension — contraindicated. Alpha blockers — additive hypotension — separate by 4 hours. CYP3A4 inhibitors (ritonavir, ketoconazole, clarithromycin) — significantly increased sildenafil levels — reduce sildenafil dose to 25mg. CYP3A4 inducers (rifampicin) — reduced sildenafil efficacy. Alcohol — additive hypotension.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment (eGFR <30): reduce starting dose to 25mg. Hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh A/B): reduce starting dose to 25mg; avoid in Child-Pugh C. Elderly (>65 years): start at 25mg; increased exposure.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): headache (16%), flushing (10%), dyspepsia (7%), nasal congestion (4%), dizziness, visual disturbances (blue-tinted vision, increased light sensitivity — 3%). | Serious: priapism (rare — requires emergency treatment to prevent permanent impotence), non-arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (NAION — sudden vision loss), sudden hearing loss, hypotension, myocardial infarction (rare, in high-risk patients). | Monitoring: blood pressure, visual symptoms, erection duration >4 hours (priapism).
contra: Concomitant nitrates (any form — GTN, isosorbide, amyl nitrite). Riociguat. Severe hepatic impairment. Recent myocardial infarction or stroke (within 6 months). Hypotension (BP <90/50). Anatomical penile deformity or predisposition to priapism (sickle cell, leukemia). NAION in one eye.

name: tadalafil
moa: Erectile enhancer/Vasodilator — PDE5 inhibitor — Selectively inhibits phosphodiesterase type 5 in corpus cavernosum, prostate, and pulmonary vasculature → prevents cGMP degradation → enhances NO-mediated vasodilation → increases erectile response and reduces BPH smooth muscle tone. Longer half-life (17.5 hours) than sildenafil allows daily dosing and more spontaneity. PAH: reduces pulmonary vascular resistance.
commonUses:
1. Erectile dysfunction — on-demand or daily dosing.
2. Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) — alone or with tamsulosin.
3. Erectile dysfunction + BPH — concurrent treatment.
4. Pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) — WHO Group 1.
5. Female sexual arousal disorder — off-label, limited evidence.
6. Raynaud's phenomenon — off-label, secondary to systemic sclerosis.
dose: Erectile dysfunction (on-demand): 10–20mg as single dose at least 30 minutes before sexual activity. Maximum 1 dose/day. Daily dosing: 2.5–5mg orally once daily. BPH: 5mg orally once daily. PAH: 40mg orally once daily. Take with or without food.
pedDose: Not indicated for pediatric use. Safety and efficacy not established for children <18 years. PAH in children: investigational only (specialist use).
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for children <18 years.
pedDoseAr: غير مخصص للاستخدام في الأطفال أقل من 18 سنة.
lactationNote: Excreted in breast milk. Used in nursing mothers for PAH when benefit outweighs risk.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP3A4 (major); extensive metabolism.
elimination: Fecal — 61% as metabolites; renal — 36%; half-life 17.5 hours (prolonged in hepatic and renal impairment).
interactions: Nitrates — profound hypotension → fatal — absolutely contraindicated. Riociguat — additive hypotension — contraindicated. Alpha blockers — additive hypotension — separate administration. CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, ritonavir) — increased tadalafil levels — reduce dose: on-demand max 10mg/72 hours, daily max 2.5mg. CYP3A4 inducers (rifampicin) — reduced tadalafil efficacy. Alcohol — additive hypotension.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment (eGFR 30–50): on-demand max 10mg/48 hours or daily 2.5mg; eGFR <30: on-demand max 5mg/72 hours, daily dosing not recommended. Hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh A/B): max 10mg; avoid in Child-Pugh C. Elderly: no adjustment.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): headache (11%), dyspepsia (8%), back pain (6%), myalgia (5%), nasal congestion (3%), flushing (3%). | Serious: priapism, NAION (sudden vision loss), sudden hearing loss, myocardial infarction (rare). | Monitoring: blood pressure, visual symptoms, erection >4 hours.
contra: Concomitant nitrates. Riociguat. Severe hepatic impairment. Recent MI/stroke (within 3 months). Anatomical penile deformity or priapism predisposition.

name: vardenafil
moa: Erectile enhancer — PDE5 inhibitor — Potent and highly selective inhibitor of phosphodiesterase type 5 in corpus cavernosum → prevents degradation of cGMP → enhances nitric oxide-mediated vasodilation → increases cavernosal blood flow during sexual stimulation → facilitates erection. More selective for PDE5 than sildenafil (less PDE6 inhibition → fewer visual side effects). Does not cause erection without sexual stimulation.
commonUses:
1. Erectile dysfunction — organic, psychogenic, or mixed.
2. Diabetic erectile dysfunction — effective in difficult-to-treat population.
3. Post-radical prostatectomy ED — improved erectile function.
4. Antidepressant-induced sexual dysfunction — off-label.
5. Female sexual arousal disorder — off-label, limited evidence.
dose: 10mg orally as single dose 25–60 minutes before sexual activity. May increase to 20mg or decrease to 5mg based on response. Maximum 1 dose/day. Orodispersible tablet: 10mg on tongue, dissolves without water. Take with or without food (high-fat meal delays absorption).
pedDose: Not indicated for pediatric use. Safety and efficacy not established for children <18 years.
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for children <18 years.
pedDoseAr: غير مخصص للاستخدام في الأطفال أقل من 18 سنة.
lactationNote: Not indicated for use in women. No human lactation data.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP3A4 (major), CYP2C9 (minor); active metabolite (M1: 28% potency of parent).
elimination: Fecal — 91–95% as metabolites; renal — 5%; half-life 4–5 hours (parent and M1).
interactions: Nitrates — profound hypotension → fatal — absolutely contraindicated. Riociguat — additive hypotension — contraindicated. Alpha blockers — additive hypotension — separate by 4 hours. CYP3A4 inhibitors (ritonavir, ketoconazole) — significantly increased vardenafil levels — max 5mg/72 hours. CYP3A4 inducers — reduced efficacy. QT-prolonging drugs (amiodarone, sotalol) — additive risk.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment (eGFR <30): no data; avoid or use 5mg with caution. Hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh A/B): reduce starting dose to 5mg; avoid in Child-Pugh C. Elderly (>65 years): start at 5mg.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): headache (10%), flushing (11%), rhinitis (9%), dyspepsia (4%), dizziness (2%). | Serious: priapism (rare), NAION, sudden hearing loss, QT prolongation (dose-dependent), hypotension. | Monitoring: blood pressure, visual symptoms, erection >4 hours, ECG (if on QT-prolonging drugs).
contra: Concomitant nitrates. Riociguat. Severe hepatic impairment. Congenital QT prolongation or concomitant QT-prolonging drugs. Recent MI/stroke.

name: avanafil
moa: Erectile enhancer — PDE5 inhibitor — Highly selective inhibitor of phosphodiesterase type 5 → prevents cGMP degradation → enhances NO-mediated vasodilation in corpus cavernosum → facilitates erection upon sexual stimulation. Fastest onset among PDE5 inhibitors (15–30 minutes vs 30–60 for sildenafil/vardenafil and 30–120 for tadalafil). Most selective for PDE5 (less cross-reactivity with PDE1, PDE6, PDE11).
commonUses:
1. Erectile dysfunction — organic, psychogenic, or mixed.
2. Diabetic erectile dysfunction — rapid onset beneficial.
3. Erectile dysfunction with desire for rapid onset — onset as fast as 15 minutes.
4. Post-prostatectomy ED — off-label.
5. Antidepressant-induced sexual dysfunction — off-label.
dose: 100mg orally as single dose 15–30 minutes before sexual activity. May increase to 200mg or decrease to 50mg. Maximum 1 dose/day. Take with or without food (food delays absorption by 1–1.5 hours).
pedDose: Not indicated for pediatric use. Safety and efficacy not established for children <18 years.
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for children <18 years.
pedDoseAr: غير مخصص للاستخدام في الأطفال أقل من 18 سنة.
lactationNote: Not indicated for use in women. No lactation data.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP3A4 (major), CYP2C9 (minor); active metabolites (M4, M16).
elimination: Fecal — 62%; renal — 21%; half-life 3–5 hours (parent), 5–10 hours (metabolites).
interactions: Nitrates — profound hypotension → fatal — absolutely contraindicated. Riociguat — additive hypotension — contraindicated. Alpha blockers — additive hypotension. CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, ritonavir) — increased avanafil levels — max 50mg/day. CYP3A4 inducers — reduced efficacy. Alcohol — additive hypotension — caution.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment (eGFR <30): no data; avoid or use 50mg. Hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh A/B): no adjustment; avoid in Child-Pugh C. Elderly: no specific adjustment.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): headache (7%), flushing (4%), nasal congestion (3%), back pain (2%). | Serious: priapism (rare), NAION, sudden hearing loss, hypotension. | Monitoring: blood pressure, visual symptoms, erection >4 hours.
contra: Concomitant nitrates. Riociguat. Severe hepatic impairment. Recent MI/stroke. Anatomical penile deformity or priapism predisposition.

name: dapoxetine / sildenafil
moa: Combination — SSRI + PDE5 inhibitor — Dapoxetine: selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor with rapid absorption and short half-life → increases serotonin in synaptic cleft → delays ejaculatory reflex via central and spinal mechanisms. Sildenafil: PDE5 inhibitor → enhances cGMP-mediated vasodilation in corpus cavernosum → facilitates erection. Together: addresses both premature ejaculation (CNS mechanism) and erectile dysfunction (peripheral vascular mechanism).
commonUses:
1. Premature ejaculation + erectile dysfunction — combined treatment.
2. Premature ejaculation — primary or acquired, with associated ED.
3. Erectile dysfunction with concomitant PE — single-pill convenience.
4. Performance anxiety — multifactorial sexual dysfunction.
dose: Dapoxetine 30–60mg + sildenafil 50–100mg as single combination dose 1–3 hours before sexual activity. Maximum 1 dose/day. Take with a full glass of water. Dapoxetine component: on-demand only; not for daily use.
maxDose: Dapoxetine 60mg + sildenafil 100mg as single dose. Maximum 1 dose/day.
pedDose: Not indicated for pediatric use.
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for children <18 years.
pedDoseAr: غير مخصص للاستخدام في الأطفال أقل من 18 سنة.
lactationNote: Not indicated for use in women. No lactation data.
metabolism: Dapoxetine: hepatic — CYP2D6, CYP3A4; extensive first-pass. Sildenafil: hepatic — CYP3A4. Both components metabolized hepatically.
elimination: Dapoxetine: renal — 98% as metabolites; half-life 1.5 hours. Sildenafil: fecal 80%, renal 13%; half-life 4 hours.
interactions: Nitrates — fatal hypotension (sildenafil component) — absolutely contraindicated. MAO inhibitors — serotonin syndrome risk (dapoxetine) — contraindicated. SSRIs/SNRIs — additive serotonin syndrome risk. CYP3A4 inhibitors — increased sildenafil levels. CYP2D6 inhibitors — increased dapoxetine levels. Alcohol — additive CNS depression.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment (eGFR <30): avoid or use lowest doses. Hepatic impairment: avoid in moderate to severe disease. Elderly: safety and efficacy not established >65 years.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): headache, dizziness, nausea, flushing, nasal congestion, dyspepsia. | Serious: serotonin syndrome, orthostatic hypotension, syncope, priapism, NAION. | Monitoring: blood pressure, orthostatic symptoms, mood changes, suicidal ideation (SSRI component).
contra: Concomitant nitrates. MAO inhibitor use. Severe hepatic impairment. Severe renal impairment. Hypotension. History of syncope. Bipolar disorder or mania (SSRI). Priapism predisposition.

name: ginseng
moa: Adaptogen/Erectile enhancer (herbal) — Ginsenosides (triterpenoid saponins) — Multiple mechanisms: enhances nitric oxide production in corpus cavernosum → vasodilation → facilitates erection. Adaptogenic properties: modulates hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis → reduces stress-induced cortisol elevation. Antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, immunomodulatory effects. Panax ginseng (Korean/Asian ginseng) primarily used. Active compounds: ginsenosides Rb1, Rg1, Rg3.
commonUses:
1. Erectile dysfunction — mild to moderate, herbal adjunct.
2. Fatigue and asthenia — adaptogenic, improves energy.
3. Cognitive function — modest improvement in memory and concentration.
4. Immune system enhancement — prevention of colds/flu.
5. Diabetes — adjuvant for glycemic control (limited evidence).
6. Menopausal symptoms — modest reduction in hot flashes.
7. Male infertility — improves sperm parameters (limited evidence).
8. Physical performance — adaptogenic, enhances stamina.
dose: 100–400mg orally once or twice daily. Standardized extract (G115): 100mg twice daily. Duration: 8–12 weeks then 2-week break. Take with meals to reduce GI upset. Intermittent cycling recommended (2–3 months on, 1 month off).
maxDose: 400mg/day. Continuous use not recommended beyond 3 months without interruption.
pedDose: Not recommended for children. Safety and efficacy not established.
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for children <18 years.
pedDoseAr: غير مخصص للاستخدام في الأطفال أقل من 18 سنة.
lactationNote: Limited data. Estrogenic effects reported; avoid use during breastfeeding.
metabolism: Hepatic — extensive metabolism by gut microflora and CYP450 enzymes; ginsenosides undergo deglycosylation.
elimination: Renal — as metabolites; half-life variable (1–24 hours depending on ginsenoside).
interactions: Warfarin — reduced anticoagulant effect — monitor INR. Antidiabetics — additive hypoglycemic effect — monitor blood glucose. MAO inhibitors — possible manic-like symptoms (theoretical) — avoid. Caffeine — additive stimulant effect. CYP3A4 substrates — variable modulation — monitor. Immunosuppressants — immunostimulant effect may counter immunosuppression.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: No specific data; caution advised. Hepatic impairment: Use with caution; avoid in severe disease. Elderly: No specific adjustment; use standard dose.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Insomnia, nervousness, headache, GI upset (nausea, diarrhea). | Serious: Hypoglycemia (in diabetic patients), hypertension (with prolonged high-dose use), estrogenic effects (mastalgia, vaginal bleeding — with prolonged use), allergic reactions. | Monitoring: Blood pressure, blood glucose (diabetes), INR (if on warfarin), sleep quality.
contra: Hypersensitivity to Panax ginseng or ginsenosides. Active bleeding or bleeding disorders. Hormone-sensitive cancers (breast, ovarian, uterine, prostate — estrogenic effect). Pregnancy (relative). Uncontrolled hypertension.

name: vitamin b complex (b1/b6/b12)
moa: Vitamin supplement — Water-soluble B vitamins — B1 (thiamine): cofactor for pyruvate dehydrogenase → essential for carbohydrate metabolism and nerve conduction. B6 (pyridoxine): precursor to pyridoxal phosphate (PLP) → cofactor for neurotransmitter synthesis (serotonin, dopamine, GABA), heme synthesis, homocysteine metabolism. B12 (cobalamin): cofactor for methionine synthase and methylmalonyl-CoA mutase → essential for DNA synthesis, myelin formation, erythropoiesis. Combined effects: neurotropic, hematinic, metabolic support.
commonUses:
1. Vitamin B deficiency — prophylaxis and treatment.
2. Peripheral neuropathy — diabetic, alcoholic, drug-induced.
3. Neuritis and neuralgia — sciatica, trigeminal neuralgia.
4. Anemia — megaloblastic (B12) and microcytic (B6).
5. Chronic fatigue — supportive therapy.
6. Pregnancy — supportive supplementation.
7. Post-gastrectomy and malabsorption syndromes.
8. Homocysteine reduction — cardiovascular risk modification.
9. Alcohol use disorder — thiamine (B1) repletion.
dose: B1 (thiamine): 100mg orally once daily. B6 (pyridoxine): 25–50mg orally once daily. B12 (cyanocobalamin/methylcobalamin): 500–1000mcg orally once daily or 1000mcg IM weekly. Combination products: 1–2 tablets orally once daily. IM injection (B complex): 1 ampoule IM every 3–7 days. Take oral form with meals.
maxDose: B1: 300mg/day. B6: 100mg/day (neuropathy risk >200mg/day prolonged). B12: no toxic upper limit established.
pedDose: Children >6 years: same as adult prophylactic dose. Children 1–6 years: half adult dose. Infants <1 year: B1 0.3–0.5mg/day, B6 0.1–0.3mg/day, B12 0.4–0.5mcg/day. Therapeutic doses higher per pediatrician guidance.
pedAgeLimit: Safe from birth. Injectable forms only as medically indicated.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال أكبر من 6 سنوات: مثل جرعة الكبار. الأطفال 1–6 سنوات: نصف الجرعة. الرضع: الجرعة يحددها الطبيب حسب الوزن والعمر.
lactationNote: Water-soluble vitamins; excreted in breast milk. Supplementation compatible with breastfeeding; may increase milk levels.
metabolism: B1: hepatic phosphorylation to active thiamine pyrophosphate. B6: hepatic phosphorylation to pyridoxal phosphate. B12: bound to transcobalamin-II → delivered to liver and tissues.
elimination: Renal — excess water-soluble vitamins excreted unchanged; B12 half-life 6 days (liver stores); B1 and B6 half-life 2–4 hours.
interactions: Levodopa — B6 enhances peripheral decarboxylation → reduces levodopa efficacy (unless carbidopa co-administered). Metformin — reduces B12 absorption — monitor B12 levels with prolonged use. Proton pump inhibitors/H2 blockers — reduced B12 absorption. Isoniazid — B6 antagonist → increased B6 requirement. Oral contraceptives — reduced B6 and B12 levels. Alcohol — reduced B1 absorption and increased excretion.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no dose adjustment (water-soluble, renally excreted). Hepatic impairment: activation of B1 and B6 may be reduced; use with caution. Elderly: reduced B12 absorption common; consider higher doses or IM route.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): nausea (with high-dose oral), mild diarrhea, urine discoloration (yellow — benign from B2/riboflavin). | Serious: B6 — peripheral neuropathy with prolonged high dose (>200mg/day, reversible on discontinuation); anaphylaxis with IV/IM B1 (rare); hypersensitivity to B12 injections (rare). | Monitoring: clinical response (neuropathy, anemia); B12 levels if prolonged therapy; neurological symptoms with high-dose B6.
contra: Hypersensitivity to any component. Leber's hereditary optic atrophy (B12 — risk of accelerated optic atrophy). B6 allergy.

name: vitamin d (cholecalciferol)
moa: Vitamin supplement/Calcium regulator — Fat-soluble vitamin (secosteroid) — Cholecalciferol (D3) hydroxylated in liver → 25-hydroxyvitamin D (calcidiol), then in kidney → 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D (calcitriol, active form). Calcitriol binds vitamin D receptor (VDR) → increases intestinal calcium and phosphorus absorption, renal calcium reabsorption, osteoclast differentiation → maintains calcium homeostasis and bone mineralization. Also immunomodulatory and anti-proliferative effects.
commonUses:
1. Vitamin D deficiency — treatment and prevention.
2. Rickets and osteomalacia — prevention and treatment.
3. Osteoporosis — adjunct with calcium.
4. Hypoparathyroidism — adjunct to calcium.
5. Chronic kidney disease — secondary hyperparathyroidism.
6. Malabsorption syndromes — supplementation.
7. Psoriasis — topical calcipotriol or oral supplementation.
8. Falls prevention — older adults with deficiency.
9. COVID-19 — immunomodulatory (investigational).
10. Muscle weakness — associated with deficiency.
dose: Deficiency treatment: 50,000 IU orally once weekly for 8 weeks, then 1000–2000 IU daily. Maintenance: 400–2000 IU orally daily. Osteoporosis: 800–2000 IU daily. Severe deficiency: 300,000–600,000 IU IM as single or divided doses. Take with meals containing fat for better absorption.
maxDose: 4000 IU/day (tolerable upper intake level). Toxicity threshold: >10,000 IU/day chronically.
pedDose: Neonates and infants: 400 IU daily (prophylaxis). Deficiency treatment: 1000–5000 IU daily for 6–8 weeks, then maintenance 400–1000 IU daily. Rickets: 1000–10,000 IU daily depending on severity. IM: 150,000–300,000 IU as single dose in severe deficiency.
pedAgeLimit: Safe from birth (prophylactic 400 IU recommended for all breastfed infants).
pedDoseAr: الرضع: 400 وحدة دولية — مرة يوميًا — للوقاية. العلاج: 1000–5000 وحدة دولية — مرة يوميًا — لمدة 6–8 أسابيع — ثم جرعة استمرارية.
lactationNote: Essential for breastfed infants. Maternal supplementation increases breast milk vitamin D levels. Compatible with breastfeeding.
metabolism: Hepatic — hydroxylation to 25-hydroxyvitamin D (calcidiol). Renal — 1-alpha-hydroxylation to active 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D (calcitriol) via CYP27B1.
elimination: Biliary — excreted in bile; half-life: calcidiol 15 days, calcitriol 4–6 hours.
interactions: Thiazide diuretics — increased calcium reabsorption → hypercalcemia risk. Corticosteroids — reduced vitamin D effect and calcium absorption. Orlistat — reduced vitamin D absorption. Cholestyramine — reduced vitamin D absorption. Anticonvulsants (phenytoin, phenobarbital) — increased vitamin D metabolism and deficiency risk. Calcium supplements — additive effect → monitor calcium levels.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: reduced conversion to active calcitriol; may require calcitriol directly. Hepatic impairment: reduced hydroxylation to calcidiol; monitor levels. Elderly: reduced skin synthesis; often require higher supplementation. Obesity: larger volume of distribution; may require 2–3× dose.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): none at therapeutic doses. | Serious: hypercalcemia (nausea, vomiting, constipation, polyuria, confusion → renal failure, cardiac arrhythmia), hyperphosphatemia, nephrocalcinosis with chronic toxicity. | Monitoring: serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D, calcium, phosphorus, PTH (in CKD), urinary calcium in high doses.
contra: Hypercalcemia. Evidence of vitamin D toxicity. Sarcoidosis, tuberculosis (granulomatous diseases — increased calcitriol production). Williams syndrome (supersensitivity to vitamin D).

name: vitamin c (ascorbic acid)
moa: Vitamin supplement/Antioxidant — Water-soluble vitamin — Ascorbic acid acts as reducing agent/antioxidant: donates electrons → neutralizes free radicals (ROS, RNS). Cofactor for prolyl hydroxylase and lysyl hydroxylase → essential for collagen synthesis (hydroxyproline, hydroxylysine), wound healing, capillary integrity. Enhances non-heme iron absorption in GI tract by reducing Fe3+ to Fe2+. Cofactor for catecholamine synthesis (dopamine β-hydroxylase), carnitine biosynthesis.
commonUses:
1. Vitamin C deficiency (scurvy) — treatment and prevention.
2. Immune support — common cold prevention (modest benefit).
3. Wound healing — adjunct, especially post-surgical.
4. Iron absorption enhancement — taken with oral iron.
5. Antioxidant — supplementation in oxidative stress.
6. Methaemoglobinemia — adjunctive reducing agent.
7. Urinary acidification — recurrent UTIs (limited evidence).
8. Critically ill patients — antioxidant replacement.
9. Burn recovery — increased metabolic demand.
10. Smokers — supplementation (smoking increases vitamin C turnover).
dose: Prophylaxis: 100–200mg orally once daily. Deficiency treatment: 500–1000mg orally once or twice daily for 2 weeks. Scurvy: 1000mg orally or IV once or twice daily for 2–4 weeks. Immune support: 500–1000mg orally once daily. Take with or without food.
maxDose: 2000mg/day (tolerable upper intake level). Higher doses short-term for specific indications.
pedDose: Prophylaxis: 25–50mg orally once daily. Deficiency: 100–300mg orally once or twice daily for 2 weeks. Scurvy: 150–300mg orally or IV once or twice daily for 2–4 weeks. Infants: 25–40mg/day.
pedAgeLimit: Safe from birth.
pedDoseAr: الوقاية: 25–50 مجم — مرة يوميًا. العلاج: 100–300 مجم — مرة إلى مرتين يوميًا. الرضع: 25–40 مجم يوميًا.
lactationNote: Excreted in breast milk. Supplementation safe and compatible with breastfeeding at recommended doses.
metabolism: Hepatic — partially metabolized to dehydroascorbic acid, oxalate; reversible oxidation-reduction.
elimination: Renal — excess excreted unchanged; half-life 10–20 days (tissue stores), renal threshold ~1.4mg/dL.
interactions: Iron — enhanced iron absorption — beneficial combination. Warfarin — high-dose vitamin C may reduce anticoagulant effect (limited evidence). Amphetamines — reduced amphetamine excretion. Aspirin — reduced vitamin C absorption and increased renal excretion. Oral contraceptives — reduced vitamin C levels. Chemotherapy — antioxidant may reduce chemotherapeutic efficacy (theoretical) — timing separation advised.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: oxalate is vitamin C metabolite; large doses can increase oxalate burden → caution in history of nephrolithiasis; avoid >1000mg/day. Hepatic impairment: no adjustment. Elderly: no specific adjustment. Smokers: increase dose by 35mg/day.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): GI upset (nausea, diarrhea — dose-related >2g/day), abdominal cramps. | Serious: oxalate nephrolithiasis with chronic high dose (>2g/day), rebound scurvy with abrupt discontinuation after prolonged high dose, hemolysis in G6PD deficiency (with IV high dose). | Monitoring: none specific at standard doses. Check urinary oxalate in stone-formers on high doses.
contra: Hypersensitivity. History of oxalate renal stones (avoid >1g/day). Hemochromatosis (enhances iron absorption). G6PD deficiency — IV high-dose may precipitate hemolysis.

name: multivitamins / minerals
moa: Nutritional supplement — Multivitamin/Multimineral — Provides essential vitamins (A, C, D, E, K, B-complex) and minerals (zinc, selenium, magnesium, calcium, iron depending on formulation) → serve as cofactors for enzymatic reactions, antioxidants, and metabolic regulators → correct or prevent subclinical deficiencies → support cellular energy production, immune function, bone health, erythropoiesis.
commonUses:
1. Nutritional supplementation — prevention of vitamin and mineral deficiency.
2. General health maintenance — adults and elderly.
3. Pregnancy and lactation — increased nutritional demands.
4. Convalescence — post-surgery, post-illness recovery.
5. Physical and mental fatigue — supportive therapy.
6. Poor dietary intake — restrictive diets, elderly, alcohol use disorder.
7. Hair, skin, and nail health — cosmetic nutraceutical support.
8. Stress — increased metabolic demand (B-complex vitamins).
dose: 1 tablet or capsule orally once daily with a meal. Follow specific product labeling as formulations vary (general multivitamin vs. specialized men's/women's/pregnancy formulas). Take with a full glass of water. Do not exceed recommended daily dose.
maxDose: 1 dose/day unless product specifies otherwise. Do not double-dose. Toxicity concerns relate to fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) and minerals (iron, selenium) with chronic overdose.
pedDose: Children >12 years: adult formulation, 1 tablet daily. Children 4–12 years: pediatric formulation at age-appropriate dose (usually half adult dose). Children <4 years: use only pediatric-specific liquid/drop formulations; do not give tablets due to choking risk.
pedAgeLimit: Do not give adult multivitamins to children <12 years. Pediatric formulations safe from 1 year.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال أكبر من 12 سنة: قرص واحد يوميًا مع الأكل. الأطفال 4–12 سنة: تركيبة الأطفال بالجرعة المخصصة للعمر.
lactationNote: Prenatal and lactation-specific formulations are compatible with breastfeeding. Avoid excessive vitamin A (>5000 IU/day).
metabolism: Varies by component — water-soluble vitamins renally excreted; fat-soluble vitamins stored in liver and adipose tissue; minerals have individual metabolic pathways.
elimination: Renal — excess water-soluble vitamins excreted. Fecal — unabsorbed minerals.
interactions: Levodopa — vitamin B6 enhances peripheral decarboxylation → reduced levodopa efficacy (without carbidopa). Warfarin — vitamin K content may antagonize anticoagulation — maintain consistent intake. Levothyroxine — calcium and iron reduce absorption — separate by 4 hours. Tetracyclines and fluoroquinolones — calcium, iron, zinc reduce absorption — separate by 2–3 hours. Bisphosphonates — calcium reduces absorption — separate by 2 hours.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: avoid formulations with high potassium, magnesium, or phosphate in advanced CKD. Hepatic impairment: avoid high-dose vitamin A. Elderly: standard formulations appropriate; consider renal function if high mineral content.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): GI upset (nausea, constipation), metallic taste (mineral content), bright yellow urine (riboflavin — benign). | Serious: hypervitaminosis A, D with chronic excessive intake; iron toxicity in overdose (especially children — leading cause of pediatric poisoning death). | Monitoring: none required at recommended doses. In suspected overdose: serum iron, calcium, vitamin A/D levels.
contra: Hypervitaminosis of any fat-soluble vitamin. Hemochromatosis or iron overload (avoid iron-containing formulations). Wilson's disease (avoid copper). Hypersensitivity to any component.

name: omega-3 fatty acids
moa: Nutritional supplement/Lipid-lowering agent — Omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (EPA/DHA) — Incorporate into cell membrane phospholipids → displace arachidonic acid → reduce production of pro-inflammatory eicosanoids (prostaglandin E2, thromboxane A2, leukotriene B4). Activate PPAR-α → increase hepatic fatty acid oxidation. Reduce hepatic VLDL triglyceride synthesis and secretion. Anti-inflammatory, antiarrhythmic, antithrombotic properties.
commonUses:
1. Hypertriglyceridemia — prescription doses (2–4g/day) reduce triglycerides.
2. Cardiovascular risk reduction — secondary prevention post-MI.
3. Rheumatoid arthritis — anti-inflammatory, reduces joint tenderness.
4. Cognitive function — age-related cognitive decline (modest benefit).
5. Depression — adjunctive therapy (EPA-rich formulations).
6. Dry eye syndrome — improves tear film composition.
7. Pregnancy and lactation — fetal neurodevelopment (DHA).
8. Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease — reduces hepatic steatosis.
dose: General health: 1000mg (1 capsule) orally once or twice daily. Hypertriglyceridemia: 2000–4000mg (2–4g) orally once daily or divided twice daily. Take with meals to improve absorption and reduce GI side effects. Prescription formulations (icosapent ethyl): 2g twice daily with meals.
pedDose: Children >2 years: 250–500mg EPA+DHA orally once daily. ADHD: 500–1000mg/day. Not for infants <2 years without pediatric guidance.
pedAgeLimit: Safe for children >2 years. Infants: DHA-supplemented formulas available.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال أكبر من سنتين: 250–500 مجم — مرة يوميًا — مع الأكل.
lactationNote: DHA important for infant neurodevelopment. Maternal supplementation increases breast milk DHA content. Compatible with breastfeeding.
metabolism: Hepatic — incorporated into phospholipids and triglycerides; beta-oxidation; stored in adipose tissue and cell membranes.
elimination: Minimal renal excretion; half-life of EPA/DHA in plasma: 1–3 days after incorporation into membranes.
interactions: Warfarin/anticoagulants — additive antiplatelet effect — monitor INR (usually not clinically significant at <3g/day). Aspirin — additive antiplatelet effect. Orlistat — reduced omega-3 absorption. Blood pressure medications — additive hypotensive effect (modest).
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment. Hepatic impairment: caution in severe disease (limited data). Elderly: no specific adjustment.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): fishy aftertaste/burp (up to 20% — mitigated by enteric coating or freezing capsules), nausea, abdominal bloating, diarrhea. | Serious: bleeding risk at >3g/day (prolonged bleeding time), atrial fibrillation (dose-related, especially >3g/day), increased LDL cholesterol (in some patients). | Monitoring: lipid profile (triglycerides, LDL), bleeding signs at high doses, liver function.
contra: Hypersensitivity to fish or shellfish (check product source). Active bleeding or bleeding disorders (relative at high doses).

name: selenium / antioxidants
moa: Nutritional supplement/Antioxidant — Essential trace element/Glutathione peroxidase cofactor — Incorporated as selenocysteine into selenoproteins (glutathione peroxidase, thioredoxin reductase, iodothyronine deiodinases) → catalyzes reduction of hydrogen peroxide and organic hydroperoxides → antioxidant defense. Essential for thyroid hormone metabolism (converts T4 to active T3). Modulates immune response and reduces inflammatory cytokine production.
commonUses:
1. Selenium deficiency — nutritional supplementation.
2. Keshan disease — selenium-responsive cardiomyopathy.
3. Hashimoto's thyroiditis — reduces TPO antibodies (modest benefit).
4. Male infertility — improves sperm motility (selenium + vitamin E).
5. Cancer prevention — antioxidant role (limited evidence).
6. Critical illness — antioxidant replacement in oxidative stress.
7. Dandruff and seborrheic dermatitis — topical selenium sulfide.
8. HIV — slows disease progression (limited evidence).
dose: 50–200mcg orally once daily. Deficiency treatment: 100–200mcg daily. Hashimoto's thyroiditis: 200mcg daily for 3–6 months. Male infertility: 200mcg daily with vitamin E. Maximum 400mcg/day. Take with a meal.
maxDose: 400mcg/day (tolerable upper intake level). Chronic toxicity threshold: >800mcg/day.
pedDose: Children: 15–55mcg/day depending on age (RDA). Infants: 15–20mcg/day. Therapeutic supplementation only under medical supervision.
pedAgeLimit: Safe from birth at age-appropriate doses.
pedDoseAr: الجرعة حسب العمر — 15–55 ميكروجرام يوميًا — للعلاج تحت إشراف الطبيب فقط.
lactationNote: RDA 70mcg/day during lactation. Compatible with breastfeeding at recommended doses.
metabolism: Hepatic — incorporated into selenoproteins; methylated and excreted.
elimination: Renal — primarily as methylated metabolites; half-life variable (days to weeks depending on incorporation into selenoproteins).
interactions: Warfarin — high-dose selenium may affect warfarin metabolism (limited evidence). Chemotherapy (cisplatin) — selenium may reduce nephrotoxicity without reducing efficacy (investigational). Valproic acid — selenium may reduce hepatotoxicity.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: reduced excretion → caution with high-dose supplementation. Hepatic impairment: caution (limited data). Elderly: standard dosing appropriate.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): garlic-like breath odor (dose-related), metallic taste, nausea, hair loss (at toxic doses). | Serious: Selenosis (chronic toxicity >800mcg/day): alopecia, nail brittleness and loss, dermatitis, peripheral neuropathy, dental caries. Acute toxicity (>1000mcg): nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, tremor, confusion → myocardial infarction, respiratory failure. | Monitoring: selenium levels if prolonged high-dose use; signs of selenosis.
contra: Selenosis or selenium toxicity. Hypersensitivity.

name: iron
moa: Nutritional supplement/Hematinic — Essential mineral/Hemoglobin component — Iron incorporated into protoporphyrin ring → forms heme → binds oxygen in hemoglobin and myoglobin → oxygen transport and delivery to tissues. Cofactor for cytochromes in mitochondrial electron transport chain → cellular energy production. Absorbed in duodenum via DMT1 transporter; regulated by hepcidin which controls ferroportin-mediated export from enterocytes.
commonUses:
1. Iron deficiency anemia — treatment and prevention.
2. Pregnancy — routine supplementation.
3. Chronic blood loss — menorrhagia, GI bleeding.
4. Malabsorption syndromes — celiac disease, post-gastrectomy.
5. Chronic kidney disease — anemia of CKD (with ESA therapy).
6. Premature infants — prophylaxis.
7. Restless legs syndrome — with documented iron deficiency.
8. Heart failure — iron deficiency even without anemia.
dose: Treatment: 60–200mg elemental iron orally once daily or divided twice daily. Prophylaxis: 30–60mg elemental iron orally once daily. Take on empty stomach (1 hour before or 2 hours after meals) for best absorption, but with food if GI upset occurs. Avoid taking with calcium, antacids, tea, or coffee (inhibit absorption). Duration: 3–6 months to replenish stores after hemoglobin normalizes.
maxDose: 200mg elemental iron/day. Acute toxicity: >20mg/kg elemental iron — potentially fatal.
pedDose: Treatment: 3–6mg/kg/day elemental iron orally divided every 8–12 hours. Prophylaxis in preterm infants: 2mg/kg/day starting at 1–2 months. Maximum 15mg elemental iron/day for infants, 30mg/day for children. Use liquid formulations; measure with calibrated dropper.
pedAgeLimit: Safe from birth. Parenteral iron in neonates only with specialist guidance.
pedDoseAr: العلاج: 3–6 مجم لكل كجم يوميًا — مقسمة كل 8–12 ساعة — على معدة فارغة إن أمكن. الوقاية: 2 مجم/كجم يوميًا للرضع الخدج.
lactationNote: Excreted in breast milk in small amounts. Routine supplementation during lactation is compatible with breastfeeding.
metabolism: Not metabolized — iron is incorporated into hemoglobin, myoglobin, and enzymes; stored as ferritin and hemosiderin in liver, spleen, bone marrow.
elimination: No active excretion mechanism; minimal losses via desquamation, menstruation, and GI tract (1–2mg/day). Half-life not applicable (physiologically recycled).
interactions: Calcium supplements, antacids, proton pump inhibitors, H2 blockers — reduced iron absorption — separate by 2–4 hours. Tetracyclines, fluoroquinolones — reduced absorption of both — separate by 2–3 hours. Levothyroxine — reduced absorption — separate by 4 hours. Methyldopa — reduced antihypertensive effect. Tea, coffee, dairy — tannins, phytates reduce absorption — avoid within 1–2 hours. Vitamin C — enhances absorption — beneficial co-administration.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: use with caution; iron overload risk if ESA therapy and inadequate monitoring. Hepatic impairment: avoid in hemochromatosis. Elderly: start at lower dose if GI side effects; investigate iron deficiency cause (malignancy screening).
sideEffects: Common (>1%): GI upset (nausea, epigastric pain, constipation — especially ferrous sulfate), dark/black stools (benign — unabsorbed iron), metallic taste. | Serious: iron overload/hemochromatosis (chronic excessive intake), acute iron poisoning (leading cause of fatal pediatric poisoning — corrosive to GI mucosa, metabolic acidosis, hepatic necrosis, cardiovascular collapse — requires emergency treatment with deferoxamine). | Monitoring: hemoglobin, hematocrit, serum ferritin, transferrin saturation, reticulocyte count (response at 1–2 weeks).
contra: Hemochromatosis or iron overload disorders. Hemolytic anemias (unless iron deficiency confirmed). Repeated blood transfusions. Active peptic ulcer (relative — worsens GI irritation). Acute iron poisoning.

name: calcium / vitamin k
moa: Nutritional supplement/Bone metabolism — Essential mineral + Fat-soluble vitamin — Calcium: primary structural component of bone (hydroxyapatite crystals); essential for neuromuscular transmission, muscle contraction, cardiac automaticity, blood coagulation (factor IV). Vitamin K: cofactor for gamma-glutamyl carboxylase → activates osteocalcin (bone mineralization) and matrix Gla protein (inhibits vascular calcification). Combined: synergistic effect on bone health; vitamin K2 (MK-7) directs calcium to bone and away from vasculature.
commonUses:
1. Calcium deficiency — supplementation.
2. Osteoporosis — prevention and treatment (with vitamin D).
3. Hypoparathyroidism — adjunct to vitamin D.
4. Chronic kidney disease — phosphate binding (calcium carbonate) and supplementation.
5. Premenstrual syndrome — symptom reduction (modest benefit).
6. Pregnancy-induced hypertension — prevention (with low calcium intake).
7. Postmenopausal bone loss — slows bone resorption.
8. Vitamin K-dependent clotting factor synthesis — supports carboxylation of osteocalcin.
dose: Calcium: 500–1200mg elemental calcium orally once or twice daily. Calcium carbonate: take with meals (requires acid for absorption). Calcium citrate: take with or without food. Vitamin K (MK-7): 45–100mcg orally once daily. Divide doses >500mg elemental calcium for better absorption. Total daily intake (diet + supplement): 1000–1200mg.
maxDose: Calcium: 2500mg/day (tolerable upper intake level — total from diet and supplements). Vitamin K: no established upper limit. Single calcium dose: 500mg elemental maximum per dose for optimal absorption.
pedDose: Calcium: infants 0–6 months: 200mg/day; 6–12 months: 260mg/day; children 1–3 years: 700mg/day; 4–8 years: 1000mg/day; 9–18 years: 1300mg/day. Vitamin K: 2.5–60mcg/day depending on age. Therapeutic supplementation under pediatric guidance.
pedAgeLimit: Safe from birth.
pedDoseAr: الجرعة حسب العمر — الرضع: 200–260 مجم يوميًا — الأطفال: 700–1300 مجم يوميًا — حسب توجيهات الطبيب.
lactationNote: Calcium and vitamin K are compatible with breastfeeding. Supplementation meets increased maternal demands.
metabolism: Calcium: absorbed in duodenum and jejunum via vitamin D-dependent active transport and passive paracellular diffusion. Vitamin K: hepatic carboxylation; recycled via vitamin K epoxide reductase.
elimination: Calcium: renal (major), fecal, sweat. Vitamin K: biliary excretion; half-life 1–2 hours (K1), 3 days (MK-7).
interactions: Levothyroxine — reduced absorption — separate by 4 hours. Tetracyclines, fluoroquinolones — chelation → reduced absorption — separate by 2–3 hours. Bisphosphonates — reduced absorption — separate by 2 hours. Thiazide diuretics — reduced calcium excretion → hypercalcemia risk — monitor calcium. Warfarin — vitamin K antagonizes warfarin effect — avoid vitamin K supplements or maintain consistent intake. Iron — reduced absorption — separate by 4 hours.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: calcium-based phosphate binders may contribute to hypercalcemia and vascular calcification in CKD. Hepatic impairment: caution with vitamin K in severe disease. Elderly: standard dosing; increased calcium requirement.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): GI upset (constipation with calcium carbonate, bloating, gas). | Serious: hypercalcemia (nausea, vomiting, confusion, polyuria, arrhythmia), milk-alkali syndrome (hypercalcemia + metabolic alkalosis + renal failure from excessive calcium + absorbable antacid), nephrolithiasis (excessive calcium supplementation). | Monitoring: serum calcium, phosphorus, PTH, vitamin D in prolonged high-dose therapy.
contra: Hypercalcemia. Severe hypercalciuria. Vitamin K: warfarin therapy (if not maintaining consistent intake). Calcium: ventricular fibrillation (IV calcium).

name: magnesium
moa: Nutritional supplement/Essential mineral — Magnesium — Cofactor for >300 enzymatic reactions including ATP-dependent kinases, DNA/RNA polymerases. Acts as calcium antagonist: physiologic calcium channel blocker → reduces vascular smooth muscle tone (vasodilation, BP lowering). Blocks NMDA receptors → antinociceptive effect (migraine). Essential for neuromuscular transmission: reduces acetylcholine release at motor end plate. Anticonvulsant (IV): cerebral vasodilation, blocks calcium channels. Osmotic laxative effect (unabsorbed magnesium salts draw water into bowel).
commonUses:
1. Magnesium deficiency — treatment and prevention.
2. Hypomagnesemia — associated with diuretics, alcoholism, diarrhea.
3. Constipation — osmotic laxative effect (magnesium hydroxide/citrate).
4. Preeclampsia/eclampsia — seizure prevention and treatment (IV magnesium sulfate).
5. Torsades de pointes — IV magnesium.
6. Migraine prophylaxis — oral magnesium reduces frequency.
7. Muscle cramps — nocturnal leg cramps (modest benefit).
8. Dyspepsia — antacid (magnesium hydroxide).
9. Preterm labor — tocolysis (IV magnesium sulfate).
10. Fibromyalgia — symptom reduction (limited evidence).
dose: Magnesium deficiency: 200–400mg elemental magnesium orally once or twice daily. Migraine prophylaxis: 400–600mg orally once daily at bedtime. Constipation: 15–30mL magnesium hydroxide (milk of magnesia) orally at bedtime. Preeclampsia: 4–6g IV magnesium sulfate loading dose, then 1–2g/hour IV infusion. Take oral supplements with meals to reduce diarrhea.
maxDose: Oral supplementation: 350mg elemental magnesium/day (tolerable upper limit from supplements — GI tolerance). Parenteral: therapeutic serum levels 4–7 mg/dL; toxicity >12mg/dL.
pedDose: Deficiency: 5–10mg/kg/day orally divided every 6–8 hours. Migraine prophylaxis (>6 years): 9mg/kg/day orally divided 3 times daily. Maximum 400mg/day. Constipation: 0.5mL/kg magnesium hydroxide orally at bedtime. Seizures: 25–50mg/kg IV magnesium sulfate (specialist only).
pedAgeLimit: Safe from birth. IV magnesium in neonates only with specialist guidance and cardiorespiratory monitoring.
pedDoseAr: نقص المغنيسيوم: 5–10 مجم لكل كجم يوميًا — مقسمة كل 6–8 ساعات. منع الصداع النصفي: 9 مجم/كجم يوميًا — مقسمة ثلاث مرات.
lactationNote: Compatible with breastfeeding. Standard supplementation poses no risk to infant.
metabolism: Not metabolized — absorbed in small intestine via passive diffusion and active transport (TRPM6/TRPM7 channels); renal regulation via PTH and vitamin D.
elimination: Renal — primarily (magnesium freely filtered, reabsorbed in loop of Henle and distal tubule). Fecal — unabsorbed fraction. Half-life variable depending on tissue stores.
interactions: Tetracyclines, fluoroquinolones — reduced absorption (chelation) — separate by 2–3 hours. Bisphosphonates — reduced absorption — separate by 2 hours. Levothyroxine — reduced absorption — separate by 4 hours. Neuromuscular blocking agents — prolonged blockade — monitor. Calcium channel blockers — additive hypotensive effect. Digoxin — magnesium depletion increases digoxin toxicity risk.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment (eGFR <30): reduce dose 50%; monitor for hypermagnesemia. Severe renal impairment (eGFR <15): avoid magnesium-containing antacids/laxatives. Hepatic impairment: no adjustment. Elderly: reduced renal function; monitor.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): diarrhea (dose-related — osmotic effect), abdominal cramping, nausea, flushing. | Serious: hypermagnesemia (>4mg/dL): hypotension, bradycardia, respiratory depression, loss of deep tendon reflexes, cardiac arrest (>12mg/dL). | Monitoring: serum magnesium, renal function, deep tendon reflexes (IV magnesium — loss of patellar reflex first sign of toxicity), blood pressure, respiratory rate.
contra: Severe renal impairment (eGFR <15) — risk of hypermagnesemia. Myasthenia gravis (IV). Heart block. Hypermagnesemia.

name: cold & flu combination preparations
moa: Combination symptomatic relief — Analgesic/Antipyretic + Decongestant + Antihistamine ± Antitussive/Expectorant — Paracetamol: central COX inhibition (analgesic, antipyretic). Pseudoephedrine: alpha-adrenergic agonist → vasoconstriction of nasal mucosa → decongestion. Chlorpheniramine/diphenhydramine: H1-receptor antagonist → reduces rhinorrhea, sneezing. Dextromethorphan: NMDA antagonist (antitussive at medullary cough center). Guaifenesin: mucolytic/expectorant → increases respiratory secretions and reduces viscosity. Caffeine: adenosine antagonist → enhances analgesia, reduces fatigue. Combined: multi-symptom relief.
commonUses:
1. Common cold — symptomatic relief of nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, sneezing.
2. Acute rhinosinusitis — viral or bacterial.
3. Seasonal allergic rhinitis — with antihistamine component.
4. Influenza — symptomatic treatment as adjunct.
5. Fever associated with URTI — antipyretic component.
6. Cough — productive (with expectorant) or dry (with antitussive).
7. Headache and body aches — analgesic component.
8. Postnasal drip — reduces secretions.
dose: 1–2 tablets/capsules orally every 4–8 hours depending on formulation. Sachet: dissolve contents in hot water, drink 3–4 times daily. Syrup: 10–15mL orally every 4–6 hours. Maximum doses vary by formulation — do not exceed recommended daily dose of each active ingredient. Do not combine multiple cold products containing same active ingredients (especially paracetamol). Maximum paracetamol: 4000mg/day; pseudoephedrine: 240mg/day.
maxDose: Follow individual product labeling. Paracetamol-containing products: max 4000mg/day (3000mg/day preferred). Duration: 48–72 hours without reassessment for most products. Do not use for >7 days.
pedDose: Children 6–12 years: pediatric-specific formulations at half adult dose or as per product. Children 2–6 years: use only pediatric formulations with age-appropriate dosing; avoid combination products with multiple active ingredients if possible. Children <2 years: do not use cold and cough products (FDA recommendation — risk of serious adverse effects, accidental overdose).
pedAgeLimit: Not recommended for children <2 years. Use pediatric formulations for children 2–12 years. Do not use adult formulations in children <12 years.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال 6–12 سنة: تركيبات الأطفال بنصف الجرعة — كل 4–6 ساعات. الأطفال أقل من سنتين: لا تستخدم أدوية البرد. الأطفال 2–6 سنوات: استخدم فقط تركيبات الأطفال المخصصة وبوصفة الطبيب.
lactationNote: Pseudoephedrine may reduce milk supply. Antihistamines may cause infant sedation. Paracetamol is safe. Use single-ingredient products when possible.
metabolism: Varies by component — paracetamol: hepatic glucuronidation/sulfation. Pseudoephedrine: hepatic, minimal. Antihistamines: hepatic CYP450. Dextromethorphan: CYP2D6 (pharmacogenomic variability).
elimination: Varies by component. Paracetamol: renal, half-life 2–4h. Pseudoephedrine: renal 55–75% unchanged, half-life 3–6h. Antihistamines: renal, half-life highly variable.
interactions: MAO inhibitors — hypertensive crisis with pseudoephedrine — contraindicated. Other paracetamol-containing products — additive hepatotoxicity — check all medication labels. SSRIs — serotonin syndrome risk with dextromethorphan component. Alcohol — additive hepatotoxicity (paracetamol), additive CNS depression. Antihypertensives — pseudoephedrine reduces BP-lowering effect. Caffeine — additive stimulant effect with other caffeine sources.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: avoid products with pseudoephedrine (accumulates). Hepatic impairment: reduce paracetamol dose to 2000mg/day maximum. Elderly: may be more sensitive to anticholinergic and stimulant effects; use with caution.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): drowsiness (antihistamine component), insomnia, nervousness, tachycardia (pseudoephedrine), dry mouth, dizziness. | Serious: hepatotoxicity (paracetamol overdose >7.5g), hypertensive crisis (pseudoephedrine + MAOI), serotonin syndrome (dextromethorphan + serotonergic drugs), paradoxical excitation in children (antihistamine), seizures (rare). | Monitoring: symptom relief, blood pressure (if pseudoephedrine), liver function with prolonged use, signs of serotonin syndrome.
contra: MAO inhibitor use within 14 days. Severe hypertension. Severe coronary artery disease. Glaucoma (angle-closure). Urinary retention. Children <2 years. Multiple products containing same active ingredients.

name: lactulose
moa: Laxative/Ammonia reducer — Osmotic disaccharide — Lactulose not absorbed in small intestine; reaches colon where gut bacteria metabolize it → short-chain fatty acids + lactic acid → lowers colonic pH → promotes water retention in bowel lumen (osmotic effect → softens stool, promotes peristalsis). In hepatic encephalopathy: acidic colonic pH converts ammonia (NH3) to non-absorbable ammonium (NH4+) → traps ammonia in bowel for excretion → reduces blood ammonia levels.
commonUses:
1. Constipation — acute and chronic.
2. Hepatic encephalopathy — prevention and treatment.
3. Fecal impaction — osmotic evacuation.
4. Opioid-induced constipation — adjunctive.
5. Elderly constipation — gentle osmotic action.
6. Pediatric functional constipation — first-line.
7. Pregnancy-related constipation — safe option.
8. Bowel preparation — pre-procedural (limited use).
dose: Constipation: 15–30mL (10–20g) orally once or twice daily. May increase to 30–45mL twice daily if needed. Hepatic encephalopathy: 30–45mL orally 3–4 times daily, titrated to produce 2–3 soft stools per day. Take with a full glass of water/juice. May take 24–48 hours for effect.
maxDose: Constipation: 60mL/day. Hepatic encephalopathy: 180mL/day (titrated to bowel response).
pedDose: Infants: 2.5–5mL orally once daily. Children 1–5 years: 5–10mL orally once daily. Children 6–12 years: 10–15mL orally once or twice daily. Adolescents: same as adult. Titrate to produce 1–2 soft stools per day.
pedAgeLimit: Safe from birth. Use with caution in neonates (limited data).
pedDoseAr: الرضع: 2.5–5 مل — مرة يوميًا. الأطفال 1–5 سنوات: 5–10 مل — مرة يوميًا. الأطفال 6–12 سنة: 10–15 مل — مرة إلى مرتين يوميًا.
lactationNote: Not systemically absorbed. Compatible with breastfeeding. Laxative of choice during lactation.
metabolism: Not metabolized in small intestine — colonic bacteria metabolize to short-chain fatty acids, hydrogen, and lactic acid.
elimination: Fecal — primarily as metabolites and unchanged lactulose (small amount). Half-life not applicable.
interactions: No significant systemic drug interactions due to lack of absorption. Other laxatives — additive effect. Oral antibiotics — may reduce colonic bacterial metabolism → reduced lactulose efficacy. Antacids — may reduce effect.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment. Hepatic impairment: effective for hepatic encephalopathy — standard dosing. Elderly: standard dosing; monitor for diarrhea and dehydration.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): flatulence, abdominal bloating/cramps, belching, diarrhea (dose-related — reduce dose). | Serious: dehydration and electrolyte imbalance with excessive diarrhea (especially elderly), hypernatremia (rare — osmotic water loss). | Monitoring: bowel movement frequency and consistency, stool pH (in hepatic encephalopathy — target pH 5–6), mental status (hepatic encephalopathy).
contra: Galactosemia (contains galactose). Intestinal obstruction. Acute surgical abdomen. Fructose intolerance (some formulations contain fructose).

name: bisacodyl
moa: Laxative — Diphenylmethane derivative (stimulant) — Prodrug hydrolyzed by intestinal enzymes and bacteria to active bis-(p-hydroxyphenyl)-pyridyl-2-methane (BHPM) → stimulates colonic peristalsis via direct contact with colonic mucosa → increases motility and reduces transit time. Also increases fluid and electrolyte secretion into bowel lumen. Enteric coating prevents gastric activation. Suppository acts locally in rectum.
commonUses:
1. Constipation — acute, short-term treatment.
2. Bowel preparation — pre-surgical, pre-colonoscopy.
3. Fecal impaction — evacuation (suppository).
4. Neurogenic bowel — spinal cord injury patients.
5. Opioid-induced constipation — adjunct.
6. Postpartum constipation — gentle evacuation.
7. Elderly constipation — short-term relief.
8. Bedridden patients — bowel management.
dose: Oral: 5–15mg (1–3 tablets) orally once daily at bedtime. Onset: 6–10 hours (oral). Suppository: 10mg rectally once daily in the morning. Onset: 15–60 minutes. Do not chew or crush tablets (enteric-coated). Do not take within 1 hour of antacids, dairy products, or PPIs (dissolves enteric coating prematurely).
maxDose: Oral: 30mg (6 tablets) for bowel preparation. Suppository: 10mg/day. Duration: no more than 5 consecutive days without medical review.
pedDose: Children 6–12 years: 5mg orally once daily at bedtime. Children 2–6 years: 5mg suppository rectally once daily. Children <2 years: 2.5–5mg suppository once daily. Do not use oral bisacodyl in children <6 years.
pedAgeLimit: Oral: not for children <6 years. Suppository: safe from 2 years (consult pediatrician <2 years).
pedDoseAr: الأطفال 6–12 سنة: 5 مجم (قرص واحد) — مرة يوميًا — عند النوم. الأطفال 2–6 سنوات: تحميلة 5 مجم — مرة يوميًا. لا تستخدم الأقراص للأطفال أقل من 6 سنوات.
lactationNote: Minimal systemic absorption from oral and rectal administration. Compatible with breastfeeding.
metabolism: Hydrolyzed in intestinal lumen and by gut bacteria to active BHPM. Minimal systemic absorption.
elimination: Fecal — active metabolite excreted in stool; minimal renal excretion. Half-life not applicable.
interactions: Antacids, PPIs, H2 blockers, dairy products — premature dissolution of enteric coating → gastric irritation — separate by at least 1 hour. Other laxatives — additive effect. Diuretics and corticosteroids — increased risk of electrolyte imbalance with excessive use.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment (minimal systemic absorption). Hepatic impairment: no adjustment. Elderly: standard dosing; ensure adequate hydration.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): abdominal cramps, diarrhea, nausea. | Serious: dehydration and electrolyte imbalance (hypokalemia) with chronic excessive use, cathartic colon (chronic overuse → permanent colonic dysfunction), proctitis (suppository — repeated use). | Monitoring: bowel movement frequency, hydration status, electrolyte levels in prolonged use.
contra: Intestinal obstruction or ileus. Acute surgical abdomen (appendicitis, peritonitis). Undiagnosed abdominal pain. Severe dehydration. Rectal fissures (suppository). Ulcerative colitis (severe).

name: sodium picosulfate
moa: Laxative — Diphenylmethane derivative (stimulant) — Prodrug metabolized by intestinal bacteria to active diphenol (bis-(p-hydroxyphenyl)-pyridyl-2-methane — same active metabolite as bisacodyl) → stimulates colonic peristalsis via direct contact with colonic mucosa → increases motility. Also osmotic effect: inhibits water absorption and increases fluid secretion into bowel lumen → softer stool. Less cramping than bisacodyl.
commonUses:
1. Constipation — acute and chronic.
2. Bowel preparation — pre-surgical, pre-endoscopy (in combination with magnesium citrate).
3. Fecal impaction — osmotic and stimulant evacuation.
4. Neurogenic bowel — spinal cord injury.
5. Opioid-induced constipation — adjunctive.
6. Postoperative constipation — gentle stimulant.
7. Bedridden patients — bowel regimen.
8. Pediatric functional constipation — first-line stimulant.
dose: Constipation: 5–10mg (10–20 drops) orally at bedtime. Onset: 6–12 hours (next morning). Bowel prep: 10mg + magnesium oxide 3.5g + citric acid 12g per sachet (Picolax/Picoprep) — follow specific preparation protocol. Do not chew drops; take with water/juice.
maxDose: Constipation: 10mg/day. Bowel prep: 20mg total (2 doses of 10mg separated by several hours). Duration: maximum 7 days for constipation without medical review.
pedDose: Children 4–10 years: 2.5–5mg (5–10 drops) orally at bedtime. Children 2–4 years: 2.5mg (5 drops) at bedtime. Infants <2 years: 1.25–2.5mg (2–5 drops) at bedtime under medical supervision only.
pedAgeLimit: Safe from infancy with appropriate dose adjustment. Use medical supervision <2 years.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال 4–10 سنوات: 5–10 نقاط — عند النوم. الأطفال 2–4 سنوات: 5 نقاط — عند النوم. الرضع أقل من سنتين: 2–5 نقاط — تحت إشراف الطبيب.
lactationNote: Minimal systemic absorption. Not excreted in breast milk in significant amounts. Compatible with breastfeeding.
metabolism: Hepatic — minimal; hydrolyzed in intestinal lumen and by gut bacteria to active metabolite.
elimination: Fecal — primarily as active metabolite; minimal renal excretion.
interactions: No significant systemic drug interactions. Other laxatives — additive effect. Diuretics and corticosteroids — increased electrolyte disturbance risk with excessive use.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment (minimal systemic absorption). Hepatic impairment: no adjustment. Elderly: standard dosing; monitor for dehydration.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): abdominal cramps (less than bisacodyl), diarrhea, nausea. | Serious: dehydration and electrolyte imbalance (hypokalemia) with chronic excessive use, melanosis coli (benign brown-black colonic pigmentation with prolonged use — reversible). | Monitoring: bowel movement frequency, hydration status, electrolytes in prolonged use.
contra: Intestinal obstruction or ileus. Acute surgical abdomen (appendicitis, peritonitis). Severe dehydration. Inflammatory bowel disease (severe acute phase). Hypersensitivity.

name: methylcobalamin
moa: Acts as a cofactor for methionine synthase (converts homocysteine to methionine) and methylmalonyl-CoA mutase. Essential for myelin synthesis, DNA production, and red blood cell maturation.
commonUses: 1. Vitamin B12 deficiency — pernicious anemia, malabsorption syndromes, dietary deficiency. 2. Diabetic neuropathy — adjunctive therapy for pain and paresthesia. 3. Megaloblastic anemia — due to B12 deficiency.
dose: B12 deficiency: 1000 mcg orally once daily OR 1000 mcg IM once weekly for 4 weeks then 1000 mg IM monthly. Neuropathy: 500–1500 mcg orally daily in divided doses.
maxDose: Oral: 2000 mcg/day. IM: 1000 mcg/week for maintenance.
pedDose: Deficiency: 6–10 mcg/kg/day orally or IM. Max: 1000 mcg/day. Neonates: 0.5–1 mcg/kg/day.
pedAgeLimit: Safe for all ages. For infants <1 year: use under guidance.
pedDoseAr: نقص فيتامين ب12: 6–10 ميكروجرام لكل كيلوجرام يوميًا — عن طريق الفم أو حقن عضل — الحد الأقصى 1000 ميكروجرام يوميًا. للرضع: 0.5–1 ميكروجرام لكل كيلوجرام يوميًا.
lactationNote: Passes into milk but is a normal nutrient; no harm expected.
metabolism: Hepatic — converted to active coenzyme forms (methylcobalamin and adenosylcobalamin).
elimination: Renal — 50–98% excreted unchanged in urine. Half-life: 6 days (stored in liver).
interactions: Chloramphenicol — may reduce hematopoietic response — monitor CBC. Proton pump inhibitors — reduce absorption — consider alternative or monitor B12 levels.
doseAdjustments: Renal: No adjustment. Hepatic: No adjustment.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Diarrhea, rash, injection site pain (IM). Serious: Hypokalemia (after starting therapy for severe anemia), anaphylaxis (rare).
contra: Hypersensitivity to cobalt or cobalamin. Leber's disease (optic atrophy may worsen).

name: royal jelly
moa: Contains proteins, lipids, vitamins, and phenolic compounds. May modulate immune function, reduce oxidative stress, and improve lipid profile, but exact mechanisms are not fully understood.
commonUses: 1. General nutritional support — as a dietary supplement. 2. Menopausal symptoms — some evidence for reducing hot flashes. 3. Fatigue — used for improving energy and well-being.
dose: 300–1000 mg orally once daily. Typically taken on an empty stomach.
pedDose: Not generally recommended for children. No established pediatric dose.
pedAgeLimit: >18 years (adults only).
pedDoseAr: غير موصى به للأطفال. فقط للبالغين.
lactationNote: Insufficient data; avoid or consult physician.
metabolism: Unknown, likely enzymatic digestion.
elimination: Unknown.
interactions: No well-documented drug interactions. May theoretically affect blood sugar (caution with antidiabetics).
doseAdjustments: No adjustments established.
sideEffects: Common: Allergic reactions (especially in asthmatics or those allergic to bee products), mild gastrointestinal upset. Serious: Anaphylaxis (rare).
contra: Hypersensitivity to bee products, royal jelly, or pollen. Asthma (risk of bronchospasm).

name: paracetamol + pseudoephedrine
moa: Pseudoephedrine stimulates alpha-1 adrenergic receptors in nasal mucosa → vasoconstriction → reduces congestion. Paracetamol inhibits COX enzymes centrally → raises pain threshold and reduces fever.
commonUses: 1. Nasal congestion — associated with common cold or allergic rhinitis. 2. Sinus pain and pressure — with fever or headache. 3. Acute upper respiratory tract infection — symptomatic relief.
dose: Paracetamol 500 mg + pseudoephedrine 30 mg orally every 6 hours as needed. Max: 4 doses (2000 mg paracetamol / 120 mg pseudoephedrine) per day.
pedDose: Not recommended for children <12 years. For 12–17 years: 500/30 mg every 6 hours as needed, max 4 doses in 24h.
