pedAgeLimit: >12 years (for combination product). Single-ingredient forms available for younger children.
pedDoseAr: للأطفال من 12 إلى 17 سنة: 500/30 مليجرام — قرص واحد كل 6 ساعات عند الحاجة — بحد أقصى 4 أقراص يوميًا. غير مناسب لمن هم أقل من 12 سنة.
lactationNote: Pseudoephedrine may pass into milk and cause irritability in infant. Paracetamol is safe.
metabolism: Hepatic — paracetamol glucuronidation/sulfation; pseudoephedrine partially hepatic.
elimination: Renal — half-life 2–3 hours for both.
interactions: MAO inhibitors — risk of hypertensive crisis (avoid within 14 days). Beta-blockers — reduced decongestant effect. Warfarin — paracetamol may potentiate effect (monitor INR).
doseAdjustments: Severe renal impairment (eGFR <30): paracetamol: extend interval to 8h; pseudoephedrine: avoid or reduce. Hepatic impairment: paracetamol: reduce dose or avoid (max 2g/day).
sideEffects: Common: Dry mouth, insomnia, nervousness, headache, nausea. Serious: Hypertension, tachycardia, severe allergic reactions (rare).
contra: Severe hypertension, coronary artery disease, MAO inhibitor use within 14 days, severe liver disease.

name: calcium carbonate + vitamin d3
moa: Calcium is essential for bone mineralization, neuromuscular transmission, and cardiac contractility. Vitamin D3 increases intestinal calcium absorption (via active transport) and promotes renal calcium reabsorption.
commonUses: 1. Calcium supplementation — for dietary deficiency or increased requirements (pregnancy, lactation, adolescence). 2. Osteoporosis prevention and treatment — adjunct to other therapies in postmenopausal women and elderly. 3. Hypocalcemia — correction of low serum calcium.
dose: Prevention: 500 mg calcium + 200 IU vitamin D3 orally once daily. Treatment: 1000 mg calcium + 800 IU vitamin D3 daily in divided doses (e.g., 500/400 twice daily). Take with meals for better absorption.
maxDose: Calcium: 1500 mg/day elemental. Vitamin D3: 4000 IU/day.
pedDose: Children 4–8 years: 500 mg calcium + 400 IU D3 once daily. Children 9–18 years: 1000 mg calcium + 600 IU D3 once daily or in divided doses.
pedAgeLimit: >4 years for this combination. Infants and toddlers: separate formulations.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال 4–8 سنوات: 500 مليجرام كالسيوم + 400 وحدة د3 — قرص واحد يوميًا. 9–18 سنة: 1000 مليجرام كالسيوم + 600 وحدة د3 — قرص واحد يوميًا أو مقسمة.
lactationNote: Safe and often recommended for maternal bone health.
metabolism: Vitamin D3: Hepatic to 25-hydroxyvitamin D, then renal to active 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D. Calcium: not metabolized.
elimination: Calcium: renal and fecal. Vitamin D3: biliary and renal. Half-life: 15–25 days for 25(OH)D.
interactions: Thiazide diuretics — increased risk of hypercalcemia — monitor calcium. Bisphosphonates — reduced absorption — separate by at least 2 hours. Thyroid hormone — may reduce absorption — separate by 4 hours.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: caution with severe CKD (eGFR <30); avoid or monitor calcium levels. Hepatic: no adjustment.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Constipation, nausea, bloating, hypercalcemia (with excessive doses). Serious: Milk-alkali syndrome, nephrolithiasis.
contra: Hypercalcemia, severe hypercalciuria, nephrolithiasis, vitamin D toxicity.

name: paracetamol + chlorpheniramine + pseudoephedrine
moa: Chlorpheniramine blocks H1 histamine receptors → reduces sneezing, rhinorrhea, and itching. Pseudoephedrine stimulates alpha-1 receptors in nasal mucosa → vasoconstriction → decongestion. Paracetamol inhibits central COX enzymes → antipyresis and analgesia.
commonUses: 1. Common cold symptoms — fever, headache, nasal congestion, runny nose, sneezing. 2. Allergic rhinitis with fever — seasonal or perennial. 3. Upper respiratory tract infection — symptomatic relief.
dose: 1–2 tablets/capsules orally every 4–6 hours as needed. Max: 8 tablets/capsules per day (paracetamol 4g limit).
pedDose: Children 6–11 years: half tablet/capsule every 4–6 hours, max 4 half-doses/day. Children 12+ years: same as adult.
pedAgeLimit: >6 years. Not for children under 6 years.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال 6–11 سنة: نصف قرص أو كبسولة كل 4–6 ساعات — بحد أقصى 4 مرات يوميًا. 12 سنة فأكثر: نفس جرعة البالغين.
lactationNote: Chlorpheniramine may cause drowsiness in infant; pseudoephedrine may cause irritability.
metabolism: Hepatic — paracetamol glucuronidation; chlorpheniramine CYP2D6; pseudoephedrine minimal hepatic.
elimination: Renal — half-life: paracetamol 2-3h, chlorpheniramine 12-15h, pseudoephedrine 4-6h.
interactions: MAO inhibitors — hypertensive crisis (avoid). Sedatives — additive CNS depression. Warfarin — paracetamol may potentiate (monitor INR).
doseAdjustments: Severe renal impairment (eGFR <30): avoid pseudoephedrine; paracetamol extend to 8h. Hepatic impairment: paracetamol max 2g/day.
sideEffects: Common: Drowsiness, dry mouth, blurred vision, urinary retention, constipation, nervousness, insomnia. Serious: Severe allergic reactions, arrhythmias (rare).
contra: Severe hypertension, coronary artery disease, MAO inhibitors (within 14 days), severe liver disease, narrow-angle glaucoma, urinary retention.

name: paracetamol + phenylephrine
moa: Phenylephrine is a direct-acting alpha-1 adrenergic agonist → vasoconstricts nasal mucosal blood vessels → reduces congestion and swelling. Paracetamol inhibits COX centrally → antipyresis and analgesia.
commonUses: 1. Nasal congestion — due to common cold or sinusitis. 2. Fever with congestion — symptomatic relief. 3. Sinus headache — associated with nasal stuffiness.
dose: Paracetamol 500 mg + phenylephrine 5–10 mg orally every 4 hours as needed. Max: 6 doses/day (paracetamol 3g limit in combination products often).
maxDose: Paracetamol: 4g/day (but combination products often limit to 3g). Phenylephrine: 60 mg/day.
pedDose: Children 6–11 years: 250 mg paracetamol + 2.5 mg phenylephrine every 4 hours, max 4 doses/day. Children 12+ years: adult dose.
pedAgeLimit: >6 years.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال 6–11 سنة: 250/2.5 مليجرام — نصف قرص كل 4 ساعات — بحد أقصى 4 مرات يوميًا. 12 سنة فأكثر: جرعة البالغين.
lactationNote: Phenylephrine may reduce milk supply; limited data on safety.
metabolism: Hepatic — paracetamol glucuronidation; phenylephrine MAO metabolism (gut and liver).
elimination: Renal — half-life: paracetamol 2-3h, phenylephrine 2-3h.
interactions: MAO inhibitors — severe hypertension (avoid). Beta-blockers — reduced decongestant effect. Digoxin — increased arrhythmia risk with phenylephrine.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment (eGFR <30): paracetamol extend to 8h. Hepatic impairment: paracetamol max 2g/day. Severe hypertension: avoid phenylephrine.
sideEffects: Common: Headache, nausea, nervousness, insomnia, increased blood pressure. Serious: Severe hypertension, arrhythmias (rare with oral dosing).
contra: Severe hypertension, MAO inhibitors (within 14 days), severe liver disease.

name: guaifenesin
moa: Increases respiratory tract fluid volume and reduces sputum viscosity via vagal reflex stimulation. Thought to loosen bronchial secretions, making cough more productive, though mechanism not fully established.
commonUses: 1. Productive cough — to loosen phlegm and thin bronchial secretions in acute respiratory infections. 2. Bronchitis — as adjunctive therapy. 3. Common cold with chest congestion — symptomatic relief.
dose: 200–400 mg orally every 4 hours as needed. Max: 2400 mg/day. Take with full glass of water.
pedDose: Children 4–5 years: 50–100 mg every 4 hours, max 600 mg/day. Children 6–11 years: 100–200 mg every 4 hours, max 1200 mg/day.
pedAgeLimit: >4 years (for guaifenesin-only products).
pedDoseAr: الأطفال 4–5 سنوات: 50–100 مليجرام كل 4 ساعات — بحد أقصى 600 مليجرام يوميًا. 6–11 سنوات: 100–200 مليجرام كل 4 ساعات — بحد أقصى 1200 مليجرام يوميًا.
lactationNote: Minimal excretion into breast milk; considered compatible with breastfeeding.
metabolism: Hepatic — extensively metabolized to beta-(2-methoxyphenoxy)-lactic acid.
elimination: Renal — half-life: 1-2 hours.
interactions: None clinically significant. May interfere with 5-HIAA and VMA urine tests (discontinue 48 hours before).
doseAdjustments: No specific adjustments. Use caution in severe renal impairment.
sideEffects: Common: Nausea, vomiting, dizziness, headache, rash. Overdose: Kidney stones (with chronic high doses).
contra: Hypersensitivity. Chronic cough requiring diagnosis (e.g., asthma, GERD, COPD).

name: prucalopride
moa: Selectively agonizes 5-HT4 receptors in the gut wall → enhances colonic peristalsis by increasing acetylcholine release from enteric neurons → accelerates colonic transit time and improves bowel movement frequency.
commonUses: 1. Chronic idiopathic constipation — in women and men when laxatives fail to provide adequate relief. 2. Opioid-induced constipation — off-label use. 3. Chronic intestinal pseudo-obstruction — off-label.
dose: 2 mg orally once daily. For elderly (>65 years): 1 mg once daily. Take with or without food at any time of day.
pedDose: Not approved for pediatric use. Limited data: 0.03 mg/kg once daily in studies (off-label).
pedAgeLimit: >18 years. Not recommended for children.
pedDoseAr: غير معتمد للاستخدام في الأطفال. فقط للبالغين.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk; avoid or discontinue breastfeeding.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP3A4 (minimal, 10%); majority metabolized by carboxylesterase enzymes.
elimination: Renal (60–70% unchanged + metabolites) and fecal. Half-life: 24-30 hours.
interactions: Probenecid, cimetidine, trimethoprim — increase prucalopride levels (reduce dose to 1mg/day). Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole) — minimal clinical effect.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: eGFR 30–50: 1mg/day; eGFR <30: 1mg every other day. Hepatic impairment: Child-Pugh B or C: 1mg/day.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Headache, nausea, diarrhea, abdominal pain, flatulence, dizziness. Usually mild and transient. Serious: Suicidal ideation (rare; use caution in depressed patients).
contra: Hypersensitivity. Intestinal perforation or obstruction. Severe inflammatory bowel disease. Recent abdominal surgery.

name: lubiprostone
moa: Activates type-2 chloride channels (ClC-2) on the luminal surface of GI epithelium → increases chloride-rich fluid secretion into intestinal lumen → softens stool and enhances motility without altering electrolyte balance.
commonUses: 1. Chronic idiopathic constipation — in adults. 2. Irritable bowel syndrome with constipation (IBS-C) — in women (approved, men off-label). 3. Opioid-induced constipation — in adults with chronic non-cancer pain.
dose: Constipation: 24 mcg orally twice daily with food and water. IBS-C: 8 mcg twice daily. OIC: 24 mcg twice daily. Take with food and full glass of water.
pedDose: Not approved for pediatric use. Safety and efficacy not established in children.
pedAgeLimit: >18 years.
pedDoseAr: غير معتمد للاستخدام في الأطفال. فقط للبالغين.
lactationNote: Unknown if excreted into breast milk; avoid or discontinue breastfeeding.
metabolism: Hepatic — extensively metabolized via CYP3A4; major metabolite M3 (active).
elimination: Fecal (60%) and renal (∼20%). Half-life: 1-2 hours (parent); 4-6 hours (M3 metabolite).
interactions: No clinically significant drug-drug interactions identified. Best taken separate from other medications (≥2 hours apart due to accelerated transit).
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment. Hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C): reduce to 16 mcg twice daily (adjust tolerability).
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Nausea (31% — take with food), diarrhea (13%), headache (11%), abdominal pain, bloating, dyspnea (rare but characteristic). Serious: Severe dyspnea usually within 1 hour of first dose; reversible. Syncope due to hypotension (rare).
contra: Hypersensitivity. Mechanical GI obstruction.

name: senna
moa: Bacterial enzymes in the colon convert senna glycosides to active rhein anthrone → stimulates colonic myenteric plexus → increases peristalsis and inhibits fluid absorption → produces soft stool within 6–12 hours.
commonUses: 1. Constipation — short-term treatment for occasional constipation. 2. Bowel preparation — before colonoscopy, surgery, or radiological procedures. 3. Opioid-induced constipation — adjunctive therapy.
dose: 15 mg orally once daily at bedtime. Max: 30 mg/day (two 15 mg tablets) for up to 7 days. Expected bowel movement within 6–12 hours.
maxDose: 30 mg/day. Use beyond 7 days not recommended without medical advice.
pedDose: Children 2–6 years: 2.5–5 mg at bedtime. Children 6–12 years: 5–10 mg at bedtime. Max: 7 days.
pedAgeLimit: >2 years. Not for infants.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال 2–6 سنوات: 2.5–5 مليجرام عند النوم. 6–12 سنوات: 5–10 مليجرام عند النوم. لمدة لا تتجاوز 7 أيام.
lactationNote: Minimal excretion into breast milk; short-term use considered compatible but monitor infant for diarrhea.
metabolism: Colonic bacteria — hydrolyzed to active metabolite rhein. Minimal hepatic metabolism.
elimination: Fecal (primarily) and renal (small amount). Half-life: not clinically applicable.
interactions: Diuretics and corticosteroids — increased risk of hypokalemia (monitor potassium). Cardiac glycosides — hypokalemia increases digoxin toxicity risk. Other laxatives — excessive bowel stimulation.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment needed. Hepatic impairment: no adjustment needed.
sideEffects: Common: Abdominal cramps, nausea, diarrhea, electrolyte imbalance (with prolonged use). Serious: Melanosis coli (benign pigmentation with long-term use), hepatotoxicity (rare with overdose).
contra: Intestinal obstruction, acute surgical abdomen, fecal impaction, undiagnosed abdominal pain, appendicitis, inflammatory bowel disease (acute phase).

name: magnesium hydroxide
moa: Magnesium hydroxide acts as an osmotic agent — poorly absorbed magnesium retains water in the intestinal lumen → increases intraluminal pressure → stimulates peristalsis and bowel evacuation within 30 minutes to 6 hours. As antacid: neutralizes gastric acid.
commonUses: 1. Constipation — acute relief of occasional constipation. 2. Gastric hyperacidity — as an antacid for heartburn, indigestion, and sour stomach. 3. Bowel preparation — before procedures.
dose: Laxative: 30–60 mL (800–1600 mg) orally once daily at bedtime with a full glass of water. Antacid: 5–15 mL (200–600 mg) orally as needed, up to 4 times daily.
maxDose: Laxative: 60 mL/day (1600 mg). Antacid: 60 mL/day. Use not exceeding 7 days for laxative purpose.
pedDose: Laxative: Children 6–11 years: 15–30 mL once daily. Antacid: Children 6–11 years: 5–10 mL as needed, max 4 times daily. Not for children under 6 years.
pedAgeLimit: >6 years. Avoid in children <6 years due to risk of hypermagnesemia.
pedDoseAr: ملين: الأطفال 6–11 سنوات: 15–30 مل مرة يوميًا عند النوم. مضاد حموضة: 5–10 مل عند الحاجة — بحد أقصى 4 مرات يوميًا.
lactationNote: Minimal absorption; considered safe during breastfeeding.
metabolism: Not metabolized. Dissociates into magnesium and hydroxide ions.
elimination: Renal (magnesium excreted by kidneys). Half-life: not applicable.
interactions: Tetracyclines — reduced absorption (separate by 2–3 hours). Fluoroquinolones — reduced absorption (separate by 2–4 hours). Bisphosphonates — reduced absorption (separate by 2 hours).
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: avoid or use extreme caution (risk of hypermagnesemia). Hepatic impairment: no adjustment.
sideEffects: Common: Diarrhea, abdominal cramping, dehydration (with excessive use). Serious: Hypermagnesemia (renal impairment), electrolyte imbalance with prolonged use.
contra: Severe renal impairment (eGFR <30), hypermagnesemia, intestinal obstruction, undiagnosed abdominal pain, appendicitis.

name: docusate sodium
moa: Docusate is an anionic surfactant that lowers surface tension at the oil-water interface in the intestine → allows water and lipids to penetrate the fecal mass → softens stool without directly stimulating peristalsis. Bowel movement occurs by normal peristalsis.
commonUses: 1. Constipation — prevention and treatment of occasional constipation, especially when straining should be avoided (post-myocardial infarction, post-hemorrhoidectomy, postpartum). 2. Bowel preparation — adjunct to stimulant laxatives before procedures. 3. Opioid-induced constipation — adjunctive therapy.
dose: 100 mg orally once to three times daily. For best results, take with full glass of water. Expect bowel movement within 12–72 hours.
pedDose: Children 3–6 years: 20–40 mg/day in divided doses. Children 6–12 years: 40–120 mg/day in divided doses. Single or split into 2–3 doses.
pedAgeLimit: >3 years. Not recommended for infants <3 years.
pedDoseAr: الأطفال 3–6 سنوات: 20–40 مليجرام يوميًا مقسمة. 6–12 سنوات: 40–120 مليجرام يوميًا مقسمة على جرعتين إلى ثلاث.
lactationNote: Minimal systemic absorption; considered safe during breastfeeding.
metabolism: Minimal — not significantly metabolized.
elimination: Fecal (>90% excreted unchanged). Half-life: not clinically significant.
interactions: Mineral oil — increased absorption of mineral oil (avoid concurrent use). Other laxatives — additive effects.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment. Hepatic impairment: no adjustment.
sideEffects: Common: Mild abdominal cramps, diarrhea, throat irritation (with liquid forms). Serious: Electrolyte imbalance (with chronic or excessive use only), rash (rare).
contra: Intestinal obstruction, acute abdominal pain, nausea/vomiting (especially if appendicitis suspected), concurrent use with mineral oil.

name: glycerin
moa: Glycerin exerts a hyperosmotic effect when inserted rectally → draws water into the rectum by osmosis → stimulates local rectal mucosa → triggers defecation reflex and softens impacted stool within 15–60 minutes.
commonUses: 1. Constipation — rectal use for rapid relief of occasional constipation, especially when oral laxatives are contraindicated. 2. Bowel evacuation — before digital rectal examination or proctoscopy.
dose: Adult suppository: one suppository (2–3 g) inserted rectally as needed. Expect bowel movement within 15–60 minutes. Use no more than one suppository per day.
maxDose: One suppository per day. Not for continuous use beyond 7 days.
pedDose: Infant suppository (1 g): one suppository as needed. Child suppository: children 2–6 years: one small suppository (1–1.5 g) as needed. Children 6–12 years: one paediatric suppository (2 g) as needed.
pedAgeLimit: Infant suppository: >3 months. Child suppository: >2 years.
pedDoseAr: للرضع: تحميلة واحدة (1 جرام) عند الحاجة. للأطفال 2–6 سنوات: تحميلة صغيرة (1–1.5 جرام) عند الحاجة. 6–12 سنة: تحميلة أطفال (2 جرام) عند الحاجة.
lactationNote: Minimal systemic absorption; safe during breastfeeding.
metabolism: Not metabolized systemically.
elimination: Fecal (excreted unchanged). Half-life: not applicable.
interactions: None clinically significant with rectal use.
doseAdjustments: No adjustments needed.
sideEffects: Common: Rectal irritation, burning sensation, abdominal cramping, diarrhea. Serious: Rectal bleeding (rare, discontinue use).
contra: Intestinal obstruction, acute abdominal pain, nausea/vomiting (suspect appendicitis), fecal impaction requiring manual disimpaction, hemorrhoids with active bleeding.

name: butamirate
moa: Butamirate acts centrally on the cough center in the medulla oblongata without affecting respiratory drive (unlike opioids). Reduces cough reflex sensitivity and has mild bronchodilator and expectorant properties. Does not cause dependence or respiratory depression.
commonUses: 1. Non-productive (dry) cough — of any etiology including acute respiratory infections, laryngitis, tracheitis, and bronchitis. 2. Preoperative cough suppression — to prevent cough during intubation. 3. Whooping cough (pertussis) — adjunctive symptomatic relief.
dose: Syrup 15 mg/5ml: 15 mL (45 mg) orally 3–4 times daily. Tablets 20 mg: 1 tablet (20 mg) every 8 hours. Max: 120 mg/day.
pedDose: Children 3–6 years (weight 14–20 kg): 5 mL syrup (15 mg) 3 times daily. Children 6–9 years (20–30 kg): 10 mL (30 mg) 3 times daily. Children 9–12 years (30–40 kg): 15 mL (45 mg) 3 times daily. Drops for infants: age-based weight dosing.
pedAgeLimit: Drops: >2 months. Syrup: >3 years. Tablets: >12 years.
pedDoseAr: للأطفال 3–6 سنوات: 5 مل شراب (15 مليجرام) — 3 مرات يوميًا. 6–9 سنوات: 10 مل (30 مليجرام) — 3 مرات يوميًا. 9–12 سنة: 15 مل (45 مليجرام) — 3 مرات يوميًا.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk; avoid or discontinue breastfeeding during treatment.
metabolism: Hepatic — extensively metabolized by CYP2D6 to active metabolites.
elimination: Renal (approximately 90% as metabolites). Half-life: 6 hours.
interactions: CNS depressants — additive sedative effect (caution with alcohol, benzodiazepines, antihistamines). MAO inhibitors — potential interaction (avoid concurrent use).
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: caution, reduce dose. Hepatic impairment: caution, reduce dose.
sideEffects: Common: Drowsiness, dizziness, nausea, diarrhea, rash. Serious: Allergic reactions (rare).
contra: Hypersensitivity. First trimester of pregnancy. Myasthenia gravis. Concurrent use with expectorants (may cause mucus accumulation). Infants <2 months.

name: ivy leaf extract
moa: Ivy leaf saponins (primarily alpha-hederin) stimulate beta-2 adrenergic receptors in bronchial epithelium → increases surfactant secretion → reduces mucus viscosity and enhances mucociliary clearance. Also has mild bronchodilator and anti-inflammatory effects.
commonUses: 1. Productive (wet) cough — acute and chronic bronchitis. 2. Respiratory tract infections — as an expectorant to clear mucus. 3. Bronchial asthma (adjunct) — may help thin secretions.
dose: Syrup (0.84 g/100ml): 5–7.5 mL (42–63 mg) orally three times daily. Elixir: 5 mL three times daily.
maxDose: 75 mg three times daily for adults.
pedDose: Children 2–4 years: 2.5 mL (21 mg) three times daily. Children 4–12 years: 5 mL (42 mg) three times daily. Infants 0–2 years: 1–2 mL (8.4–16.8 mg) three times daily (use paediatric drops).
pedAgeLimit: Syrup: >2 years. Drops: >3 months.
pedDoseAr: للأطفال 2–4 سنوات: 2.5 مل — 3 مرات يوميًا. 4–12 سنة: 5 مل — 3 مرات يوميًا. للرضع 3–24 شهرًا: 1–2 مل — 3 مرات يوميًا.
lactationNote: Insufficient safety data; avoid or consult physician.
metabolism: Not well characterized; saponins are partially metabolized in the gut.
elimination: Unknown.
interactions: None well-documented. Theoretical caution with bronchodilators (additive effect).
doseAdjustments: No specific adjustments needed.
sideEffects: Common: Mild gastrointestinal upset (nausea, diarrhea). Rare: Allergic reactions (rash, pruritus).
contra: Hypersensitivity to ivy or other Araliaceae family plants.

name: ambroxol
moa: Ambroxol stimulates type II pneumocytes → increases surfactant production and reduces mucus viscosity by breaking disulfide bonds in mucoproteins. Also increases airway fluid secretion → improves mucociliary clearance. Has local anesthetic and anti-inflammatory properties.
commonUses: 1. Productive cough — acute and chronic bronchitis, bronchial asthma, COPD. 2. Respiratory tract disorders — with excessive or thick mucus. 3. Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) — off-label for surfactant stimulation.
dose: 30–60 mg orally three times daily. Extended-release: 75 mg once daily. Max: 120 mg/day.
pedDose: Children 2–5 years: 7.5 mg (2.5 mL syrup) three times daily. Children 6–12 years: 15 mg (5 mL syrup) three times daily. Children 12+ years: adult dose.
pedAgeLimit: Syrup: >2 years. Not for infants <2 years without medical advice.
pedDoseAr: للأطفال 2–5 سنوات: 7.5 مجم (2.5 مل شراب) — 3 مرات يوميًا. 6–12 سنة: 15 مجم (5 مل شراب) — 3 مرات يوميًا.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk; avoid or use with caution.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP3A4 extensively to active metabolites.
elimination: Renal (80–90% as metabolites). Half-life: 7–12 hours.
interactions: Antibiotics (amoxicillin, cefuroxime, erythromycin) — may increase antibiotic concentration in lung tissue (potentially beneficial). Antitussives — concurrent use may lead to mucus accumulation (avoid).
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: severe (eGFR <30): reduce dose by 50%. Hepatic impairment: caution, reduce dose.
sideEffects: Common: Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, heartburn, rash. Serious: Severe allergic reactions (rare), anaphylaxis.
contra: Hypersensitivity. First trimester of pregnancy. Peptic ulcer disease.

name: carbocisteine
moa: Carbocisteine reduces mucus viscosity by regulating sialomucin production in bronchial glands → decreases the number and size of mucin disulfide bridges → normalizing the ratio of acidic to neutral mucoproteins. Unlike N-acetylcysteine, it does not act directly on disulfide bonds.
commonUses: 1. Productive cough — acute and chronic respiratory disorders including bronchitis, COPD, and bronchiectasis. 2. Thick mucus — to reduce viscosity and facilitate clearance. 3. Chronic rhinosinusitis — adjunctive therapy.
dose: 750 mg (two 375 mg capsules or 15 mL syrup 250mg/5ml) orally three times daily. Maintenance: 750–1500 mg daily in divided doses. Max: 2250 mg/day.
pedDose: Children 2–5 years: 125–250 mg three times daily. Children 6–12 years: 250–375 mg three times daily. Children 12+ years: adult dose.
pedAgeLimit: >2 years. Not for infants <2 years.
pedDoseAr: للأطفال 2–5 سنوات: 125–250 مجم — 3 مرات يوميًا. 6–12 سنة: 250–375 مجم — 3 مرات يوميًا.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk; avoid or use with caution.
metabolism: Hepatic — partially metabolized.
elimination: Renal (mostly unchanged). Half-life: 2 hours.
interactions: Antitussives — may cause mucus accumulation (avoid concurrent use). Antibiotics — no significant interactions.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: reduce dose by 50–75% if eGFR <30. Hepatic impairment: caution, but no specific adjustment.
sideEffects: Common: Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, headache. Serious: Bullous skin reactions (rare — Stevens-Johnson syndrome), GI bleeding.
contra: Hypersensitivity. Active peptic ulcer disease.

name: bisoprolol
moa: Bisoprolol selectively blocks beta-1 adrenergic receptors in the heart → reduces heart rate, decreases myocardial contractility, and lowers renin release from the kidneys → decreased cardiac output and blood pressure. Cardioselectivity minimizes bronchospasm risk.
commonUses: 1. Hypertension — alone or in combination with other antihypertensives. 2. Chronic stable angina — reduces cardiac workload and oxygen demand. 3. Chronic heart failure — stable NYHA Class II–III with reduced ejection fraction (≤35% on ACE-inhibitors, diuretics, and optionally digoxin).
dose: Hypertension/Angina: 5 mg orally once daily. May increase to 10 mg once daily if needed. Heart failure: Start 1.25 mg once daily, double every 2 weeks to target 10 mg once daily as tolerated.
maxDose: 10 mg/day (20 mg/day in some guidelines for hypertension).
pedDose: Not approved for pediatric use. Off-label: 0.1–0.2 mg/kg once daily, max 5 mg/day.
pedAgeLimit: >18 years. Not recommended for children without specialist consultation.
pedDoseAr: غير معتمد للاستخدام في الأطفال. استشارة الطبيب المختص ضرورية.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk; use with caution, monitor infant for bradycardia and hypoglycemia.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP3A4 (50%), CYP2D6 (50%). Half-life: 10–12 hours.
elimination: Renal (50% unchanged) and fecal (50% as metabolites).
interactions: Verapamil/diltiazem — severe bradycardia and AV block (avoid combination). Clonidine — rebound hypertension if clonidine stopped first. Insulin — masks hypoglycemia symptoms. NSAIDs — reduce hypotensive effect.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: eGFR 20–40: 2.5–5 mg/day; eGFR <20: 1.25–2.5 mg/day. Hepatic impairment: severe (Child-Pugh C): start 1.25 mg/day, titrate cautiously.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Bradycardia, fatigue, dizziness, headache, hypotension, cold extremities, nausea, diarrhea. Serious: Heart block, worsening heart failure, bronchospasm (rare with cardioselectivity), masked hypoglycemia.
contra: Cardiogenic shock, uncompensated heart failure, severe bradycardia (<60 bpm), sick sinus syndrome, second/third degree AV block without pacemaker, severe asthma/COPD, severe peripheral arterial disease.

name: nebivolol
moa: Nebivolol is a highly cardioselective beta-1 antagonist that also stimulates endothelial nitric oxide synthase (eNOS) → increases nitric oxide production → causes vasodilation and reduces peripheral vascular resistance. Unlike other beta-blockers, it does not worsen erectile dysfunction or fatigue as much.
commonUses: 1. Essential hypertension — alone or in combination with other antihypertensives. 2. Chronic heart failure — stable NYHA Class II–III with reduced ejection fraction (add-on to ACE inhibitors, diuretics, and digoxin in elderly >70 years).
dose: Hypertension: 5 mg orally once daily. May increase to 10 mg once daily after 2 weeks if needed. Heart failure: Start 1.25 mg once daily, double every 1–2 weeks to target 10 mg once daily as tolerated.
pedDose: Not approved for pediatric use. Limited data: 0.1–0.2 mg/kg once daily, max 5 mg/day (off-label).
pedAgeLimit: >18 years. Not recommended for children.
pedDoseAr: غير معتمد للاستخدام في الأطفال. فقط للبالغين.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk; use with caution, monitor infant for bradycardia.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP2D6 (extensive metabolizers: 10% to active metabolite; poor metabolizers: unchanged). Half-life: 10 hours (extensive), 30–50 hours (poor).
elimination: Renal (38% unchanged, rest as metabolites) and fecal. Half-life highly variable by CYP2D6 status.
interactions: Verapamil/diltiazem — severe bradycardia (avoid combination). CYP2D6 inhibitors (fluoxetine, paroxetine, quinidine) — increase nebivolol levels. Clonidine — rebound hypertension if clonidine stopped first. NSAIDs — reduce hypotensive effect.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: eGFR <30: start 2.5 mg/day, titrate cautiously. Hepatic impairment: Child-Pugh B/C: contraindicated or start 1.25 mg/day with extreme caution.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Headache, fatigue, dizziness, bradycardia, hypotension, nausea, dyspnea (especially in heart failure start). Less common (<1%): Peripheral edema, nightmares. Serious: Heart block, worsening heart failure, bronchospasm (rare due to cardioselectivity).
contra: Cardiogenic shock, uncompensated heart failure, severe bradycardia (<60 bpm), sick sinus syndrome, second/third degree AV block without pacemaker, severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C), severe asthma/COPD.

name: metoprolol
moa: Metoprolol selectively blocks beta-1 adrenergic receptors in the heart → reduces heart rate, decreases myocardial contractility, slows AV conduction, and lowers renin release → decreased cardiac output, blood pressure, and myocardial oxygen demand.
commonUses: 1. Hypertension — alone or in combination. 2. Angina pectoris — chronic stable angina. 3. Heart failure — stable NYHA Class II–III with reduced ejection fraction (using extended-release/succinate formulation). 4. Acute myocardial infarction — reduces mortality. 5. Atrial fibrillation/flutter — rate control.
dose: Hypertension/Angina (immediate-release): 100 mg orally daily in divided doses (50 mg twice daily) or 100–200 mg once daily (extended-release). Heart failure (succinate): Start 25 mg once daily (12.5 mg for severe failure), double every 2 weeks to target 200 mg once daily. MI: 50 mg every 6 hours for 48 hours IV then oral.
maxDose: Immediate-release: 400 mg/day. Extended-release (succinate): 400 mg once daily.
pedDose: 0.5–1 mg/kg once daily (extended-release) or divided twice daily (immediate-release). Max: 2 mg/kg/day or 200 mg/day.
pedAgeLimit: >6 years (limited pediatric data). Use under specialist guidance.
pedDoseAr: 0.5–1 مليجرام لكل كيلوجرام يوميًا — الحد الأقصى 2 مليجرام/كجم/يوم أو 200 مليجرام يوميًا — تحت إشراف طبيب متخصص.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk; use with caution, monitor infant for bradycardia and hypoglycemia.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP2D6 extensively. Half-life: 3–7 hours (immediate-release), 20–24 hours (extended-release).
elimination: Renal (95% as metabolites).
interactions: Verapamil/diltiazem — severe bradycardia and heart block (avoid IV combination). Clonidine — rebound hypertension if clonidine stopped first. Digoxin — additive bradycardia. Insulin — masks hypoglycemia. NSAIDs — reduce hypotensive effect. CYP2D6 inhibitors (fluoxetine, paroxetine, amiodarone) — increase metoprolol levels.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment needed. Hepatic impairment: severe cirrhosis — reduce dose significantly (start 25 mg).
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Bradycardia, fatigue, dizziness, depression, headache, dyspnea, cold extremities, diarrhea. Serious: Heart block, worsening heart failure, bronchospasm (rare with cardioselectivity), masked hypoglycemia, lupus-like syndrome (rare).
contra: Cardiogenic shock, uncompensated heart failure, severe bradycardia (<60 bpm), sick sinus syndrome, second/third degree AV block without pacemaker, severe asthma/COPD, severe peripheral arterial disease.

name: propranolol
moa: Propranolol non-selectively blocks beta-1 (cardiac) and beta-2 (bronchial/vascular) adrenergic receptors → reduces heart rate, myocardial contractility, and renin release. Also crosses BBB to block central beta-receptors. No intrinsic sympathomimetic activity (ISA).
commonUses: 1. Hypertension — especially in high-renin or hyperdynamic states. 2. Angina pectoris — chronic stable angina. 3. Atrial fibrillation/flutter — rate control. 4. Essential tremor — first-line therapy. 5. Migraine prophylaxis — reduces frequency and severity. 6. Thyrotoxicosis — adjunctive therapy for symptoms. 7. Anxiety (performance/situational) — off-label for acute anxiety.
dose: Hypertension/Angina: 40 mg orally twice daily, increase to 80–160 mg twice daily. Migraine: 80 mg daily in divided doses (40 mg twice daily), max 240 mg/day. Essential tremor: 40 mg twice daily up to 320 mg/day. Thyrotoxicosis: 10–40 mg three to four times daily.
maxDose: 320 mg/day (up to 640 mg/day for severe conditions under specialist care).
pedDose: Infants/children: 0.5–1 mg/kg twice daily, increase every 3–5 days. Max: 2–4 mg/kg/day. For hemangioma: 2 mg/kg/day in divided doses (strictly under specialist care).
pedAgeLimit: >1 month (for hemangioma); cautiously in infants.
pedDoseAr: للأطفال: 0.5–1 مليجرام لكل كيلوجرام — مرتين يوميًا — الحد الأقصى 2–4 مليجرام/كجم/يوم. للرضع أثناء علاج أورام الأوعية الدموية: تحت إشراف طبيب متخصص.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk; use with caution, monitor infant for bradycardia, hypoglycemia, and respiratory depression.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP1A2, CYP2D6; first-pass effect high. Half-life: 4–5 hours.
elimination: Renal (<1% unchanged).
interactions: Verapamil/diltiazem — severe bradycardia (avoid combination). Clonidine — rebound hypertension if clonidine stopped first. Lidocaine — decreased lidocaine clearance. Warfarin — increased INR. Insulin/sulfonylureas — masks hypoglycemia. NSAIDs — reduce hypotensive effect.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment. Hepatic impairment: severe cirrhosis — reduce dose significantly (50–75% reduction).
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Bradycardia, fatigue, dizziness, depression, nightmares, erectile dysfunction, cold extremities, bronchospasm (especially in asthma/COPD), Raynaud's phenomenon. Serious: Heart block, worsening heart failure, hypoglycemia masking (especially in diabetics), severe bronchospasm.
contra: Asthma or severe COPD, cardiogenic shock, uncompensated heart failure, severe bradycardia (<60 bpm), sick sinus syndrome, second/third degree AV block without pacemaker, severe peripheral arterial disease, pheochromocytoma (untreated).

name: amlodipine
moa: Amlodipine blocks L-type calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle and cardiac myocytes → inhibits calcium influx → causes arterial vasodilation (decreases peripheral vascular resistance → lowers BP) and coronary vasodilation (increases oxygen supply). Vascular selectivity > cardiac, so minimal negative inotropy.
commonUses: 1. Hypertension — alone or in combination with other antihypertensives (ACE inhibitors, ARBs, beta-blockers, diuretics). 2. Chronic stable angina — reduces frequency of anginal episodes. 3. Vasospastic angina (Prinzmetal's angina) — first-line therapy. 4. Coronary artery disease — reduces cardiovascular events in proven CAD.
dose: Hypertension/Angina: 5 mg orally once daily. May increase to 10 mg once daily after 7–14 days. Max: 10 mg/day. In elderly or hepatic impairment: start 2.5 mg once daily.
pedDose: Children 6–17 years: 2.5–5 mg once daily. Not established in children <6 years.
pedAgeLimit: >6 years (for hypertension).
pedDoseAr: للأطفال 6–17 سنة: 2.5–5 مليجرام — مرة يوميًا.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk in small amounts; considered compatible with breastfeeding.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP3A4 extensively. Half-life: 30–50 hours (long half-life allows once daily dosing).
elimination: Renal (60% as metabolites) and fecal. Half-life: 30–50 hours.
interactions: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, itraconazole, clarithromycin, grapefruit juice) — increase amlodipine levels (monitor for hypotension/edema). CYP3A4 inducers (rifampin, St. John's wort) — decreased effect. Simvastatin — simvastatin levels increased (limit simvastatin to 20 mg/day with amlodipine).
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment. Hepatic impairment: moderate-severe — start 2.5 mg once daily.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Peripheral edema (ankle swelling), headache, dizziness, flushing, fatigue, palpitations, nausea. Edema is dose-dependent (less with 5 mg, more with 10 mg). Serious: Severe hypotension, worsening angina (rare), gingival hyperplasia with long-term use, hepatotoxicity (rare).
contra: Hypersensitivity, cardiogenic shock, severe aortic stenosis, unstable angina (except vasospastic), severe heart failure (decompensated).

name: ramipril
moa: Ramipril (prodrug) → active metabolite ramiprilat → inhibits ACE → blocks conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II → reduces vasoconstriction and aldosterone release → decreases blood pressure, preload afterload, and prevents cardiac remodeling. Also increases bradykinin (contributes to cough and vasodilation).
commonUses: 1. Hypertension — alone or in combination. 2. Heart failure — reduces mortality and hospitalizations (post-MI or chronic HF with reduced EF). 3. Post-myocardial infarction — improves survival in patients with clinical evidence of heart failure or LV dysfunction. 4. Diabetic nephropathy — slows progression in type 1 and type 2 diabetes with microalbuminuria or overt proteinuria. 5. High cardiovascular risk — primary prevention in patients with diabetes, stroke, or vascular disease.
dose: Hypertension: 2.5 mg orally once daily, adjust to 5–10 mg once daily. Heart failure/post-MI: Start 1.25–2.5 mg twice daily, target 5 mg twice daily (10 mg/day). Max: 10 mg/day. High CV risk: 10 mg once daily.
maxDose: 10 mg/day (20 mg/day in some guidelines but not FDA approved).
pedDose: Not recommended in children. Safety not established.
pedAgeLimit: >18 years.
pedDoseAr: غير معتمد للاستخدام في الأطفال. فقط للبالغين.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk; use with caution, especially in premature or low-birth-weight infants.
metabolism: Hepatic — ramipril converted to active ramiprilat by esterases in liver. Half-life: 13–17 hours (ramiprilat).
elimination: Renal (60% ramiprilat, 40% other metabolites). Half-life: 3–4 hours (ramipril), 13–17 hours (ramiprilat).
interactions: ARBs/aliskiren — double RAAS blockade increases hyperkalemia and renal impairment (avoid, especially in diabetics). Potassium supplements/potassium-sparing diuretics — hyperkalemia risk. Lithium — lithium toxicity (monitor levels). NSAIDs — reduce antihypertensive effect, increase renal impairment risk.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: eGFR <30: start 1.25 mg once daily, max 5 mg/day. Hemodialysis: give post-dialysis. Hepatic impairment: biliary cirrhosis — use with caution, reduced dose.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Dry cough (5–20% — characteristic, non-productive, resolves with discontinuation), dizziness, headache, fatigue, hypotension (first dose). Serious: Angioedema (swelling of face, lips, tongue, larynx — emergency), hyperkalemia, renal impairment, neutropenia (rare, in collagen vascular disease).
contra: Pregnancy (second/third trimester — D), history of ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema, bilateral renal artery stenosis, severe aortic stenosis, hereditary/idiopathic angioedema.

name: enalapril
moa: Enalapril is a prodrug converted to active enalaprilat in the liver. Inhibits ACE → blocks angiotensin II formation → reduces vasoconstriction and aldosterone secretion → decreases blood pressure, preload, afterload, and cardiac remodeling. Increases bradykinin levels (responsible for cough).
commonUses: 1. Hypertension — alone or in combination. 2. Heart failure — reduces mortality and hospitalizations (usually with diuretics and digitalis). 3. Asymptomatic left ventricular dysfunction — prevents symptomatic heart failure. 4. Diabetic nephropathy — slows progression in type 1 and type 2 diabetes with microalbuminuria or overt proteinuria.
dose: Hypertension: 5 mg orally once daily, adjust to 10–40 mg daily in one or divided doses. Max: 40 mg/day. Heart failure/LV dysfunction: Start 2.5 mg once or twice daily, target 10 mg twice daily (20 mg/day).
pedDose: Children (>1 month): 0.08 mg/kg once daily up to 5 mg total. Adjust to 0.1–0.6 mg/kg/day in 1–2 doses. Max: 40 mg/day.
pedAgeLimit: >1 month (for hypertension). Not for neonates without specialist care.
pedDoseAr: للأطفال أكبر من شهر: 0.08 مجم لكل كيلوجرام مرة يوميًا حتى 5 مجم — يمكن ضبط إلى 0.1–0.6 مجم/كجم/يوم مقسمة على جرعة إلى جرعتين. الحد الأقصى 40 مجم يوميًا.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk in very small amounts; considered compatible.
metabolism: Hepatic — enalapril (prodrug) hydrolyzed to active enalaprilat. Half-life: 11 hours (enalaprilat).
elimination: Renal (60–70% as enalaprilat and metabolites). Half-life: 1–3 hours (enalapril), 11 hours (enalaprilat) — prolonged in renal failure.
interactions: ARBs/aliskiren — avoid combination (hyperkalemia, renal failure). Potassium supplements/potassium-sparing diuretics — hyperkalemia. Lithium — toxicity (monitor levels). NSAIDs — reduce effect, increase renal impairment. Gold injections (sodium aurothiomalate) — nitritoid reactions (flushing, hypotension).
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: eGFR 30–80: 5 mg/day; eGFR <30: 2.5 mg/day. Hemodialysis: give post-dialysis. Hepatic impairment: severe cirrhosis — reduce starting dose.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Dry cough (5–20%), dizziness, headache, fatigue, hypotension (first dose especially in diuretic-treated patients). Serious: Angioedema (emergency), hyperkalemia, renal impairment (especially with NSAIDs or in renal artery stenosis), neutropenia (rare).
contra: Pregnancy (second/third trimester — D), history of ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema, bilateral renal artery stenosis, severe aortic stenosis.

name: captopril
moa: Captopril directly inhibits ACE (contains sulfhydryl group that binds to active site) → blocks angiotensin I to angiotensin II conversion → reduces vasoconstriction and aldosterone secretion. Unlike other ACE inhibitors, it is not a prodrug (active orally). Short duration requires multiple daily dosing.
commonUses: 1. Hypertension — alone or in combination. 2. Heart failure — used with diuretics and digitalis (short duration of action less preferred than longer ACE inhibitors). 3. Diabetic nephropathy — slows progression in type 1 diabetes with proteinuria >500 mg/day. 4. Post-myocardial infarction — improves survival in patients with LV dysfunction.
dose: Hypertension: 25 mg orally 2–3 times daily (taken 1 hour before meals). Adjust to 25–50 mg 2–3 times daily. Max: 450 mg/day (rare). Heart failure: Start 6.25–12.5 mg 2–3 times daily, titrate to 25–50 mg 2–3 times daily. Max: 150 mg 3 times daily.
pedDose: Hypertension: 0.3–0.5 mg/kg/dose orally 2–3 times daily. Max: 6 mg/kg/day or 450 mg/day.
pedAgeLimit: >1 month. Use under specialist care.
pedDoseAr: ارتفاع الضغط للأطفال: 0.3–0.5 مجم لكل كيلوجرام لكل جرعة — 2–3 مرات يوميًا — الحد الأقصى 6 مجم/كجم/يوم أو 450 مجم يوميًا — تحت إشراف طبيب متخصص.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk in small amounts; considered compatible.
metabolism: Hepatic — minimal (captopril is active form). Half-life: 2 hours.
elimination: Renal (95% unchanged). Half-life prolonged in renal impairment.
interactions: ARBs/aliskiren — avoid combination. Potassium supplements — hyperkalemia. Lithium — toxicity. NSAIDs — reduce effect, increase renal impairment. Diuretics — risk of first-dose hypotension (reduce diuretic dose or hold before first captopril).
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: eGFR 10–50: 6.25–12.5 mg 2–3 times daily; eGFR <10: 6.25 mg 1–2 times daily. Hepatic impairment: no adjustment.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Cough (5–20%), rash (especially with sulfhydryl group — 5–10%), dysgeusia (loss of taste — 2–4%), proteinuria (rare after first few months), hypotension, dizziness, fatigue. Serious: Angioedema (emergency), neutropenia (rare, especially in collagen vascular disease or renal impairment), hyperkalemia, renal failure.
contra: Pregnancy (second/third trimester — D), history of ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema, bilateral renal artery stenosis, hereditary/idiopathic angioedema.

name: valsartan
moa: Valsartan selectively blocks the AT1 subtype of angiotensin II receptors on vascular smooth muscle and adrenal glands → prevents angiotensin II from binding → inhibits vasoconstriction and aldosterone secretion → lowers blood pressure, reduces cardiac afterload, and prevents cardiac remodeling. Does not inhibit ACE (no cough).
commonUses: 1. Hypertension — alone or in combination with other antihypertensives. 2. Heart failure — reduces mortality and hospitalizations (NYHA Class II–IV with LVEF ≤40% on standard therapy). 3. Post-myocardial infarction — reduces cardiovascular mortality in stable patients with LV dysfunction or heart failure. 4. Diabetic nephropathy — slows progression in type 2 diabetes (with hypertension and microalbuminuria).
dose: Hypertension: 80–160 mg orally once daily. May increase to 320 mg once daily. Heart failure: 40 mg twice daily, titrate to 160 mg twice daily target. Post-MI: Start 20 mg twice daily, titrate to 160 mg twice daily. Max: 320 mg/day.
pedDose: Children 6–16 years: 1.3 mg/kg once daily (max 40 mg) up to 2.7 mg/kg (max 160 mg) once daily. Not for children <6 years.
pedAgeLimit: >6 years (for hypertension).
pedDoseAr: للأطفال 6–16 سنة: 1.3 مجم لكل كيلوجرام مرة يوميًا (حد أقصى 40 مجم) حتى 2.7 مجم/كجم (حد أقصى 160 مجم) مرة يوميًا.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk; avoid or discontinue breastfeeding.
metabolism: Hepatic — minimal (20% metabolized; not CYP450-dependent largely). Half-life: 6 hours.
elimination: Biliary/fecal (70% unchanged, 30% as metabolites). Half-life: 6 hours.
interactions: ACE inhibitors/aliskiren — avoid combination (hyperkalemia, renal failure). Potassium supplements — hyperkalemia. Lithium — toxicity (monitor levels). NSAIDs — reduce antihypertensive effect, increase renal impairment risk.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment for eGFR >10; caution in severe impairment. Hepatic impairment: biliary cirrhosis or severe impairment — reduce to 40 mg/day.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Headache, dizziness, fatigue, cough (much less than ACE inhibitors), diarrhea, arthralgia, back pain. Serious: Angioedema (rare), hyperkalemia, renal impairment, hypotension (especially in volume-depleted patients), hepatotoxicity (rare).
contra: Pregnancy (second/third trimester — D), severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C), biliary cirrhosis, hypersensitivity.

name: candesartan
moa: Candesartan selectively blocks AT1 angiotensin II receptors in vascular smooth muscle and adrenal gland → prevents angiotensin II-mediated vasoconstriction and aldosterone release → lowers blood pressure, reduces preload/afterload, and inhibits cardiac and vascular remodeling. Does not inhibit ACE (no cough).
commonUses: 1. Hypertension — alone or in combination with other antihypertensives (especially HCTZ or CCBs). 2. Heart failure — reduces mortality and hospitalizations (NYHA Class II–IV with LVEF ≤40%) in patients intolerant to ACE inhibitors. 3. Diabetic nephropathy — slows progression in type 2 diabetes with hypertension and microalbuminuria.
dose: Hypertension: 8 mg orally once daily, adjust to 8–32 mg once daily. Max: 32 mg/day. Heart failure: Start 4 mg once daily, double every 2 weeks to target 32 mg once daily as tolerated.
pedDose: Children 6–16 years (<50 kg): 4–8 mg once daily; (>50 kg): 8–16 mg once daily. Not for children <6 years or <20 kg.
pedAgeLimit: >6 years and weight >20 kg.
pedDoseAr: للأطفال 6–16 سنة: أقل من 50 كجم: 4–8 مجم مرة يوميًا — أكثر من 50 كجم: 8–16 مجم مرة يوميًا.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk; avoid or discontinue breastfeeding.
metabolism: Hepatic — minimal (candesartan eliminated largely unchanged). Half-life: 9 hours.
elimination: Renal (33% unchanged), biliary/fecal (67% unchanged). Half-life: 9 hours.
interactions: ACE inhibitors/aliskiren — avoid combination (hyperkalemia, renal impairment). Potassium supplements — hyperkalemia. Lithium — toxicity (monitor levels). NSAIDs — reduce antihypertensive effect, increase renal impairment risk.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: mild-mod: no adjustment; severe: start 4 mg once daily. Hepatic impairment: mild-mod: no adjustment; severe: start 2 mg once daily.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Headache, dizziness, fatigue, URI (cold-like symptoms), back pain. Less common: cough (1–3% — much less than ACE inhibitors). Serious: Angioedema (rare), hyperkalemia, renal impairment, hypotension (in volume depletion).
contra: Pregnancy (second/third trimester — D), severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C), biliary cirrhosis, hypersensitivity.

name: hydrochlorothiazide
moa: Hydrochlorothiazide inhibits the sodium-chloride symporter (NCC) in the distal convoluted tubule → blocks sodium and chloride reabsorption → increases urinary excretion of sodium, chloride, water, potassium, and magnesium. Reduces blood pressure by decreasing plasma volume and peripheral vascular resistance.
commonUses: 1. Hypertension — first-line therapy alone or in combination. 2. Edema — due to heart failure, hepatic cirrhosis, or renal dysfunction. 3. Nephrolithiasis — calcium stones (hypercalciuria). 4. Diabetes insipidus — nephrogenic (off-label).
dose: Hypertension: 12.5–25 mg orally once daily. Edema: 25–100 mg once daily or every other day. Max: 100 mg/day (short-term), 50 mg/day (maintenance).
pedDose: Hypertension/edema: 1–2 mg/kg/day once daily or in divided doses. Max: 3 mg/kg/day or 50 mg/day.
pedAgeLimit: >6 months (safe in younger when medically necessary).
pedDoseAr: ارتفاع الضغط والوذمة: 1–2 مجم لكل كيلوجرام يوميًا — مرة واحدة أو مقسمة على جرعتين — الحد الأقصى 3 مجم/كجم/يوم أو 50 مجم يوميًا.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk in low amounts; considered compatible with breastfeeding but monitor infant for electrolyte disturbances.
metabolism: Not metabolized. Excreted unchanged.
elimination: Renal (95% unchanged). Half-life: 5–15 hours.
interactions: Lithium — toxicity (reduce lithium dose by 50%). Digoxin — hypokalemia increases digoxin toxicity. NSAIDs — reduce diuretic and antihypertensive effect. Cholestyramine/colestipol — reduce HCTZ absorption (separate by 2 hours).
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: eGFR <30: ineffective (use loop diuretic). Hepatic impairment: caution — risk of electrolyte disturbances and hepatic encephalopathy.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Hypokalemia, hyponatremia, hypomagnesemia, hypercalcemia, hyperuricemia, hyperglycemia, dizziness, headache, photosensitivity. Serious: Pancreatitis (rare), severe electrolyte disturbances, anaphylaxis, Stevens-Johnson syndrome (very rare).
contra: Anuria, severe renal impairment (eGFR <30), hypersensitivity to thiazides or sulfonamides.

name: amlodipine + valsartan
moa: Amlodipine blocks L-type calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle → vasodilation. Valsartan blocks AT1 angiotensin II receptors → further vasodilation and reduced aldosterone secretion. Complementary mechanisms produce additive BP reduction with fewer side effects than each alone.
commonUses: 1. Hypertension — for patients requiring combination therapy who are not controlled on monotherapy with either component. 2. Stage 2 hypertension — initial therapy in some guidelines. 3. Cardiovascular risk reduction — in patients with hypertension and vascular disease.
dose: One tablet orally once daily containing amlodipine 5–10 mg + valsartan 80–320 mg. Start with lowest dose (5/80 or 5/160) and titrate as needed. Max: amlodipine 10 mg + valsartan 320 mg once daily.
pedDose: Not recommended for children. Safety and efficacy not established.
pedAgeLimit: >18 years.
pedDoseAr: غير معتمد للاستخدام في الأطفال. فقط للبالغين.
lactationNote: Both components excreted into breast milk; avoid or discontinue breastfeeding.
metabolism: Amlodipine: hepatic CYP3A4. Valsartan: minimal hepatic metabolism (20%).
elimination: Amlodipine: renal (60% metabolites). Valsartan: biliary/fecal (70% unchanged). Half-life: amlodipine 30-50h, valsartan 6h.
interactions: CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, clarithromycin, grapefruit juice) — increase amlodipine levels. Potassium supplements — hyperkalemia risk (via valsartan). Lithium — toxicity. NSAIDs — reduced efficacy, renal impairment risk.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: severe: use cautiously. Hepatic impairment: moderate-severe: use lower amlodipine dose (start 2.5/160).
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Peripheral edema (amlodipine component — less than amlodipine alone), headache, dizziness, fatigue, hypotension, hyperkalemia. Serious: Angioedema (rare), severe hypotension, renal impairment, hepatotoxicity.
contra: Pregnancy (second/third trimester — D), severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C), cardiogenic shock, severe aortic stenosis, hypersensitivity to either component.

name: amlodipine + valsartan + hydrochlorothiazide
moa: Triple blockade: Amlodipine (CCB) → vasodilation. Valsartan (ARB) → further vasodilation + aldosterone inhibition. HCTZ (thiazide) → volume reduction via distal tubule sodium excretion. Three complementary pathways provide additive BP reduction with lower doses of each.
commonUses: 1. Hypertension — patients requiring triple therapy who are not controlled on dual therapy. 2. Stage 2 or resistant hypertension — initial or add-on therapy. 3. Severe hypertension — with cardiovascular risk factors.
dose: One tablet orally once daily containing amlodipine 5–10 mg + valsartan 160–320 mg + HCTZ 12.5–25 mg. Start with lowest triple dose (5/160/12.5) or step up from dual therapy. Max: amlodipine 10 mg + valsartan 320 mg + HCTZ 25 mg once daily.
pedDose: Not recommended for children.
pedAgeLimit: >18 years.
pedDoseAr: غير معتمد للاستخدام في الأطفال. فقط للبالغين.
lactationNote: All components excreted into breast milk; avoid or discontinue breastfeeding.
metabolism: Amlodipine: CYP3A4. Valsartan: minimal. HCTZ: not metabolized.
elimination: Amlodipine: renal (60%). Valsartan: fecal (70% unchanged). HCTZ: renal (95% unchanged). Half-life: amlodipine 30-50h, valsartan 6h, HCTZ 5-15h.
interactions: CYP3A4 inhibitors (amlodipine) — increase amlodipine levels. Potassium supplements — hyperkalemia (valsartan). Lithium — toxicity (HCTZ and valsartan). NSAIDs — reduced efficacy, renal impairment. Digoxin — hypokalemia (HCTZ) increases digoxin toxicity.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: eGFR 30-50: caution; eGFR <30: avoid HCTZ, use alternative diuretic. Hepatic impairment: moderate-severe: use lower amlodipine dose.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Peripheral edema, headache, dizziness, fatigue, hypotension, hyperkalemia, hypokalemia, hyperuricemia, cough (less than ACE inhibitors). Serious: Angioedema (rare), severe hypotension, renal impairment, electrolyte disturbances, pancreatitis (very rare).
contra: Pregnancy (second/third trimester — D), anuria, severe renal impairment (eGFR <30), severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C), cardiogenic shock, severe aortic stenosis, hypersensitivity to any component (including sulfonamides).

name: indapamide
moa: Indapamide inhibits the sodium-chloride symporter (NCC) in the distal convoluted tubule → increases sodium, chloride, water, potassium, and magnesium excretion. Also has vasodilator properties (reduces peripheral vascular resistance) independent of diuretic effect.
commonUses: 1. Hypertension — alone or in combination with other antihypertensives. 2. Edema — due to heart failure (less common than loop diuretics).
dose: Hypertension: 1.5 mg orally once daily (extended-release SR formulation). Immediate-release: 2.5 mg once daily. Max: 2.5 mg/day (immediate), 1.5 mg/day (SR).
pedDose: Not recommended for children. Safety not established.
pedAgeLimit: >18 years.
pedDoseAr: غير معتمد للاستخدام في الأطفال.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk; use with caution.
metabolism: Hepatic — extensively metabolized (60-80%).
elimination: Renal (60-70% as metabolites), fecal (20%). Half-life: 14-18 hours (SR), 18 hours (immediate).
interactions: Lithium — toxicity. Digoxin — hypokalemia increases toxicity. NSAIDs — reduce antihypertensive effect. Potassium-depleting drugs (amphotericin B, corticosteroids) — hypokalemia risk.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: eGFR <30: avoid. Hepatic impairment: severe: caution (risk of electrolyte disturbances and encephalopathy).
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Hypokalemia (5-10% — dose-dependent), hyponatremia, hypomagnesemia, hyperuricemia, hyperglycemia, dizziness, fatigue. Less than HCTZ for metabolic side effects at low dose. Serious: Severe hypokalemia, pancreatitis (rare), angioedema (very rare).
contra: Anuria, severe renal impairment (eGFR <30), severe hepatic impairment, hypersensitivity to sulfonamides.

name: diltiazem
moa: Diltiazem blocks L-type calcium channels in cardiac myocytes and vascular smooth muscle → slows AV node conduction (prolongs PR interval) → reduces heart rate and myocardial contractility. Also causes coronary and peripheral vasodilation. Unlike dihydropyridines, minimal reflex tachycardia.
commonUses: 1. Hypertension — alone or in combination. 2. Chronic stable angina — reduces frequency of attacks. 3. Vasospastic angina (Prinzmetal's) — first-line. 4. Atrial fibrillation/flutter — rate control (especially in patients with COPD or asthma where beta-blockers contraindicated).
dose: Hypertension: 180–240 mg orally once daily (extended-release). Angina: 30–60 mg orally 3–4 times daily (immediate-release) or 120–360 mg once daily (extended). Rate control in AF: 120–360 mg once daily (extended). Max: 480 mg/day.
pedDose: Not established for hypertension in children. Off-label: 1.5–2 mg/kg/day in divided doses.
pedAgeLimit: >18 years routinely. Limited pediatric data.
pedDoseAr: غير معتمد للأطفال. يُستخدم أحيانًا خارج النشرة: 1.5–2 مجم لكل كيلوجرام يوميًا مقسمة على جرعتين إلى 3 — تحت إشراف متخصص.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk; use with caution.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP3A4 extensively. Active metabolite desacetyldiltiazem. Half-life: 3–8 hours (immediate), 5–12 hours (extended).
elimination: Renal (35% as metabolites), biliary/fecal (65%).
interactions: Beta-blockers — severe bradycardia and heart block (caution). Digoxin — increased digoxin levels (monitor). Carbamazepine — increased carbamazepine levels. Statins (atorvastatin, lovastatin, simvastatin) — increased statin levels (limit simvastatin to 20 mg). CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, clarithromycin, grapefruit) — increase diltiazem.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment. Hepatic impairment: severe — reduce dose (start 60 mg extended or immediate).
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Headache, dizziness, fatigue, peripheral edema, constipation (especially with extended-release), bradycardia, first-degree AV block. Serious: Second/third degree AV block, severe bradycardia, worsening heart failure, hepatotoxicity (rare, reversible), Steven-Johnson syndrome (very rare).
contra: Cardiogenic shock, severe bradycardia (<60 bpm), sick sinus syndrome, second/third degree AV block without pacemaker, severe hypotension, acute MI with pulmonary congestion, severe aortic stenosis, Wolff-Parkinson-White with AF (risk of ventricular fibrillation).

name: methyldopa
moa: Methyldopa is converted to alpha-methylnorepinephrine (active metabolite) in the CNS → stimulates alpha-2 adrenergic receptors in the medulla → reduces sympathetic outflow from the brain → decreases peripheral vascular resistance → lowers blood pressure without reducing cardiac output or renal blood flow.
commonUses: 1. Hypertension — especially in pregnancy (drug of choice for chronic hypertension in pregnancy). 2. Severe hypertension — in patients with renal impairment where other agents contraindicated.
dose: 250 mg orally 2–3 times daily. Increase every 2 days as needed. Max: 3 g/day.
pedDose: 10 mg/kg/day in 2–4 divided doses. Max: 65 mg/kg/day or 3 g/day.
pedAgeLimit: Safe in children (monitor closely).
pedDoseAr: 10 مجم لكل كيلوجرام يوميًا — مقسمة على 2–4 جرعات — الحد الأقصى 65 مجم/كجم/يوم أو 3 جرام يوميًا.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk; considered compatible with breastfeeding.
metabolism: Hepatic — converted to active alpha-methylnorepinephrine. Half-life: 1–2 hours (parent), 5–10 hours (active metabolite).
elimination: Renal (primarily as metabolites).
interactions: Other antihypertensives — additive hypotension. MAO inhibitors — hypertensive crisis (avoid). Iron salts — reduce absorption (separate by 2 hours). Anesthetics — exaggerated hypotensive response.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: reduce dose by 50% if eGFR <30. Hepatic impairment: caution — active hepatic disease contraindicated.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Sedation (most common — initial therapy), drowsiness, dizziness, headache, dry mouth, fatigue, bradycardia, nasal congestion. Serious: Hemolytic anemia (positive Coombs test in 10-20%, hemolysis in <1%), hepatotoxicity (hepatitis, rarely fatal), myocarditis, extrapyramidal symptoms (parkinsonism).
contra: Active hepatic disease (hepatitis, cirrhosis), hemolytic anemia, pheochromocytoma, MAO inhibitor use within 14 days.

name: doxazosin
moa: Doxazosin selectively blocks alpha-1 adrenergic receptors in the prostate, bladder neck, and vascular smooth muscle → relaxes smooth muscle in prostate capsule and bladder neck → improves urine flow. Also blocks vascular alpha-1 receptors → vasodilation → reduces peripheral resistance → lowers BP.
commonUses: 1. Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) — reduces urinary obstruction symptoms (hesitancy, frequency, nocturia). 2. Hypertension — third-line therapy, especially in patients with both BPH and hypertension. 3. Pheochromocytoma (off-label) — preoperative management.
dose: BPH: Start 1 mg orally once daily at bedtime. Increase to 2 mg, then 4 mg every 1–2 weeks as needed. Max: 8 mg/day. Hypertension: 1–16 mg once daily. Start 1 mg at bedtime to avoid first-dose syncope.
pedDose: Not recommended for children. Safety not established.
pedAgeLimit: >18 years.
pedDoseAr: غير معتمد للاستخدام في الأطفال.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk; use with caution.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP3A4 and CYP2C19 extensively. Half-life: 19–22 hours.
elimination: Fecal (65% as metabolites), renal (5-10%).
interactions: Other alpha-blockers — additive hypotension. PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil) — additive hypotension (caution, separate doses). Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, clarithromycin, ritonavir) — increase doxazosin levels (monitor).
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment needed. Hepatic impairment: severe — contraindicated; moderate — reduce dose.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Dizziness, headache, fatigue, hypotension (especially postural/orthostatic), first-dose syncope (with 1 mg starting dose), nasal congestion, dizziness, asthenia. BPH-specific: Retrograde ejaculation (rare — more with tamsulosin). Serious: Priapism (prolonged erection >4h — emergency), severe hypotension.
contra: Hypersensitivity, history of intraoperative floppy iris syndrome (IFIS) during cataract surgery, severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C), concomitant use with other alpha-blockers.

name: carvedilol
moa: Carvedilol blocks beta-1 (heart) and beta-2 (bronchial/vascular) receptors plus alpha-1 receptors (vasodilation) without intrinsic sympathomimetic activity. Combined beta-blockade reduces heart rate and contractility; alpha-blockade causes peripheral vasodilation → reduces afterload. Also has antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.
commonUses: 1. Heart failure — stable NYHA Class II–IV with reduced ejection fraction (add-on to ACE inhibitors and diuretics). 2. Hypertension — alone or in combination. 3. Post-myocardial infarction — reduces mortality in patients with LV dysfunction.
dose: Heart failure: Start 3.125 mg twice daily. Double every 2 weeks as tolerated to target 25 mg twice daily (if <85 kg) or 50 mg twice daily (>85 kg). Hypertension: 6.25–12.5 mg twice daily, increase to 25 mg twice daily. Max: 100 mg/day (divided twice daily).
pedDose: Not approved for children. Limited data: 0.1 mg/kg twice daily, titrate to 0.4–0.5 mg/kg twice daily.
pedAgeLimit: >18 years. Limited pediatric experience.
pedDoseAr: غير معتمد للأطفال. يُستخدم أحيانًا: 0.1 مجم/كجم مرتين يوميًا — زيادة إلى 0.4–0.5 مجم/كجم مرتين يوميًا تحت إشراف متخصص.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk; use with caution, monitor infant for bradycardia and hypoglycemia.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP2D6, CYP2C9, CYP3A4. Active metabolites. Half-life: 7–10 hours.
elimination: Biliary/fecal (60% as metabolites), renal (15% unchanged).
interactions: Digoxin — increased digoxin levels (monitor). Cyclosporine — increased cyclosporine levels. Insulin — may enhance hypoglycemic effect (monitor glucose). CYP2D6 inhibitors (fluoxetine, paroxetine) — increase carvedilol levels. Verapamil/diltiazem — severe bradycardia. Cimetidine — increased carvedilol levels.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment. Hepatic impairment: severe — contraindicated (Child-Pugh C); moderate — use with caution.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Dizziness, fatigue, hypotension, bradycardia, weight gain, diarrhea, hyperglycemia. Less common: Orthostatic hypotension, worsening heart failure (initial therapy), edema. Serious: Heart block, severe bradycardia (<50 bpm), bronchospasm (especially in asthma/COPD — risk higher than cardioselective beta-blockers), hepatotoxicity (rare, severe), thrombocytopenia.
contra: Cardiogenic shock, uncompensated heart failure, severe bradycardia (<60 bpm), sick sinus syndrome, second/third degree AV block without pacemaker, severe hepatic impairment, severe asthma/COPD, bronchial asthma.

name: amoxicillin + clavulanate
moa: Amoxicillin binds penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs) → inhibits bacterial cell wall cross-linking → osmotic lysis. Clavulanate irreversibly inhibits beta-lactamases (enzymes produced by resistant bacteria) → protects amoxicillin from degradation → restores activity against beta-lactamase-producing strains. Active against gram-positive (Streptococcus, Enterococcus, Staphylococcus aureus (not MRSA), some gram-negatives (E. coli, H. influenzae, K. pneumoniae).
commonUses: 1. Community-acquired pneumonia — mild to moderate. 2. Acute bacterial sinusitis. 3. Acute otitis media — especially if recent antibiotic use or treatment failure. 4. Skin and soft tissue infections — animal bites, diabetic foot, cellulitis. 5. Urinary tract infections — complicated or recurrent. 6. COPD exacerbations. 7. Dental infections — abscess.
dose: Usual dose: 500/125 mg orally every 8 hours OR 875/125 mg every 12 hours. For severe infections: 500/125 mg every 8 hours or 875/125 mg every 12 hours. Take at start of meals to reduce GI upset.
pedDose: 40–45 mg amoxicillin/kg/day in 3 divided doses (every 8h) for mild-moderate infections. For severe infections: 80-90 mg/kg/day in 2-3 divided doses. Max: 3000 mg amoxicillin/day.
pedAgeLimit: Safe for children >3 months (weight-based dosing).
pedDoseAr: 40–45 مجم أموكسيسيلين لكل كيلوجرام يوميًا — مقسمة على 3 جرعات (كل 8 ساعات) للعدوى المتوسطة. للعدوى الشديدة: 80–90 مجم/كجم/يوم مقسمة على 2–3 جرعات. الحد الأقصى 3000 مجم أموكسيسيلين يوميًا.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk; considered compatible (rare risk of diarrhea or candidiasis in infant).
metabolism: Minimal hepatic metabolism. Both excreted largely unchanged.
elimination: Renal (50-70% unchanged amoxicillin; 30-40% clavulanate). Half-life: 1-1.5 hours.
interactions: Probenecid — increases amoxicillin levels. Methotrexate — decreased methotrexate clearance (monitor). Oral contraceptives — possible reduced efficacy (backup method not routinely needed but caution). Warfarin — increased INR (monitor). Allopurinol — increased rash risk.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: eGFR 10-30: 500/125 mg every 12h; eGFR <10: 500/125 mg every 24h. Hepatic impairment: caution in severe impairment — monitor LFTs.
sideEffects: Common (>1%): Diarrhea (most common — 9-15%), nausea, vomiting, rash, candidiasis (oral/vaginal). Serious: Severe diarrhea (C. difficile colitis — pseudomembranous colitis), anaphylaxis (rare, in penicillin allergy), cholestatic jaundice (rare, more common in elderly and men), Stevens-Johnson syndrome (very rare).
contra: Penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis, urticaria, rash). History of amoxicillin-clavulanate associated jaundice/hepatic dysfunction. Infectious mononucleosis (ampicillin rash risk 80-100%). PKU (suspension contains aspartame).

name: ciprofloxacin
moa: Ciprofloxacin inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV → prevents DNA supercoiling and replication → bacterial cell death. Highly active against aerobic gram-negatives including Pseudomonas aeruginosa.
commonUses: 1. Urinary tract infections — uncomplicated and complicated, including pyelonephritis. 2. Respiratory tract infections — acute bronchitis, bronchiectasis, anthrax. 3. Prostatitis — acute and chronic bacterial. 4. Skin and soft tissue infections. 5. Bone and joint infections. 6. Intra-abdominal infections (with metronidazole). 7. Typhoid fever. 8. Gonorrhea. 9. Infectious diarrhea — Campylobacter, Shigella, Salmonella, E. coli.
dose: 250–750 mg orally every 12 hours. IV: 200–400 mg every 12 hours over 60 minutes. Take 1 hour before or 2 hours after meals. Avoid with dairy.
pedDose: Children 1-17 years: 10-20 mg/kg/dose every 12 hours (max 750 mg/dose) for complicated UTIs. Not routinely used due to arthropathy risk.
pedAgeLimit: >1 year for specific indications only.
pedDoseAr: للأطفال 1-17 سنة: 10-20 مجم لكل كيلوجرام لكل جرعة كل 12 ساعة (حد أقصى 750 مجم) لالتهابات المسالك البولية المعقدة. لا يُستخدم روتينيًا.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk; avoid or discontinue breastfeeding.
metabolism: Hepatic — 15% (CYP1A2). Half-life: 4 hours.
elimination: Renal (50% unchanged), fecal (35%).
interactions: Antacids, iron, zinc, calcium — reduced absorption (separate by 2 hours before or 6 hours after). Theophylline — increased levels. Warfarin — increased INR.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: eGFR <30: 250-500 mg every 24h. Hepatic impairment: no adjustment.
sideEffects: Common: Nausea, diarrhea, headache, dizziness. Serious: Tendon rupture, QT prolongation, peripheral neuropathy, C. difficile colitis, phototoxicity.
contra: Hypersensitivity to fluoroquinolones. Tendon disorders with fluoroquinolones. Myasthenia gravis. Children under 18 (except specific indications).

name: levofloxacin
moa: Levofloxacin inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV → blocks DNA replication. Broader gram-positive coverage than ciprofloxacin, especially Streptococcus pneumoniae. Retains good gram-negative activity.
commonUses: 1. Community-acquired pneumonia. 2. Acute bacterial sinusitis. 3. Acute bronchitis exacerbation. 4. Complicated and uncomplicated UTIs, pyelonephritis. 5. Chronic bacterial prostatitis. 6. Skin and soft tissue infections. 7. Anthrax. 8. Tuberculosis — second-line.
dose: 250–750 mg orally or IV once daily. IV infusion over 60 minutes. Duration depends on infection.
pedDose: Anthrax only (children >6 months): 8 mg/kg/dose every 12 hours (max 250 mg/dose). Not routinely used.
pedAgeLimit: >6 months for anthrax only. Avoid otherwise.
pedDoseAr: للجمرة الخبيثة فقط (أطفال >6 شهور): 8 مجم لكل كيلوجرام كل 12 ساعة. لا يُستخدم روتينيًا.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk; avoid.
metabolism: Hepatic — minimal (<5%). Half-life: 6-8 hours.
elimination: Renal (87% unchanged).
interactions: Antacids, iron, zinc — reduced absorption (separate by 2 hours). Warfarin — increased INR. NSAIDs — seizure risk.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: eGFR 20-49: 250-500 mg every 24-48h; eGFR <20: 250-500 mg every 48h.
sideEffects: Common: Nausea, diarrhea, headache, dizziness, insomnia. Serious: Tendon rupture, QT prolongation, peripheral neuropathy, C. difficile colitis.
contra: Hypersensitivity to fluoroquinolones. Tendon disorders. Myasthenia gravis. Children under 18.

name: moxifloxacin
moa: Moxifloxacin inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV → blocks DNA replication. Enhanced gram-positive coverage including drug-resistant Streptococcus pneumoniae. Has anaerobic activity. No significant Pseudomonas activity.
commonUses: 1. Community-acquired pneumonia. 2. Acute bacterial sinusitis. 3. Acute bacterial exacerbation of chronic bronchitis. 4. Skin and soft tissue infections. 5. Intra-abdominal infections (off-label). 6. Tuberculosis — second-line.
dose: 400 mg orally or IV once daily. IV infusion over 60 minutes.
pedDose: Not approved for children.
pedAgeLimit: >18 years.
pedDoseAr: غير معتمد للأطفال. لا يُستخدم لمن هم أقل من 18 سنة.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk; avoid.
metabolism: Hepatic — glucuronidation. Half-life: 12 hours.
elimination: Fecal (45% unchanged), renal (20%).
interactions: Antacids, iron, zinc — reduced absorption (separate by 2 hours). Class III antiarrhythmics — QT prolongation risk. Warfarin — increased INR.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment. Hepatic impairment: mild-moderate: no adjustment.
sideEffects: Common: Nausea, diarrhea, headache, dizziness, QT prolongation. Serious: Tendon rupture, peripheral neuropathy, C. difficile colitis, hepatotoxicity.
contra: Hypersensitivity to fluoroquinolones. Tendon disorders. Prolonged QT interval. Myasthenia gravis. Children under 18.

name: cefotaxime
moa: Cefotaxime binds to penicillin-binding proteins → inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis → cell lysis. Excellent gram-negative coverage including Enterobacteriaceae. Good gram-positive coverage (not MRSA).
commonUses: 1. Lower respiratory tract infections. 2. Urinary tract infections. 3. Sepsis and bacteremia. 4. Intra-abdominal infections (with anaerobic coverage). 5. Gonorrhea. 6. Meningitis. 7. Bone and joint infections. 8. Surgical prophylaxis.
dose: 1–2 grams IV/IM every 6-8 hours. Meningitis: 2 grams every 6 hours. Max: 12 g/day.
pedDose: Infants/children: 50-100 mg/kg every 6-8 hours. Meningitis: 100-200 mg/kg/day divided. Neonates: 50 mg/kg every 12 hours (week 1) or every 8 hours (weeks 2-4).
pedAgeLimit: Safe for all ages including neonates.
pedDoseAr: للأطفال: 50-100 مجم لكل كيلوجرام كل 6-8 ساعات. للولدان حسب العمر.
lactationNote: Low excretion into breast milk; compatible.
metabolism: Hepatic — desacetylcefotaxime (active metabolite). Half-life: 1-1.5 hours.
elimination: Renal (60% unchanged).
interactions: Probenecid — increases levels. Aminoglycosides — synergistic but monitor renal function.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: eGFR 10-50: 1-2 g every 8-12h; eGFR <10: 1-2 g every 12-24h.
sideEffects: Common: Injection site pain, diarrhea, rash, elevated LFTs. Serious: Anaphylaxis, C. difficile colitis.
contra: Hypersensitivity to cephalosporins. Severe penicillin allergy (cross-reactivity 5-10%).

name: ceftriaxone
moa: Ceftriaxone binds to penicillin-binding proteins → inhibits cell wall synthesis. Long half-life allows once-daily dosing. No Pseudomonas activity.
commonUses: 1. Community-acquired pneumonia. 2. Urinary tract infections. 3. Sepsis. 4. Meningitis. 5. Gonorrhea (single dose IM). 6. Intra-abdominal infections. 7. Bone and joint infections. 8. Surgical prophylaxis. 9. Lyme disease. 10. Typhoid fever.
dose: 1–2 grams IV/IM once daily. Gonorrhea: 500 mg IM once. Meningitis: 2 grams every 12 hours. Max: 4 g/day.
pedDose: Children: 50-100 mg/kg once daily or divided every 12 hours. Meningitis: 100 mg/kg/day. Neonates: 50 mg/kg once daily.
pedAgeLimit: Safe for neonates, but avoid with hyperbilirubinemia (displaces bilirubin).
pedDoseAr: للأطفال: 50-100 مجم لكل كيلوجرام مرة يوميًا. لا يُستخدم للولدان مع اليرقان.
lactationNote: Low excretion; compatible.
metabolism: Not metabolized. Excreted unchanged.
elimination: Renal (60%), biliary (40%). Half-life: 6-9 hours.
interactions: Calcium-containing IV solutions — risk of precipitation (fatal in neonates). Probenecid — increases levels.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: no adjustment. Hepatic impairment: no adjustment.
sideEffects: Common: Injection site pain, diarrhea, rash, elevated LFTs, biliary sludging. Serious: Anaphylaxis, C. difficile colitis, hemolytic anemia.
contra: Hypersensitivity to cephalosporins. Neonates with hyperbilirubinemia. Concomitant IV calcium in neonates.

name: cefixime
moa: Cefixime binds to penicillin-binding proteins → inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis. Oral third-generation cephalosporin with good gram-negative coverage. No Pseudomonas activity.
commonUses: 1. Urinary tract infections. 2. Acute otitis media. 3. Pharyngitis, tonsillitis. 4. Gonorrhea (oral alternative). 5. Acute bronchitis, sinusitis.
dose: 400 mg orally once daily (or 200 mg every 12 hours). Gonorrhea: 400 mg once.
pedDose: Children (>6 months, <45 kg): 8 mg/kg once daily or divided every 12 hours. Max: 400 mg/day.
pedAgeLimit: >6 months.
pedDoseAr: للأطفال >6 شهور: 8 مجم لكل كيلوجرام مرة يوميًا أو كل 12 ساعة.
lactationNote: Low excretion; compatible.
metabolism: Not metabolized.
elimination: Renal (50% unchanged), biliary. Half-life: 3-4 hours.
interactions: Antacids — reduced absorption (separate by 2 hours). Warfarin — increased INR.
doseAdjustments: Renal impairment: eGFR 10-30: reduce by 50%; eGFR <10: 200 mg once daily.
sideEffects: Common: Diarrhea (15-20% — most common), nausea, abdominal pain. Serious: C. difficile colitis, anaphylaxis.
contra: Hypersensitivity to cephalosporins.

name: azithromycin
moa: Azithromycin binds to 50S ribosomal subunit → inhibits protein synthesis. Long tissue half-life allows short-course therapy. Active against respiratory pathogens and atypical bacteria.
commonUses: 1. Respiratory infections — CAP, bronchitis, pharyngitis, sinusitis, otitis media. 2. Skin infections. 3. Chlamydia. 4. Gonorrhea. 5. Pertussis. 6. MAC prophylaxis.
dose: 500 mg daily for 3 days OR 500 mg day 1 then 250 mg days 2-5. Chlamydia: 1 g once. IV: 500 mg once daily over 60 minutes.
maxDose: 2 g single dose (chlamydia). Typical: 1.5 g total over 3-5 days.
pedDose: 10 mg/kg once daily for 3 days OR 10 mg/kg day 1 then 5 mg/kg days 2-5.
pedAgeLimit: >6 months.
pedDoseAr: للأطفال: 10 مجم لكل كيلوجرام مرة يوميًا لمدة 3 أيام.
lactationNote: Excreted into breast milk; use with caution.
metabolism: Hepatic — minimal. Half-life: 68 hours.
elimination: Biliary/fecal (50% unchanged).
interactions: Antacids — reduced absorption (separate by 2 hours). Warfarin — increased INR. Statins — myopathy risk. Colchicine — toxicity (avoid).
doseAdjustments: No adjustments needed.
sideEffects: Common: Diarrhea, nausea, abdominal pain, headache. Serious: QT prolongation, C. difficile colitis, hepatotoxicity.
contra: Hypersensitivity to macrolides. Severe QT prolongation. Concomitant colchicine.

name: clarithromycin
